text: March 29, 2019 (this “Amendment”) and amends that certain the Distributor Motor Fuel Agreement dated as of January 23, 2018 (the “Agreement”), by and between Sunoco, LLC ("Company"), and 7-Eleven, Inc. and SEI Fuel Services, Inc. (collectively, "Distributor"). Background: A. The Parties wish to enter into this Amendment to amend and modify the Agreement, as more particularly provided herein. NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the parties hereto agree to amend the Agreement as follows: 1. Waiver of Make-Up Payment. The Parties have agreed that any Make-Up Payment otherwise due from Distributor to Company for the first Contract Year shall be waived. The difference between the Expected Annual Supply Margin for the first Contract Year and the actual Annual Supply Margin attained for the first Contract Year shall be added (as, and not in addition to, the Shortfall amount) to the Expected Annual Supply Margin for the second Contract Year as of April 1, 2019. The Parties agree to work together to find the means to ramp up growth volume in the second Contract Year to a level sufficient to avoid having Make-Up Payments in the future. 2. Bulk Sales of Fuel. The Parties agree that to help Distributor satisfy its Expected Annual Supply Margin obligations pursuant to this Agreement, Distributor may elect to purchase motor fuel in bulk from Company at Company’s cost plus [***] (the “Bulk Volume”). Bulk Volume purchased during this period shall be applied to Distributor’s Expected Annual Supply Margin obligations for the Contract Year in which the purchases occur. Volumes, locations, and timing requested of Bulk Volume purchases by Distributor will be evaluated by Company and approved in Company’s commercially reasonable discretion. Upon the agreement by Company and Distributor with respect to timing, location and volumes of Bulk Volume purchases, Distributor commits to purchasing the Bulk Volume that has been nominated. Company’s general terms and conditions applicable to bulk fuel sales will apply for all purchases of Bulk Volume hereunder unless the Parties agree otherwise in writing. 3. Expected Annual Supply Margins. Section 1.6(b) of the Agreement is hereby amended to provide that (a) for the second Contract Year, the Expected Annual Supply Margin shall [***], (b) for the third Contract Year and for each Contract Year thereafter, the Expected Annual Supply Margin shall be [***]. 4. Additional Locations. Section 1.9(b) of the Agreement is hereby amended to add the followings sentences at the end of such section: “Notwithstanding the foregoing, Distributor may add an unlimited number of additional Locations (the “Additional Locations”) with the consent of Company, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned or delayed and may only be withheld if (A) such Additional Location will create a potential conflict with any other existing Company supplied retail gasoline station, or (B) Company is unable to supply such proposed Additional Location on a commercially reasonable basis.

text: This Management Services Agreement (the “Agreement”) is dated as of June 1, 2019 (the “Effective Date”) and is between Steel Services Ltd. (“Steel Services”), a Delaware corporation, having an office at 590 Madison Avenue, 32nd Floor, New York, New York 10022 and Steel Connect, Inc., a Delaware corporation (the “Company”), having an office at 1601 Trapelo Road, Suite 170, Waltham, Massachusetts 02451. WHEREAS, the Company desires to have Steel Services furnish certain services to the Company, as set forth on Exhibit A attached hereto, as it may be amended from time to time pursuant to the terms hereof (the “Services”), and Steel Services has agreed to furnish the Services, pursuant to the terms and conditions hereinafter set forth. The Company has obtained all necessary approvals under its corporate governance documents for its entry into this Agreement. WHEREAS, a Special Committee (the “Committee”) of the Board of Directors of the Company (the “Board”) comprised of disinterested directors approved this Agreement and recommended the Board’s approval, and a majority of the disinterested directors of the Company has voted to approve this Agreement. NOW, THEREFORE, the parties therefore agree as follows: Section 1.Engagement of Steel Services 1.01. During the term of this Agreement, Steel Services shall provide to the Company the Services by way of the non-exclusive services of persons designated by Steel Services to perform the Services in accordance with the terms and provisions of this Agreement (the “Designated Persons”), as described and defined on Exhibit A, as may be necessary or desirable or as may be reasonably requested or required in connection with the business, operations, and affairs (both ordinary and extraordinary) of the Company and its subsidiaries and affiliates. 1.02. In performing the Services, Steel Services shall be subject to the supervision and control of the Committee and shall report to the Committee and/or such other person designated by the Committee (an “Authorized Person”) in accordance with such procedures as may be adopted by the Committee and Steel Services from time to time. Steel Services shall provide a quarterly report to the Committee or the Authorized Person summarizing the Services provided in such detail as the Committee or such Authorized Person and Steel Services shall mutually agree (each a “Quarterly Report”). Each Quarterly Report shall be due on or before the 60th day following the expiration of the applicable fiscal quarter of the Company. Steel Services may 1 incur obligations or enter into transactions on behalf of the Company subject to any mutually agreed upon limits as established from time to time by the Committee and Steel Services.

text: EXECUTION VERSION TRANSITION SERVICES AGREEMENT This Transition Services Agreement (this “Agreement”) is made and entered into this 26th day of August, 2019, by and among Kaman Corporation, a Connecticut corporation (“Seller”), LJ KIT Blocker, Inc., a Delaware corporation (“Purchaser 1”), LJ KAI Blocker, Inc., a Delaware corporation (“Purchaser 2”), and LJ KFP Blocker, Inc., a Delaware corporation (collectively with Purchaser 1 and Purchaser 2, “Purchasers,” and each individually, a “Purchaser”) and Kaman Industrial Technologies Corporation, a Connecticut corporation (the “Company”). WITNESSETH: WHEREAS, Seller and Purchasers have entered into a Share Purchase Agreement, dated as of June 25, 2019 (the “Purchase Agreement”), pursuant to which, at the Closing, Seller has agreed to sell and transfer the Shares of the Company to Purchasers, and Purchasers have agreed to purchase the Shares of the Company from Seller; WHEREAS, Seller currently provides certain services to the Company and its subsidiaries, including (i) Ruby Automation, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company (as the successor company to Kaman Automation, Inc.), (ii) Kaman Fluid Power, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company, and (iii) Ruby PR, LLC, a Puerto Rico limited liability company (as the successor company to Industrial Rubber & Mechanics, Inc.) (collectively, the “Subsidiaries” and each a “Subsidiary”); WHEREAS, in order to assist in the transition of the Business to Purchasers after the Closing, upon the terms and subject to the conditions set forth in this Agreement, Purchasers desire to have the Company and its Subsidiaries receive from Seller, and Seller has agreed to provide, or cause to be provided, certain transition services as set forth herein to the Company and its Subsidiaries; and WHEREAS, Purchasers and the Company acknowledge that Seller is not in the business of providing the transition services set forth in this Agreement to third parties and that such services will be provided by Seller at Purchasers’ request and solely to accommodate Purchasers and the Company and its Subsidiaries during the transitional period after the Closing so as to maintain the continuity of the Business and to enable the Company and its Subsidiaries to establish stand-alone status without continued reliance upon the services of Seller (“Transition”). NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing and the mutual representations, warranties, covenants and agreements set forth herein, and upon the terms and subject to the conditions set forth in this Agreement, the Parties hereto agree as follows: ARTICLE I DEFINITIONS Capitalized terms used but not defined in this Agreement will have the respective meanings ascribed to such terms in the Purchase Agreement, except as expressly provided herein. ARTICLE II SERVICES Section 2.1Scope of Services.

text: This Transition Services Agreement (this “Agreement”), dated as of November 1, 2019 (the “Effective Date”), is made by and between Aytu Bioscience, Inc., a Delaware corporation (“Buyer”) and Cerecor, Inc., a Delaware corporation (“Seller”). All capitalized terms used herein (or in any schedule) but not defined herein have the meanings given to them in the Purchase Agreement (defined below). Recitals A. Buyer and Seller are parties to an Asset Purchase Agreement, dated as of October 10, 2019, (the “Purchase Agreement”), under which Buyer agreed to purchase from Seller certain assets of Seller used in the operation of the Business. B. In connection with the Purchase Agreement and transactions contemplated thereby, and for purposes of easing the transition of the Business to Buyer and minimizing interruptions to the continuing operations of Seller, the parties have agreed to enter into this Agreement for the purpose of providing certain transition services to each other. Agreement In consideration of the mutual covenants contained herein, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, Buyer and Seller agree: 1. Transition Services. (a) Applicable Transition Services. Upon the terms and subject to the conditions contained herein and in the Schedules hereto, Seller shall provide, or cause its Affiliates to provide, to Buyer the services described on Schedule A-1 and Schedule A-3 (together with the services described in the other provisions of this Agreement that Seller is obligated to provide to Buyer, collectively, the “Seller-Provided Services”) and Buyer shall provide, or cause its Affiliates to provide, to Seller the services described on Schedule A-2 and Schedule A-3 (together with the services described in the other provisions of this Agreement that Buyer is obligated to provide to Seller collectively, “Buyer-Provided Services,” and together with the Seller-Provided Services, the “Transition Services,” it being understood that the services described on Schedule A-3 are mutual services that each party will provide to the other). The parties agree to negotiate in good faith the provision of any additional related services reasonably requested by Buyer to be provided by Seller, or by Seller to be provided by Buyer, including any applicable pricing. Any such additional services so provided by Seller or Buyer, as applicable, shall constitute Transition Services under this Agreement and be subject to this Agreement as if fully set forth on Schedule A-1, Schedule A-2, or Schedule A-3, as applicable, beginning as of the commencement of such additional services. During the first three (3) months following Closing, Buyer will provide the Transition Services free of charge. Thereafter, Seller will pay Buyer for any continuing Buyer-Provided Services related to the continued commercialization of the Excluded Products at the rate specified in Schedule A-2. The Transition Services provided by Sellers will be free of charge throughout the term of this agreement. (b) Non-Assignable Assets.

(“Purchaser”) and Vital Therapies, Inc. (“Seller”). The words and terms defined in the Asset Purchase Agreement (defined below) shall have the same meaning when used in this First Amendment unless the context expressly requires otherwise RECITALS A.Whereas, on March 13, 2019 Seller and Purchaser entered into an Asset Purchase Agreement (the “Asset Purchase Agreement”) in connection with a purchase and sale of certain ELAD System related assets, or the Purchased Assets. B.Whereas, Purchaser has requested additional time in order to fund the balance of the Escrow Amount. AGREEMENT NOW THEREFORE, in consideration of and in reliance upon the covenants herein contained the parties agree as follows: 1.Extension of Escrow Funding Period. Section 2.03 of the Asset Purchase Agreement is hereby amended to change the “March 26, 2019” date to “April 2, 2019” for Purchaser to wire transfer to the Escrow Agent the remaining Escrow Amount ($1,350,000), for deposit into the Escrow Account. 2.Other Terms of the Asset Purchase Agreement. Except as amended herein, all other terms, covenants and conditions of Asset Purchase Agreement, shall remain in full force and effect. 3.Counterparts. This First Amendment may be executed in two or more counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument. Delivery of an executed counterpart of a signature page to this First Amendment by facsimile, portable document format (.pdf), DocuSign or other electronic transmission shall be equally as effective as delivery of a manually executed counterpart of this First Amendment. *** Signature Page Follows *** IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties hereto have executed this First Amendment as of the Effective Date. SELLER Vital Therapies, Inc. _____________________________________________ By: Duane Nash Title: President and Chief Executive Officer PURCHASER RH Cell Therapeutics Corp. _____________________________________________ By: Haibin Ruan Title: President and Chief Executive Officer SECOND AMENDMENT TO ASSET PURCHASE AGREEMENT THIS SECOND AMENDMENT TO ASSET PURCHASE AGREEMENT (the “Second Amendment”) is made as of April 4, 2019 (the “Effective Date”), by and between RH Cell Therapeutics Corp. (“Purchaser”) and Vital Therapies, Inc. (“Seller”). RECITALS A. The Buyer, the Seller and the Stockholders have previously entered in that certain Asset Purchase Agreement, dated January 18, 2019 (the “Asset Purchase Agreement”). B. The parties hereto desire to amend the Asset Purchase Agreement as set forth herein. C. Pursuant to Section 7.2 of the Asset Purchase Agreement, the Asset Purchase Agreement may be amended by the Parties only by an instrument in writing signed on behalf of the Buyer, the Seller and the Stockholders. D. Holdco is a wholly-owned subsidiary of 1847 Holdings LLC, a Delaware limited liability company, and the parent company of Buyer. AGREEMENT NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual promises herein contained, the parties hereto, intending to be legally bound, hereby agree as follows: 1. Definitions. All capitalized terms used herein without definition shall have the meanings ascribed to them in the Asset Purchase Agreement. a. Section 1.4(a) as set forth in the Asset Purchase Agreement shall be amended and restated in its entirety to read as follows: “In consideration for the sale, assignment and delivery of the Purchased Assets, Buyer shall (i) pay an aggregate purchase price equal to Six Million, Two Hundred Thousand Dollars ($6,200,000) (the “Purchase Price”), as the same may be adjusted pursuant to this Agreement, payable in accordance with this Section 1.4(a) on the Closing Date, (ii) assume the Assumed Liabilities and (iii) issue to each of Steve Goedeker and Michael Goedeker shares of the Common Stock of Holdco equal to 11.25% non-dilutable interest in all of the issued and outstanding common stock of Holdco as of the Closing Date (the “Holdco Shares”).” b. Section 2.3 is hereby added to the Asset Purchase Agreement as follows: “2.3 Representations and Warranties of Holdco. Holdco represent and warrant to, and agrees with, the Seller and the Stockholders as follows: - 1 - (a) Organization. Holdco is a corporation duly incorporated and in good standing under the laws of the State of Delaware. (b) Binding Obligation. Holdco has all requisite corporate power and authority to enter into and perform its obligations under this Agreement. All corporate acts and other proceedings required to be taken by Holdco to authorize the execution, delivery and performance by Holdco of this Agreement and the transactions contemplated hereby, have been duly and properly taken. This Agreement has been duly-executed and delivered by Holdco and constitutes the legal, valid and binding obligation of Holdco, enforceable against Holdco in accordance with its terms.

text: EXECUTION VERSION February 9, 2018 TRMT CB Lender LLC c/o Tremont Realty Advisors LLC Two Newton Place 255 Washington Street, Suite 300 Newton, MA 02458 Attention: Douglas Lanois Re: Master Repurchase Agreement, dated as of February 9, 2018 (as amended, restated, supplemented or otherwise modified and in effect from time to time, the “Master Repurchase Agreement”) by and between TRMT CB Lender LLC (“Seller”) and Citibank, N.A. (“Buyer”). Ladies and Gentlemen: Reference is hereby made to, and this fee letter (this “Fee Letter”) is hereby incorporated by reference into, the Master Repurchase Agreement. Capitalized terms used herein that are not otherwise defined herein shall have the meanings set forth in the Master Repurchase Agreement. Section 1.Definitions. The following terms shall have the meanings set forth below. “Applicable Spread” shall mean, with respect to each Purchased Asset, the “Applicable Spread” set forth below unless otherwise agreed to between Seller and Buyer on a case-by-case basis and in each case as set forth in the related Confirmation: Property Type Multi Family Asset Applicable Spread Commercial Asset Applicable Spread Hotel Asset Applicable Spread Purchase Price Debt Yield Greater than or equal to 8.5% 200 Greater than or equal to 9.5% 210 Greater than or equal to 11.0% 225 Less than 8.5% 225 Less than 9.5% 235 Less than 11.0% 250 Notwithstanding the above, with respect to each Purchased Asset purchased pursuant to a Transaction on a Purchase Date, the “Applicable Spread” may be any other amount determined by Buyer in its sole discretion; provided, that any such change in the Applicable Spread determined by Buyer on a Purchase Date with respect to a Purchased Asset shall not have any effect on the Applicable Spread for Purchased Assets already purchased on prior Purchase Dates. “Effective Purchase Price Percentage” means, with respect to each Purchased Asset, the quotient of the outstanding Purchase Price divided by the outstanding principal balance of such Purchased Asset. “Exit Fee” shall mean, with respect to the repurchase of a Purchased Asset, an amount equal to the product of 0.5% multiplied by the then outstanding Purchase Price for such Purchased Asset. “Facility Amount” shall mean $100,000,000. “Guarantor Threshold” shall mean $1,000,000.00. “Margin Amount” shall mean, with respect to any Purchased Asset on any date, an amount equal to the product of (i) the Margin Percentage on such date with respect to such Purchased Asset and (ii) the Purchase Price of such Purchased Asset on such date. “Margin Percentage” shall mean, with respect to any Purchased Asset as of any date, a percentage equal to the quotient of (i) one (1) divided by (ii) the Purchase Price Percentage for such Purchased Asset as of any such date.

Marinello (“Executive”). W I T N E S E T H : WHEREAS, the Company has previously entered into an employment agreement with the Executive, dated March 2, 2017, (the “Prior Employment Agreement”) that agreed to employ Executive as the President and Chief Executive Officer of the Company and The Hertz Corporation, a Delaware corporation and the primary operating subsidiary of the Company (“Hertz”), and for Executive to serve as a member of the Boards of Directors of the Company and Hertz (the “Boards”); WHEREAS, the Prior Employment Agreement is scheduled to expire on December 31, 2019; WHEREAS, the Company and Executive desire to replace and supersede the Prior Employment Agreement in its entirety by entering into this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement setting forth the terms of Executive’s employment with the Company and its subsidiaries and affiliates from and after the Effective Date; NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the foregoing, the premises and mutual covenants contained in this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are acknowledged, the Company and Executive agree as follows: 1.Agreement to Employ; Employment Period; No Conflict. (a) Upon the terms and subject to the conditions of this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement, the Company agrees to employ Executive, and Executive accepts such employment, for the period commencing on the Effective Date and ending on December 31, 2020 (or such earlier date upon which Executive’s employment is terminated in accordance with Section 5). The period during which Executive is employed pursuant to this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement shall be referred to as the “Employment Period.” Concurrent with the execution of this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement, Executive shall execute the waiver and release set forth on Exhibit A attached hereto (the “Exhibit A Waiver and Release”). In consideration for Executive executing and not revoking the Exhibit A Waiver and Release, the Company hereby represents that as of the date hereof the Boards do not presently have any actual knowledge of any valid claims by the Company or Hertz against Executive. (b) Executive represents that she is entering into this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement voluntarily and that she is not subject to any contractual restriction that would prevent her from functioning as President and Chief Executive Officer of the Company and Hertz, or limit her ability to do so at any time during the Employment Period (the “Executive Representation”). (c) The Company represents that it has full authority and all necessary approvals to enter into this Amended and Restated Employment Agreement. 2.Position and Responsibilities; Location; Standard of Services. (a) Position and Responsibilities.

Mold-Masters (2007) Limited - Employment Agreement Page 1 2.3 The Employee agrees and undertakes to use the Employee's best efforts and to devote such attention, energy, skill and time as is necessary to satisfactorily fulfill all of the duties and responsibilities of the President, Mold-Masters Asia and to fulfill all duties and responsibilities assigned to the Employee by the Company from time to time. text: This Cerner Executive Severance Agreement (this “Executive Severance Agreement”), effective as of December 14, 2017 (the “Effective Date”), is a supplement to and amendment of the employment agreement dated July 14, 2003 between John T. Peterzalek (“you”/“your”) and Cerner Corporation, a Delaware corporation (“Cerner”). RECITALS A. You are the Executive Vice President, Worldwide Client Relationships of Cerner and have been employed by Cerner since July 14, 2003. You have been the Executive Vice President, Worldwide Client Relationships of Cerner since October 1, 2017. B. You entered into an employment agreement with Cerner dated July 14, 2003 (your “Employment Agreement”) and a mutual arbitration agreement with Cerner dated November 23, 2015. C. You and Cerner wish to amend your Employment Agreement by adding contractual severance terms as set forth in this Executive Severance Agreement. D. In consideration for your continuing employment with Cerner, the restricted stock granted to you on December 14, 2017, the potential severance payments and potential acceleration of the vesting of outstanding equity incentive awards described herein, the potential benefits to you in the event of a Change in Control, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which you and Cerner hereby acknowledge, you and Cerner hereby agree to the following supplemental terms and conditions to your Employment Agreement. E. Definitions of capitalized terms used but not otherwise defined herein can be found in Appendix A. AGREEMENT In consideration of the mutual covenants, promises, and obligations set forth herein, the parties agree to amend and supplement your Employment Agreement as follows: 1. PARAGRAPH 2 MODIFICATION. Paragraph 2 of your Employment Agreement is deleted in its entirety and replaced with the following: 2. EMPLOYMENT RELATIONSHIP; SEVERANCE AND BENEFITS. To the extent permitted by law, your employment relationship with Cerner is “at will,” which means that you may resign from Cerner at any time, for any reason or for no reason at all, and without advance notice (except as described below). It also means that Cerner may terminate your employment at any time - for any legally permitted reason or for no reason at all and without advance notice, subject to Cerner’s potential obligations to you under Paragraph 2.C below. B. Resignation and Termination. 1. Termination by Cerner. Cerner may terminate your employment (i) at any time with or without Cause, or (ii) upon your Disability. Your employment with Cerner shall be deemed automatically terminated upon your death.

A reference to a party in this Separation Agreement necessarily includes those persons and/or entities described in the foregoing sentence. PREAMBLE WHEREAS, Waste Management, Inc. (together with any entity that is a direct or indirect majority-owned subsidiary of Waste Management, Inc., “Waste Management”) and Executive previously entered into that certain Employment Agreement dated September 2002, as may have been amended from time to time (the “Employment Agreement”); WHEREAS, pursuant to such Employment Agreement, Executive has been continuously employed by the Company or an affiliate thereof since such time; WHEREAS, the Parties agree that upon his separation, execution and non-revocation of a waiver and release of claims, Executive will receive certain benefits described in Exhibit C of this Separation Agreement; WHEREAS, the Company and Executive now jointly desire to enter into this Separation Agreement to set forth the terms and conditions of Executive’s termination; and NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the premises and agreements contained herein, and for other good and valuable consideration, the Company and Executive hereby agree as follows: 1.Executive’s Separation. The employment of Executive with the Company shall terminate on August 11, 2018 (“Employment Termination Date”). The parties agree that, except as otherwise expressly specified herein, the Employment Agreement shall have been deemed terminated as of the Employment Termination Date. Executive acknowledges and agrees that all officer positions he held with any Waste Management entity ceased as of the Employment Termination Date. In addition, Executive shall resign from all industry or charitable board or trade group leadership positions he currently holds by virtue of his employment with the Company (including, without limitation, those positions set forth on Exhibit A). It is expressly agreed to and acknowledged by the parties that Executive is entitled to the compensation and benefits set forth in Exhibit B, (which, for the avoidance of doubt, shall survive termination of the Employment Agreement hereunder), whether or not he executes this Separation Agreement. 2.Payment of Additional Consideration. In consideration of the premises and promises herein contained, and subject to Executive executing and not revoking this Separation Agreement, it is agreed that the Company will provide Executive those certain benefits specifically detailed in Exhibit C to this Separation Agreement. It is expressly agreed to and acknowledged by the parties that Executive is not entitled to the benefits set forth in Exhibit C until such time as he executes this Separation Agreement and it becomes effective and irrevocable, by its terms. The consideration set forth in this Section 2 is in full, final and complete settlement of any and all claims which Executive could make in any complaint, charge, or civil action, whether for actual, nominal, compensatory, or punitive damages (including attorneys’ fees).

text: May 18, 2017 Re:Employment Agreement Dear Sheila: I have recently reviewed the terms and conditions of your employment and am pleased to provide this letter agreement (the “Agreement”) modifying the terms of your employment in your role as VP, Clinical Operations of Proteostasis Therapeutics, Inc. (“Proteostasis” or the “Company”). This Agreement amends, restates, and supersedes in its entirety the letter agreement dated September 29, 2016 between you and the Company (the “Offer Letter Agreement”). This Agreement is effective as of the date hereof. 1.This letter agreement outlines the terms of your employment with the Company. Position and Duties. You currently serve as VP, Clinical Operations of the Company. In this capacity, you will report directly to the Chief Development Officer (“CDO”) of the Company and will have responsibility for performing those duties as are customary for, and consistent with, your position with Company, as well as those duties as the CDO may from time to time designate. You shall use your best efforts and devote your full working time to performing your responsibilities for the Company. In addition, and as a condition of your continued employment with the Company, on or before you execute this Agreement, you shall execute the Company’s standard form of Employee Proprietary Information, Inventions, Non-Competition and Non-Solicitation Agreement (the “Restrictive Covenants Agreement”). Notwithstanding the foregoing, you may serve on other boards of directors or undertake other professional or charitable activities if (i) they do not conflict with any of your obligations to the Company and (ii) such activities are approved by the Board of Directors (the “Board”) or its Executive Committee. You may also undertake charitable activities without the approval of the Board or its Executive Committee if (i) they do not conflict with any of your obligations to the Company and (ii) they do not involve a material time commitment, individually or in the aggregate. 2.Compensation and Benefits. Your base salary will be at the rate of $300,150.00 per year and will be paid out on a bi-weekly basis for so long as you remain an employee of Proteostasis. Your base salary shall be reviewed annually by the Board or the Compensation Committee of the Board (the “Compensation Committee”). Proteostasis Therapeutics, Inc. | 200 Technology Square, Fourth Floor, Cambridge, MA 02139 | T: 617.225.0096 ACTIVE/86576310.2 Sheila Wilson May 18, 2017 (b)Bonus Plan. You will be eligible to receive an annual cash bonus pursuant to the Company’s Senior Executive Cash Incentive Bonus Plan, as it may be amended from time to time, with an individual “target bonus opportunity” for purposes of such plan of 30% of your base salary.

text: EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT AMENDED AND RESTATED EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT, dated as of November 25, 2019 (this “Employment Agreement”), by and between Evoqua Water Technologies LLC, a Delaware limited liability company (the “Company”), and Benedict J. Stas (the “Executive”) (each of the Executive and the Company, a “Party,” and collectively, the “Parties”) and, solely for purposes of Sections 2.5 and 3.2 hereof, Evoqua Water Technologies Corp. WHEREAS, the Parties previously entered into that certain Employment Agreement dated February 26, 2015 (the “Original Employment Agreement”), and amended on September 6, 2017 (the “First Amendment”) and June 22, 2018 (the “Second Amendment” and together with the Original Employment Agreement and the First Amendment, the “Prior Agreement”); WHEREAS, the Parties desire to amend and restate the Prior Agreement in its entirety as set forth herein; WHEREAS, the Company desires to continue to employ the Executive as Chief Financial Officer of Holdings and wishes to be assured of his services on the terms and conditions hereinafter set forth; and WHEREAS, the Executive desires to continue to be employed by the Company as Chief Financial Officer of Holdings and to perform and to serve the Company and Holdings on the terms and conditions hereinafter set forth. NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual covenants contained herein and other valid consideration, the sufficiency of which is acknowledged, the Parties hereto agree as follows: Section 1.Employment. 1.1 Term. The term of the Executive’s employment under this Employment Agreement will commence on the date hereof and continue until terminated pursuant to Section 3 hereof (the period of the Executive’s employment hereunder, the “Employment Period”). During the Employment Period, the Executive shall serve as Chief Financial Officer and such other positions as an officer or director of Holdings and such affiliates of Holdings as Holdings shall determine from time to time, and shall report directly to the Chief Executive Officer of Holdings (the “Reporting Person”) or his designee. In his position of Chief Financial Officer of Holdings, the Executive shall perform duties assigned to him by the Reporting Person or his designee. During the Employment Period, the Executive shall devote substantially all of his business time and attention to the business and affairs of Holdings and the Company, shall faithfully serve Holdings and the Company, and shall conform to and comply with the lawful and reasonable directions and instructions given to him by the Reporting Person or his designee. During the Employment Period, the Executive shall use his best efforts to promote and serve the interests of Holdings and the Company and shall not engage in any other business activity, whether or not such activity shall be engaged in for pecuniary profit; provided, that the Executive may (a) serve any civic, charitable, educational or professional organization and (b) manage his personal investments, in each case so long as any such activities do not (x) violate the terms of this Employment Agreement (including Section 4) or (y) materially interfere with the Executive’s duties and responsibilities to Holdings or the Company. Section 2. Compensation.

text: EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT hwke_ex102.htm EXHIBIT 10.2 EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT This EMPLOYMENT AGREEMENT (this “Agreement”), dated as of [__]1, 2019 (hereafter the “Effective Date”), is between Hawkeye Systems, Inc., a Nevada corporation (hereafter the “Company”) and Michael Mansouri (hereafter the “Executive“). The Company hereby employs the Executive, and the Executive hereby agrees to employment with the Company, upon all the terms and conditions set forth below. Executive represents and warrants that: (a) he has full power and authority to enter into this Agreement, (b) he is not restricted in any manner whatsoever from performing the duties described below, and (c) no agreement, covenant or other matter prohibits or limits her ability or authority to enter into this Agreement or perform all of the duties described below. The “Employment Term” and Executive’s employment under this Agreement shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue for a period of three (3) years from the date of this Agreement, ending at the close of business on September __, 2020, provided, however, that the Employment Term shall automatically extend for successive one-year periods (such extensions also being referred to as the “Employment Term”), as long as neither party has given written notice to the other party at least 30 days prior to the end of the then current term that such term shall not be extended, and further provided that the Agreement has not been terminated earlier in accordance with the provisions set forth below. text: SLOVITER, Circuit Judge. This appeal requires us to determine whether the District Court properly dismissed plaintiffs’ complaint on the ground that the challenged agency action was committed to administrative discretion and therefore not subject to judicial review. Plaintiffs, recipients of benefits under a federal grant program administered by the Department, contend that the agency’s revision of eligibility criteria deprived them of a statutory entitlement without notice, reasoned explanation, or an opportunity to be heard. The District Court concluded that the governing statute conferred such broad authority upon the Secretary that no meaningful standard existed by which a court could assess the legality of the revision. We disagree. Judicial review of administrative action is presumed unless Congress has expressed, either in terms or by necessary implication, an intent to preclude it. That presumption is not overcome merely because an agency has been entrusted with policy judgment or technical expertise. The relevant inquiry is whether the statute, regulations, or settled course of administrative practice supplies law to apply. Here, Congress did not authorize the Secretary to distribute benefits according to unguided preference. It directed that funds be made available to eligible persons within enumerated categories, required administration consistent with the purposes of the Act, and imposed reporting obligations designed to ensure fair and uniform implementation. Those provisions, read together, furnish adequate standards for review. Nor is the agency insulated from review by characterizing the revision as an allocation decision. Plaintiffs do not ask the court to substitute its judgment for that of the Secretary as to the wisest use of limited funds. They allege that the agency adopted a rule inconsistent with the statutory scheme and applied it retroactively to persons who had already satisfied previously announced criteria. Such claims fall comfortably within the traditional competence of courts. The Administrative Procedure Act does not forbid judicial inquiry simply because the challenged decision has budgetary consequences. The complaint also adequately alleges injury. Plaintiffs assert that they lost benefits for which they had qualified, incurred financial obligations in reliance on continued participation, and were afforded no procedure through which to contest the termination. These allegations are concrete, particularized, and fairly traceable to the agency’s action. Whether plaintiffs ultimately can prove entitlement to relief is a matter for further proceedings, not a basis for dismissal at the threshold. We express no view on the merits of plaintiffs’ statutory or constitutional claims. The agency may be able to demonstrate that the revised criteria comport with the Act, that adequate reasons supported the change, or that any procedural deficiency was harmless. But those determinations require a record and an application of the proper legal standards. By treating the matter as wholly unreviewable, the District Court failed to give effect to the strong presumption favoring judicial review of administrative action affecting individual rights. The judgment of the District Court will therefore be reversed, and the case remanded for proceedings consistent with this opinion.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit

text: In so doing, the court reasoned that the relevant statutory provisions, 11 U.S.C. §§ 547 and 550, must be read together to give effect to the trustee's avoidance power. Section 547(b) authorizes the trustee to avoid certain prepetition transfers that prefer one creditor over others. Section 550(a) then permits recovery of the property transferred, or its value, from the initial transferee or any subsequent transferee. The court concluded that these provisions establish a two-step framework. The first step identifies whether a transfer is voidable. The second determines from whom the estate may recover. The debtor argued that the statute limits recovery to the initial transferee alone. The court rejected that reading. Section 550(a)(2) expressly reaches "any immediate or mediate transferee of such initial transferee," language that would be surplusage under the debtor's interpretation. A construction that renders statutory text meaningless cannot stand. The court declined to adopt it. The court also addressed the debtor's reliance on the "for value" defense in section 550(b)(1). That defense protects a subsequent transferee who takes for value, in good faith, and without knowledge of the voidability of the transfer avoided. The debtor here failed to establish good faith. The record showed that the transferee knew of the debtor's insolvency at the time of the transfer. Knowledge of insolvency, coupled with the timing of the payments, defeated the good-faith requirement. The defense therefore did not apply. Turning to the measure of recovery, the court held that section 550(a) permits the estate to recover the value of the property when return of the property itself is impractical. The transferred assets had been commingled and partly dissipated. The court fixed the recovery amount at the value of the transfers on the petition date, supported by the trustee's uncontested valuation evidence. The court's reading harmonizes the avoidance and recovery provisions and preserves the equitable distribution the Code contemplates. Creditors of equal rank share ratably in the estate. A preferential transfer that undermines this principle may be avoided and its value recovered. The judgment for the trustee follows from the plain terms of the statute.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit

label: ai
text: The regulatory posture of Appalachian Power Co. before this Commission has long turned on the tension between its obligation to provide reliable service and its entitlement to a fair return on prudently incurred investment. That tension does not resolve itself through general appeals to utility financial health. It resolves through evidence tied to specific rate-base components and the ratemaking treatment each component warrants. The record here establishes that Appalachian Power seeks recovery of costs associated with generation assets it characterizes as necessary to meet peak demand. The company bears the burden of proving prudence. It has not met that burden as to the full amount claimed. The evidence shows that a portion of the capacity additions rested on load forecasts the company itself revised downward within the same rate period. A forecast the utility later disowned cannot support a finding that the underlying expenditure was reasonable when made. Appalachian Power responds that hindsight review is improper, and on that narrow point the company is correct. Prudence is measured against the information available to management at the time of the decision. The Commission does not evaluate an investment by asking whether it turned out well. It asks whether a reasonable utility manager, acting on the data then available, would have made the same commitment. The distinction matters because it protects utilities from being penalized for outcomes they could not have predicted. It offers no shelter, however, where the utility ignored contemporaneous data that contradicted its stated basis for acting. That is the difficulty here. The internal planning documents Appalachian Power produced show that its own engineers questioned the demand projections before the capital was committed. Management proceeded anyway. The company cannot now invoke the good-faith standard while the record demonstrates that the relevant decision-makers had reason to doubt the premise on which they relied. The proper remedy is not wholesale disallowance. The Commission should permit recovery of the capacity reasonably supported by the demand data management could credibly credit at the time. It should disallow the increment attributable to the discredited projection. This approach honors the forward-looking nature of prudence review and denies the company a return on investment it has failed to justify. Two further points warrant attention. First, the rate design proposed by Appalachian Power shifts a disproportionate share of fixed-cost recovery to residential customers. The company offered no cost-of-service study reconciling that allocation with the customer classes actually driving peak demand. Without that reconciliation, the proposed design cannot stand. Second, the return on equity the company requests exceeds the range supported by its own witness on cross-examination, who conceded that comparable utilities operate profitably at a lower figure. The Commission should approve the revenue requirement only to the extent the record supports each element. Appalachian Power remains entitled to recover its prudent costs and to earn a reasonable return. It is not entitled to recover expenditures its own records call into question.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit

label: ai
text: The custody arrangement concerning the minor child, Robert M., presents a question that the trial court resolved without adequate attention to the child's stated preferences. Robert M., now fourteen, expressed to the guardian ad litem a consistent wish to reside primarily with his father. The trial court acknowledged this preference but assigned it no weight in the final order. That treatment misapplies the governing standard. Under the statute, a child's reasonable preference is a factor the court must consider once the child has reached sufficient age and maturity. The record establishes both. Robert M. articulated specific reasons grounded in his schooling, his friendships, and his relationship with each parent. His reasons were not the product of manipulation, and the guardian ad litem so testified. A preference of this character cannot be noted and then discarded. The father argues that the trial court's silence on the substance of the preference amounts to reversible error. We agree in part. A court need not defer to a child's wishes, and the child's preference does not control the outcome. The court retains discretion to weigh that preference against the full range of factors bearing on the child's welfare. What the court may not do is treat a mature child's reasoned preference as if it were absent from the record. Here, the written findings recite the statutory factors in sequence. The findings address each parent's stability, work schedule, and capacity to provide care. On the preference factor, the order states only that "the court has considered the child's wishes." That recitation, standing alone, does not permit meaningful review. We cannot determine whether the court weighed the preference and found it outweighed, or instead overlooked it. When the record contains substantial evidence on a mandatory factor, the findings must show how the court treated that evidence. The mother responds that the trial court's ultimate placement decision rests on independent grounds sufficient to sustain the order. She points to the father's history of relocation and the disruption that a change of residence would cause. Those concerns are legitimate and appear in the record. They do not cure the deficiency in the findings. A sound result reached through incomplete reasoning still requires correction when the omission concerns a factor the legislature made mandatory. We therefore vacate the custody portion of the order and remand for further findings. On remand, the trial court shall make explicit findings addressing Robert M.'s preference, the weight assigned to it, and the reasons for that weight. The court may take additional evidence if it deems that step useful. It may reach the same placement decision or a different one. Our holding requires only that the court's treatment of the child's preference appear on the face of the findings. The remaining portions of the order, including the child support calculation and the visitation schedule, are affirmed. The father raised no error as to those provisions, and the record supports them. Vacated in part, affirmed in part, and remanded.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit

label: ai
text: COLE, Circuit Judge. The question presented is whether the district court erred in dismissing the plaintiff's retaliation claim for failure to exhaust administrative remedies. We hold that it did, and we reverse. The plaintiff filed a charge with the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission in March 2019, alleging that her employer discriminated against her on the basis of race. Four months later, after she had complained internally about the pending charge, the employer terminated her. She did not file a second charge addressed to the termination. The district court concluded that this omission barred her retaliation claim. That conclusion rests on a misreading of our precedent. A plaintiff need not file a separate charge for each act of retaliation that grows out of an earlier discrimination charge. The rule serves a practical purpose. Retaliation for filing a charge is often the very conduct the administrative process fails to anticipate, and requiring a fresh filing would trap claimants in a cycle of paperwork while the underlying dispute remains unresolved. Where the retaliation is reasonably related to the original charge and flows from the employer's response to it, the exhaustion requirement is satisfied. The record supplies that relationship. The plaintiff's termination came after she raised her pending charge with a supervisor. The temporal proximity, roughly two weeks, and the direct reference to the charge place this conduct within the scope of the administrative complaint. The employer's argument to the contrary treats exhaustion as a formal trap rather than a notice mechanism. The purpose of the charge requirement is to give the agency and the employer an opportunity to resolve the dispute before litigation. That purpose was served here. The employer knew of the charge, and its alleged retaliation responded to that knowledge. The district court relied on decisions from other circuits that require separate filings, but those authorities do not bind us, and they cut against the rule we have adopted. Our own cases hold that a retaliation claim arising after the filing of a charge may proceed without a second charge. The district court had no warrant to depart from that holding. The employer also contends that the plaintiff waived this argument below. The record shows otherwise. She raised the reasonable-relationship theory in her opposition to the motion to dismiss and cited the governing authority. The district court addressed the argument on its merits and rejected it. There is no waiver. We express no view on whether the plaintiff can prove her retaliation claim. The employer may have legitimate reasons for the termination, and the record on that question is undeveloped. Our holding is narrow. The plaintiff cleared the exhaustion hurdle, and the district court should have allowed the claim to proceed. We reverse the dismissal of the retaliation claim and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The dismissal of the remaining claims is affirmed.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit

label: ai
text: Mounts’s argument is, at bottom, a plea that nomenclature not defeat compensation. He maintains that whether his deteriorated hearing is described as an aggravation of a preexisting infirmity, a new injury, a consequential condition, or the natural progression of an earlier loss, the operative fact remains the same: his hearing was measurably worse after the events for which he seeks relief, and the law should afford a remedy for that additional damage. Stated differently, Mounts contends that the court should look past the label assigned to the condition and determine whether the evidence establishes a compensable diminution in physical function attributable to the defendant’s conduct or the compensable employment-related exposure. That contention has force only to the extent it is consistent with the governing statutory scheme and the evidentiary burdens imposed by it. Courts do not award recovery for a medical description in the abstract; they award recovery for legally cognizable harm proved by competent evidence to have been caused, in the requisite degree, by a compensable event or exposure. Thus, if Mounts proves that his hearing worsened and that the worsening was proximately caused or materially aggravated by the actionable occurrence, the fact that a physician characterizes the condition in one manner while the tribunal characterizes it in another should not, standing alone, bar recovery. The law is concerned with substance over form, and a claimant is not required to select the perfect medical label at the risk of forfeiting an otherwise valid claim. But the principle has limits. A court may not convert every decline in hearing into a compensable loss merely because the claimant experiences a real and regrettable impairment. The distinction between causation and classification remains essential. If the evidence shows only that Mounts’s hearing deteriorated from age, disease, hereditary condition, or ordinary progression unrelated to the alleged compensable cause, then recovery is unavailable regardless of terminology. Likewise, if the proof demonstrates a preexisting hearing deficit but fails to establish a measurable additional loss attributable to the relevant event or exposure, the claim cannot succeed by recasting the condition as “damage” in a broader sense. Compensation law does not insure perfect health; it provides redress for injury within the boundaries fixed by statute and precedent. Accordingly, the proper inquiry is not what verbal category best captures Mounts’s worsened hearing, but whether the record contains reliable medical and factual evidence establishing an additional, compensable impairment. The tribunal should identify the baseline condition, determine the extent of any subsequent deterioration, and decide whether the alleged cause contributed to that deterioration in a legally sufficient manner. If those elements are proved, Mounts may recover for the incremental loss, even though the condition might be described by several different terms. If they are not proved, the court’s refusal to award damages is not an impermissible elevation of form over substance, but the necessary consequence of a failure of proof. The label matters less than the causal showing; the right to recover turns on the latter.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit

label: ai
text: In response to GTW's motion for summary judgment, the plaintiff submitted an affidavit from Dr. Halloran, his treating orthopedic surgeon. The affidavit states that the plaintiff sustained a herniated disc at L4-L5, that the injury required surgical fusion, and that the condition is consistent with a traumatic event of the kind alleged in the complaint. GTW argues that the affidavit fails to raise a genuine issue of material fact on causation and that it should be disregarded because it contradicts Dr. Halloran's earlier deposition testimony. We disagree. GTW's contradiction theory rests on the sham-affidavit rule, which permits a court to disregard an affidavit that flatly conflicts with prior sworn testimony offered by the same witness. The rule applies only when the later statement is a clear reversal of the earlier one, unaccompanied by any explanation for the change. It does not reach testimony that clarifies, supplements, or elaborates on an ambiguity in the deposition. The record here does not show the direct conflict GTW asserts. At his deposition, Dr. Halloran testified that he could not attribute the disc herniation to any single incident without reviewing the full imaging series. His affidavit reflects that he later completed that review and reached a conclusion. That progression is not a reversal. A physician who declines to opine before examining the relevant records, and who forms an opinion after examining them, has not contradicted himself. He has done what the deposition testimony contemplated. GTW's remaining objection goes to the weight of the affidavit rather than its admissibility. GTW contends that Dr. Halloran did not rule out degenerative causes and that the plaintiff's medical history includes prior complaints of back pain. These are proper subjects for cross-examination at trial. On summary judgment, the court does not weigh competing medical opinions or resolve conflicts between the treating physician's conclusion and the defense expert's contrary view. The court asks only whether the plaintiff has produced evidence from which a reasonable jury could find causation. Dr. Halloran's affidavit supplies that evidence. The affidavit satisfies the foundational requirements of Rule 56. It is based on the physician's personal treatment of the plaintiff, his review of the imaging, and his clinical training. It sets out the factual basis for each conclusion rather than resting on a bare assertion. An affidavit of this character is competent to defeat summary judgment even when the opposing party offers a well-credentialed expert to the contrary. The existence of dueling experts is the paradigm of a triable dispute. We hold that the affidavit is admissible and that it creates a genuine issue of material fact on causation. The sham-affidavit rule does not apply because the affidavit clarifies rather than contradicts the deposition. GTW's arguments about the strength of Dr. Halloran's opinion are for the jury. The district court's grant of summary judgment to GTW is reversed, and the case is remanded for trial.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit

label: ai
text: Glover's own submissions defeat the argument he presses on appeal. In his motion for a new trial, he conceded that the State disclosed Ramirez as a witness more than four months before trial and produced a summary of her expected testimony at the same time. That summary tracked, in substance, the account Ramirez gave from the stand. Glover therefore cannot claim surprise, and the record forecloses any suggestion that the timing of the State's disclosure hindered his preparation. The distinction between disclosure and use is one Glover blurs throughout his brief. He does not argue that Ramirez said anything at trial that departed from the pretrial summary. He argues instead that the summary itself was inadequate to alert him to the significance of her testimony. That position is untenable on this record. The summary identified Ramirez as an eyewitness to the transaction, described the location and time of her observation, and stated that she would identify Glover as the person she saw. Nothing in her trial testimony exceeded that description. Counsel confirmed his awareness at the pretrial conference. When the court asked whether the defense intended to challenge any of the State's disclosures, counsel responded that he had reviewed the witness list and the accompanying summaries and had no objection to their form. He requested no continuance. He filed no motion to exclude. He sought no additional discovery concerning Ramirez. A defendant who examines the State's evidence, voices satisfaction with it, and proceeds to trial cannot later recast that evidence as a surprise. The prejudice inquiry confirms the result. To obtain relief on a discovery theory, Glover must show that earlier or fuller disclosure would have changed the outcome. He has not attempted that showing. He does not identify any witness he would have called, any impeachment he would have developed, or any line of cross-examination he abandoned for lack of notice. His cross of Ramirez at trial covered her vantage point, the lighting, and her prior contact with him. That cross reflects preparation, not ambush. Glover also relies on a line of cases addressing testimony first revealed during trial. Those cases have no application here. Each turned on evidence that the State withheld until the witness took the stand, depriving the defense of any chance to respond. Glover received the substance of Ramirez's testimony months in advance and used that time to prepare. The concern animating those decisions is absent. We hold that Glover's acknowledged awareness of Ramirez's testimony before trial defeats his claim of unfair surprise. A defendant who knows what a witness will say, examines the disclosure, and elects to proceed cannot convert his own strategic choices into reversible error. The trial court properly admitted Ramirez's testimony, and Glover's argument to the contrary fails.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit

label: ai
text: Under California law, the phrase “immediate presence,” as used in the law of robbery, is not confined to property physically touching the victim or held in the victim’s hand. A thing is deemed in the immediate presence of a person when it is so within the victim’s reach, inspection, observation, or control that the person could, if not overcome by force or fear, retain possession of it. This formulation reflects the settled understanding that robbery is an offense against both possession and personal security. The gravamen of the crime is not merely the taking of property, but the taking accomplished by force or fear from a person whose possessory interest is directly invaded. Accordingly, property may be taken from the immediate presence of a victim even though it is located in another room, behind a counter, inside a vehicle, or otherwise separated by some physical distance, provided the victim maintains a right and practical ability to control it but for the perpetrator’s coercive conduct. The inquiry is functional, not mechanical. Courts examine whether the victim’s relationship to the property at the time of the taking was such that violence or intimidation operated to prevent resistance, pursuit, protection, or retention. If the victim is forced to move away from cash, keys, merchandise, or other property, and the defendant then takes it, the statutory requirement is ordinarily satisfied because the force or fear has severed the victim’s ability to exercise control. This interpretation is especially important in cases involving employees, custodians, or persons with constructive possession. A store clerk need not own the money in a register to be a robbery victim; the clerk’s duty and authority to safeguard the property suffice. Likewise, a person responsible for premises or goods may be dispossessed when threats compel compliance or prevent intervention. The law thus recognizes that possession is not limited to title, and immediate presence is not limited to arm’s length proximity. At the same time, the doctrine has boundaries. Property wholly outside the victim’s awareness, access, and control may not be deemed within immediate presence merely because it belongs to the victim or is later discovered missing. A theft does not become robbery unless the taking is joined to force or fear directed at a person whose possessory interest is thereby overcome. The required nexus between coercion and taking must remain real, not hypothetical. In my view, California’s rule properly captures the social harm robbery is meant to punish. A narrow rule requiring physical contact with the property would reward artificial distinctions and fail to account for the ordinary ways people exercise dominion over things. A broad but control-based rule, by contrast, preserves the distinction between theft and robbery while protecting victims whose possession is defeated through intimidation. The phrase “immediate presence” should therefore be applied with attention to practical control, the victim’s capacity to protect the property, and whether the defendant’s force or fear was the means by which that capacity was neutralized.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The colloquy that followed did not aid the defendant's position. Defense counsel conceded that the victim had been moved from the kitchen to the rear bedroom, but argued that the movement served only to facilitate the burglary already in progress. On this theory, the confinement was incidental to the taking of property and could not support a separate conviction for kidnapping. Counsel pressed the point that the defendant harbored no independent purpose to detain the victim and that the restraint lasted only as long as the search of the premises. The argument misreads both the record and the governing standard. Whether asportation is merely incidental to another felony turns on several factors: the distance the victim was moved, the duration of the confinement, whether the movement increased the risk of harm beyond that inherent in the underlying offense, and whether the movement had significance independent of that offense. No single factor controls, and the inquiry is not satisfied by labeling the conduct a burglary. Here, the victim was bound at the wrists and left in a locked room for the better part of an hour. The burglary, by the defendant's own account, was complete once the property had been gathered near the front door. The continued confinement after that point cannot be explained as a step in the taking. It exposed the victim to a risk of harm that the burglary itself did not require. A defendant who intends only to remove property need not restrain the occupant beyond the moment necessary to avoid interference. The restraint imposed here exceeded that measure. Counsel's reliance on the absence of an independent motive also fails. The statute does not require proof that the defendant sought to confine the victim as an end in itself. It requires proof that the defendant unlawfully restrained the victim under conditions that meet the statutory elements. Motive may bear on other charges, but it does not convert a completed kidnapping into a component of the burglary. The jury heard evidence that the defendant checked on the victim twice while removing property and tightened the bindings on the second occasion. That conduct reflects a decision to maintain the confinement, not an unavoidable consequence of the theft. The cases counsel cited do not compel a different result. In each, the movement was brief and coextensive with the underlying felony, and the victim faced no risk beyond that created by the felony itself. The present facts diverge on both points. The victim was moved to a location that reduced the chance of discovery and rescue, and the confinement outlasted the theft. Those distinctions are dispositive. The trial court correctly instructed the jury that it could convict on both counts if it found the elements of each satisfied. The evidence supported that finding. We therefore reject the contention that the kidnapping merged into the burglary, and we affirm the conviction on the kidnapping count.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The Commission also found that H. Produce Company had engaged in a course of conduct designed to depress the prices paid to independent growers in the affected marketing region. The record supports this finding. Internal correspondence introduced at the hearing shows that H. Produce circulated target price ranges to its buyers and instructed them not to exceed those figures regardless of local supply conditions. This instruction operated as a ceiling on grower compensation. The company contends that these communications reflected nothing more than ordinary budgeting. That characterization does not withstand scrutiny. A budget projects expected costs. The documents here set enforceable limits and tied buyer performance reviews to compliance with them. When a purchaser commanding a substantial share of regional demand fixes the maximum it will pay and disciplines agents who deviate, the effect on growers is direct and measurable. The Commission quantified that effect. Prices paid by H. Produce during the relevant period fell roughly eleven percent below the prices paid by comparable purchasers in adjacent regions where no such instructions were shown. The company offered no alternative explanation for this divergence. Its expert attributed the gap to differences in crop quality, but the underlying data did not distinguish between grades, and the Commission was entitled to discount an opinion resting on an unsupported premise. We reject the argument that the Commission exceeded its authority by examining pricing conduct at all. The governing statute reaches practices that unfairly affect the market for agricultural commodities, and the depression of grower prices through coordinated buyer directives falls within that reach. The company reads the statute to require proof of an agreement with competitors. No such element appears in the text. Unilateral conduct that distorts the market suffices when the actor holds the market position that H. Produce holds here. The remaining objections concern the scope of the remedy. The Commission ordered H. Produce to cease circulating fixed price ceilings and to report its purchasing terms quarterly for two years. That order is proportionate to the violation found. It does not dictate the prices the company must pay. It requires only that pricing decisions respond to market conditions rather than to internal directives that suppress them. On this record, the finding stands and the remedy is affirmed.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: The Commission’s order must be sustained if it reflects a reasoned application of the Federal Power Act to the record before it. Section 10 of the Act, 16 U.S.C. § 803, does not make the issuance of a license turn upon a single use or private advantage, but upon the Commission’s judgment that the project is best adapted to a comprehensive plan for improving or developing the waterway and for serving the public interest. In licensing the pumped storage hydroelectric facility, the Commission expressly considered power supply, grid reliability, fish and wildlife resources, recreation, land use, and the protection of affected riparian interests. That is the inquiry Congress assigned to it. Petitioners argue that the order is unlawful because the facility will consume more electricity in pumping water than it generates during discharge. But that objection misunderstands the nature of pumped storage. The relevant benefit is not net energy production in isolation, but the capacity to store off-peak energy and return it to the grid when demand is greatest or intermittent resources are unavailable. The Commission was entitled to credit substantial evidence that the project would provide peaking capacity, ancillary services, and operational flexibility supporting system reliability. Nor did the Commission disregard environmental consequences. The order imposed conditions governing minimum flows, reservoir fluctuations, erosion control, aquatic monitoring, and mitigation measures recommended through the administrative process. The Commission was not required to adopt every proposed condition so long as it explained why the measures selected adequately protected statutory resources. Its discussion, though not exhaustive on every subsidiary point, establishes a rational connection between the facts found and the conditions imposed. Finally, the challengers’ preference for alternative sites does not demonstrate legal error. The Commission compared reasonable alternatives and concluded that the licensed proposal, as conditioned, better served the comprehensive development standard. Because that determination lies within the agency’s technical expertise and is supported by substantial evidence, the petition for review is denied.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: Petitioners contend that the Commission failed to consider the full range of harms attending the transmission line that would carry the generated power. They point to what they describe as siting, health, safety, environmental, and archaeological problems, and they argue that the Commission's refusal to address these concerns renders its order arbitrary. The record does not support this characterization. The Commission expressly acknowledged each category of objection. It found, however, that the transmission line lay outside the scope of the certification proceeding then before it. That proceeding concerned the generating facility, not the linear infrastructure connecting that facility to the grid. The line's routing, its proximity to residential parcels, and its potential effect on documented cultural sites fell within the jurisdiction of a separate permitting authority operating under a distinct statutory mandate. The Commission was not obligated to duplicate a review that another agency would conduct on a fuller record. We have long recognized that an agency may confine its inquiry to the matters committed to its charge. The Commission did not ignore the objections. It directed petitioners to the forum equipped to evaluate them and declined to prejudge questions that would receive dedicated attention elsewhere. This allocation of responsibility reflects a rational division of administrative labor, not an evasion of duty. Petitioners' health and safety claims rest on generalized assertions about electromagnetic exposure and construction hazards. They offered no evidence tying these concerns to the specific corridor at issue, and no study contradicting the Commission's finding that such matters would be assessed during the line's own approval process. An agency need not respond in detail to objections unsupported by particularized proof. The archaeological objection suffers the same defect. Petitioners identified no protected site along the proposed route and cited no survey establishing the presence of resources that federal or state law would shield. The environmental argument fares no better. The Commission reviewed the direct effects of the generating facility and reasonably deferred analysis of the line's separate footprint to the agency charged with that task. Segmentation of this kind is permissible where the two projects proceed under independent authorizations and where the deferred review will be genuine rather than illusory. Petitioners have not shown that the later review would be cursory or foreclosed. On this record, the Commission's treatment of the transmission concerns was reasoned and within its discretion. It considered the objections, explained why they belonged to another proceeding, and grounded its conclusion in the governing statutory scheme. We therefore reject the petitioners' challenge to this portion of the order and sustain the Commission's determination.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: The Commission acknowledges that the Alabama-Tennessee Alternative would cause less environmental harm than the North Alabama route it approved. This concession is not disputed on appeal, and the record supports it. The alternative traverses fewer wetlands, disturbs less forested habitat, and crosses no perennial stream requiring open-cut construction. The Commission's own environmental staff documented these advantages in the final environmental impact statement. Having conceded the point, the Commission was obligated to explain why it nonetheless approved the more damaging route. The National Environmental Policy Act does not compel an agency to select the least harmful alternative. It does require the agency to consider environmental consequences and to articulate a reasoned basis for its choice. Where an agency identifies a superior alternative and then rejects it, the reviewing court must be able to trace the path of the agency's reasoning. The order under review fails that standard. The Commission gave both the environmental issues and the alternative to North Alabama, the project sponsor, in a manner that abdicated its independent responsibility. Rather than weighing the identified environmental costs against the asserted benefits of the preferred route, the Commission deferred to North Alabama's assessment of its own proposal. The order recites North Alabama's conclusions and adopts them without meaningful scrutiny. An agency may consider an applicant's preferences and the practical constraints a project faces. It may not substitute the applicant's judgment for its own. The distinction matters because the applicant and the public have divergent interests. North Alabama has every incentive to favor the route that serves its commercial objectives. The Commission exists to test those objectives against the statutory factors Congress prescribed, including environmental protection. When the Commission simply ratifies the applicant's preference, it performs no function that the applicant could not perform for itself. The Commission's brief offers post hoc justifications for the North Alabama route. It cites cost, constructability, and proximity to existing infrastructure. These factors may well support the Commission's choice. The problem is that the order does not rely on them in any developed way. A court reviews the agency's stated reasoning, not the reasoning counsel supplies afterward. The Chenery principle forecloses reliance on grounds the agency did not itself invoke. Nor can the Commission cure the defect by pointing to the volume of the environmental impact statement. Compiling data is not the same as weighing it. The statement gathers the relevant information. The order was supposed to apply that information to a reasoned decision. It did not. We therefore vacate the order and remand for further proceedings. On remand, the Commission must confront the environmental superiority it has conceded and explain, in terms grounded in the record, why the countervailing considerations justify the North Alabama route. If the balance favors North Alabama, the Commission may say so and support its conclusion. What it may not do is delegate the balancing to the party whose application is under review.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: Midcoast alleges that the Commission’s decision to establish a presumption of rolled-in rates was erroneous for two reasons: first, the Pricing Policy is not a binding rule adopted pursuant to notice-and-comment procedures, and, second, even if treated as a policy statement, it rests on an impermissible departure from the Commission’s prior insistence that expansion facilities be priced incrementally unless existing customers are affirmatively protected. Neither contention warrants vacatur. The Commission did not purport to impose an inflexible command applicable without regard to record evidence. It announced a rebuttable presumption to guide the initial allocation of costs where an expansion is integrated with an existing system and where the record does not demonstrate that existing shippers will subsidize new service. Such a presumption is a familiar administrative device. It channels proof, promotes consistency, and gives regulated entities fair notice of the considerations that will inform future adjudications. It does not relieve the Commission of its statutory obligation to determine, in each case, whether the resulting rates are just and reasonable under the Natural Gas Act.
