The presence of exceptions, the opportunity for opponents to introduce contrary evidence, and the Commission’s express retention of discretion all confirm that the Pricing Policy operates as a policy statement rather than as a legislative rule. Nor was the Commission required to adhere indefinitely to a pricing preference developed under different market conditions. An agency may revise its policies when it supplies a reasoned explanation for doing so and acknowledges the interests affected by the change. The Commission explained that rolled-in pricing may better reflect the economic reality of system expansions that enhance reliability, flexibility, and operational efficiency for the entire pipeline grid. It also found that automatic incremental pricing can deter efficient construction, fragment capacity, and assign costs in a manner inconsistent with the integrated nature of pipeline operations. Those are matters within the Commission’s technical competence, and a reviewing tribunal is not free to substitute its own ratemaking judgment for that of the agency. Midcoast’s principal objection is that a presumption favoring rolled-in rates risks shifting expansion costs to captive existing customers. That concern is legitimate, but the Commission did not ignore it. The policy requires examination of whether existing customers will experience rate increases unsupported by commensurate system benefits. Where the evidence shows material subsidization, cost causation may require incremental pricing or other protective measures. The presumption therefore does not predetermine the outcome; it identifies the starting point for analysis while preserving the burden to justify the actual rate design on the administrative record. The Commission’s explanation is adequate under the deferential standard of review governing ratemaking policy. It articulated a rational connection between the facts found and the choice made, addressed the competing interests of existing and expansion shippers, and preserved case-specific adjudication. Midcoast has shown disagreement with the Commission’s policy judgment, not legal error. Accordingly, the establishment of a rebuttable presumption in favor of rolled-in rates was within the Commission’s statutory authority and was neither arbitrary nor capricious.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: The Commission's pricing framework rests on a comparison between two measures that operate at different levels. The first measure looks outward to the system as a whole. It asks whether the project, once built, produces benefits that accrue across the network rather than to a single class of customers. Those benefits include improved reliability, reduced congestion costs, deferred capital investment elsewhere, and the avoided expense of alternative facilities that the project renders unnecessary. The proponent bears the burden of quantifying these effects. Assertions of general benefit will not satisfy that burden. The record must contain evidence tying each claimed benefit to a specific mechanism and, where possible, to a dollar figure. The second measure looks inward to the customers who already pay for the system. Every new project imposes a cost, and that cost enters the rate base. Existing customers absorb some portion of it through their monthly charges. The Commission examines the magnitude of that portion and its distribution. A project that yields modest system-wide gains but shifts a heavy burden onto current ratepayers will not survive scrutiny. The inquiry is not whether existing customers bear any increase, because nearly every capital project produces some increase. The inquiry is whether the increase they bear is proportionate to the benefit they receive. These two measures do not resolve into a single number. The Commission weighs them against each other. When system-wide benefits are substantial and the rate impact on existing customers is contained, approval follows. When the benefits are diffuse and the rate impact is concentrated, the balance tips toward denial or toward a modified cost allocation. The Commission may condition approval on a pricing structure that assigns costs to the parties who capture the corresponding benefits. That authority prevents one class from subsidizing gains enjoyed by another. The order under review reflects this balance. The Commission credited the utility's evidence of network reliability gains and found those gains supported by the load-flow studies in the record. It then turned to the rate impact and found that the proposed allocation placed a disproportionate share on residential customers who would receive little direct benefit from the transmission upgrade. Rather than reject the project, the Commission reallocated a portion of the cost to the industrial customers whose demand drove the need for the facility. That reallocation reconciles the two measures. It preserves the system-wide benefit while protecting existing residential ratepayers from a burden they did not create. We find no error in this approach. The statute directs the Commission to set just and reasonable rates. A rate that ignores system-wide benefits fails that standard because it undervalues legitimate project justifications. A rate that ignores the impact on existing customers fails for the opposite reason. The Commission's two-factor analysis honors both concerns. Its factual findings rest on substantial evidence in the record. We therefore affirm the pricing decision and the cost allocation the Commission adopted.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: The record establishes that Robinson exercised authority at Kedar without meaningful oversight from anyone above him. He controlled the operating accounts, approved expenditures, executed contracts, and set the terms on which the organization did business. No board committee reviewed his decisions before they took effect. No senior officer held a countersignature or veto over disbursements. The evidence shows a single individual holding powers that most organizations of comparable size divide among several people precisely to guard against the risks that materialized here. That concentration of control matters to the questions before the court. When one person can spend, commit, and account for the same funds, the ordinary checks that expose error and deter self-dealing disappear. Robinson decided what to pay, wrote the checks, and then prepared or supervised the records that described those payments. The parties tasked with holding him accountable relied on the very documents he produced. That arrangement invited the discrepancies the plaintiff now identifies, and it removed the independent evidence that would ordinarily confirm or refute his explanations. The defense characterizes this discretion as a reasonable feature of a lean operation that trusted a capable manager. The court does not accept that framing on this record. Trust is not a substitute for controls, and a manager's competence does not excuse the absence of a structure to verify his conduct. The relevant question is not whether Robinson was talented. It is whether Kedar retained any means of confirming that his stewardship matched his representations. On the evidence presented, it did not. Robinson's own testimony reinforces the point. He acknowledged that he alone reconciled the accounts, that no outside auditor examined the books during the period at issue, and that he answered to no one on routine financial matters. He described this as efficient. The court reads it differently. An arrangement that leaves a single fiduciary as the sole author and sole reviewer of his financial conduct is not efficient. It is unguarded. The consequences of that structure appear throughout the disputed transactions. Funds moved between accounts without contemporaneous documentation. Payments to related parties went unexamined. Expenses that a second reviewer would have questioned passed without comment because no second reviewer existed. Each of these failures traces back to the same source, which is the discretion Robinson held and no one constrained. The court draws no conclusion here about Robinson's intent. That question turns on other evidence and is addressed separately. What the court finds at this stage is narrower and firmer. The degree of unchecked authority Robinson enjoyed is inconsistent with the standard of care Kedar owed its stakeholders, and it deprives the defense of the corroboration it would need to rebut the plaintiff's accounting. A fiduciary who insists on operating without oversight cannot later demand the benefit of the doubt that oversight would have supplied. Robinson placed himself in that position, and the record must be read accordingly.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: The record establishes that the information was dismissed on March 16, 1998, and that date is legally significant. An information is not a mere administrative notation; it is the accusatory pleading by which the State invokes the jurisdiction of the court and places a defendant in jeopardy of prosecution, subject to the applicable constitutional and statutory limitations. Once that pleading was dismissed, the prosecution founded upon it ceased to be pending unless and until a new charging instrument was properly filed. The dismissal therefore operated as a termination of that particular proceeding, and any later attempt to rely upon the dismissed information as though it remained viable would be inconsistent with ordinary principles of criminal procedure. Nothing in the stated fact suggests that the dismissal was clerical, conditional, or without legal effect. In the absence of a contrary order, the court must treat the March 16, 1998 dismissal as an operative judicial act. The consequences of that act depend, of course, upon the character of the dismissal. If it was entered with prejudice, the State was barred from refiling the same charge, and the defendant obtained a final adjudicative protection against renewed prosecution on that information. If it was entered without prejudice, the State may have retained authority to commence a new proceeding, but only by filing a new and timely charging instrument in compliance with governing limitation periods, speedy-trial requirements, and due process constraints. In either circumstance, the dismissed information itself could not serve as a continuing basis for restraint, conviction, enhancement, or collateral disability. This conclusion is especially important where a subsequent court or agency is asked to treat the prior charge as evidence of guilt, criminal history, or an unresolved accusation. A dismissal does not necessarily establish factual innocence, but it does establish that the particular prosecution did not proceed to judgment. Legal consequences may not be built upon a terminated accusation as if it were an adjudication. The distinction between an allegation and a conviction is fundamental. Courts are bound to honor that distinction because liberty interests, reputational interests, and statutory consequences often turn on whether a matter was merely charged or actually adjudicated. Accordingly, the March 16, 1998 dismissal should be given its ordinary procedural effect. It ended the case initiated by that information, removed that pleading from the category of pending charges, and precluded reliance on it except for purposes for which dismissed charges may lawfully be considered. Any party asserting a continuing legal consequence from the dismissed information bears the burden of identifying a valid order, statute, or subsequent proceeding that revives or independently supports that consequence. Without such a showing, the dismissed information remains precisely that: a terminated accusatory instrument, not a conviction, not an admission, and not a live prosecution. The court should therefore treat March 16, 1998 as the controlling date on which the information lost operative force.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: At the hearing, the district court articulated the reasoning behind its interim ruling. It stated: "At this point I'm not persuaded that Mr. Delgado has established a substantial likelihood of success on the merits of his breach claim." That statement, though preliminary, frames the analysis that governs the preliminary injunction inquiry and directs our review of the record. The court's phrasing carries legal significance. By anchoring its conclusion to the phrase "at this point," the court signaled that its assessment rested on the evidence then before it, not on a final determination of the parties' rights. A preliminary injunction is an interim measure. It preserves the status quo pending a full trial. The court's reservation of judgment tracks that limited purpose and confirms that the ruling did not resolve the underlying dispute. The movant bears the burden of demonstrating a likelihood of success. The district court found that Mr. Delgado had not carried that burden on the current record. It identified two gaps. First, the contract's performance provisions did not clearly obligate the defendant to deliver on the schedule Mr. Delgado described. Second, the correspondence Mr. Delgado offered as evidence of the defendant's intent was equivocal and admitted of competing interpretations. Those findings supplied a rational basis for denying interim relief. We review the denial of a preliminary injunction for abuse of discretion. Legal conclusions embedded in that ruling receive de novo review, and factual findings stand unless clearly erroneous. Under that standard, the district court's expression of doubt about the merits does not itself constitute error. A court may deny an injunction when the movant has failed to satisfy any one of the governing factors. Likelihood of success is the first among them, and a shortfall there permits denial without further analysis of the remaining elements. Mr. Delgado contends that the court applied too demanding a standard when it required persuasion at the preliminary stage. The record does not support that reading. The court measured his showing against the correct threshold, asking whether he had demonstrated a substantial likelihood rather than a certainty of success. That is the recognized formulation, and the court applied it faithfully. The interim nature of the ruling also matters to what it decides. The court's statement left open the possibility that a developed record would alter the calculus. Discovery may yield documents that clarify the defendant's obligations. Testimony may resolve the ambiguity in the disputed correspondence. The court's language invited that development rather than foreclosing it, and Mr. Delgado remains free to renew his request as the evidence accumulates. The district court neither misstated the legal standard nor drew unsupported factual inferences. Its refusal to grant preliminary relief rested on a reasoned assessment of an incomplete record. We find no abuse of discretion in that determination, and we affirm the denial of the preliminary injunction.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: COFFEY, Circuit Judge. The defendant appeals his conviction on two counts of distributing cocaine, arguing that the district court erred in admitting evidence of prior sales that the government offered under Rule 404(b). We affirm. At trial, the government introduced testimony from a confidential informant who described three earlier transactions with the defendant. The court admitted this testimony to prove intent and knowledge, over the defendant's objection that it served only to establish a propensity to deal drugs. The jury convicted on both counts, and the defendant received a sentence of 120 months. Rule 404(b) forbids the use of prior acts to show that a defendant acted in conformity with a criminal disposition. The rule permits such evidence for other purposes, including proof of intent, knowledge, and absence of mistake. We have repeatedly held that a district court must satisfy a four-part inquiry before admitting evidence of this kind. The evidence must address a matter other than propensity, the prior act must be similar and close in time to the charged offense, the proof must suffice for a jury to find the act occurred, and the probative value must not be substantially outweighed by unfair prejudice. The record shows that the district court applied each element of that inquiry. The court found that the defendant had placed his intent squarely in dispute by contending that he was merely present at the scene and lacked knowledge of the drugs. Once the defendant advanced that theory, the prior sales became relevant to rebut it. The informant's account of transactions occurring within the preceding six months met the similarity and temporal requirements we have described. His firsthand testimony, subject to cross-examination, gave the jury an adequate basis to find that the sales took place. The defendant contends that the court failed to weigh prejudice against probative value on the record. The transcript refutes that claim. The judge stated that the evidence carried significant probative force on the contested question of intent and that a limiting instruction would confine the jury's use of it. The court then instructed the jury, both when the testimony came in and again in its final charge, that the prior sales could not be considered as proof of a criminal character. We presume that jurors follow such instructions, and nothing in this record disturbs that presumption. We review the admission of prior-act evidence for abuse of discretion, and we will reverse only when no reasonable basis supports the trial court's decision. The court here identified the permissible purpose, tied the evidence to a genuinely disputed issue, and guarded against misuse through repeated instruction. That approach reflects a careful application of the governing standard rather than a departure from it. The defendant's remaining arguments concerning the sufficiency of the evidence lack merit. The conviction is affirmed.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: Contrary to Malek’s testimony that she was raped repeatedly in front of him, Mrs. Rahman’s sworn statement described no such incident, identified no assailant in Malek’s presence, and placed Malek outside the house during the period in which the alleged assault was said to have occurred. That discrepancy is not collateral. It concerns the central factual predicate on which the prosecution relied to establish both the gravity of the offense and Malek’s asserted opportunity to observe the perpetrators. Where a witness claims to have personally witnessed repeated sexual assaults, while the alleged victim’s own account omits the witness’s presence and contradicts the circumstances he described, a court may not treat the inconsistency as a minor variance attributable to trauma, delay, or imperfect recollection without first explaining why the conflict does not undermine reliability. The record contains no such explanation. The trial court credited Malek in general terms, referring to his demeanor and the purported consistency of his narration, but it did not confront the irreconcilable portions of the testimony. Nor did the corroborating evidence supply what the testimony lacked. The medical evidence established that Mrs. Rahman suffered injuries consistent with an assault, but it did not identify the assailants, the number of assaults, or Malek’s presence. The neighbors’ testimony placed several men near the residence that evening, yet none testified to seeing the acts Malek described. The investigating officer likewise conceded that Malek’s first statement did not include several details later introduced at trial. These omissions matter because the law does not permit conviction upon suspicion, outrage, or the seriousness of the accusation alone. Sexual violence is often committed in secrecy, and the testimony of a single credible witness may be sufficient. But sufficiency depends on credibility assessed against the entire record, not merely on the emotive force of the allegation. When the prosecution’s principal eyewitness gives an account that is materially at odds with the complainant’s own sworn version, the factfinder must determine whether the inconsistency is reconcilable, whether it reflects mistake, exaggeration, or fabrication, and whether independent evidence restores confidence in the disputed claim. On this record, the State did not meet that burden as to the events Malek claimed to have observed. The evidence may support findings that Mrs. Rahman was attacked and that grave offenses occurred, but the specific allegation that Malek witnessed repeated rapes, in the manner and under the circumstances he described, remains unproven beyond a reasonable doubt. The distinction is not technical. It preserves the boundary between proof and assumption, and it protects both the complainant’s dignity and the accused’s right to be judged only on evidence that withstands adversarial testing. Accordingly, Malek’s testimony could not properly be used as the decisive basis for findings requiring his direct observation of the charged acts. Any judgment resting on that premise must be set aside or reconsidered under the correct standard, with explicit attention to the conflict between his account and Mrs. Rahman’s testimony.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: The BIA noted that Mrs. Alvarez had entered the United States without inspection in 1998 and had remained continuously present since that date. Her application for cancellation of removal depended on satisfying the ten-year physical presence requirement under 8 U.S.C. section 1229b(b)(1)(A). The Board found that the Notice to Appear served on her in 2007 did not stop the accrual of physical presence because it omitted the date and time of her hearing. That omission proved decisive. Under Pereira v. Sessions and its progeny, a defective notice fails to trigger the stop-time rule. The Board reasoned that Mrs. Alvarez had accumulated the requisite decade of presence by the time she filed her application, and the government's later attempt to cure the notice through a supplemental document did not retroactively terminate the accrual period. The BIA next addressed the exceptional and extremely unusual hardship standard. It observed that Mrs. Alvarez has two children who are United States citizens, one of whom suffers from a chronic respiratory condition requiring ongoing treatment. The immigration judge had discounted this evidence, concluding that adequate medical care would be available in the country of removal. The Board rejected that conclusion as unsupported by the record. No country conditions evidence in the file established that comparable treatment existed or that the family could afford it. The Board also faulted the immigration judge for failing to weigh the cumulative effect of the hardship factors. Each factor, considered alone, may not rise to the statutory threshold. Taken together, the medical needs of the child, the absence of family support abroad, and the disruption to the children's education present a stronger case than the judge acknowledged. The record reflects that the older child has known no home other than the one in which she was raised. The government argued that Mrs. Alvarez failed to establish good moral character during the relevant period. The Board found this contention unpersuasive. The single traffic citation in the record does not bar a finding of good moral character, and the government identified no other conduct that would. The immigration judge made no adverse credibility determination, and Mrs. Alvarez's testimony stood unrebutted. Having identified these errors, the Board declined to reach a final determination on relief. The proper course is remand. The immigration judge must reassess the hardship evidence under the correct legal framework and develop the record on country conditions. The Board expressed no view on the ultimate merits of the application. The dissent would have affirmed, reasoning that the hardship standard sets a demanding bar that ordinary family separation cannot meet. That view understates the specific medical evidence in this record. The child's condition is documented, and the treatment gap is not speculative. The majority correctly returns the matter for further proceedings consistent with its opinion.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: The claim of retaliation as against Thiesen fares no better. Thomsen asserts that in September 1994 Thiesen, a member of the Board, angrily ordered him to leave a town meeting. Even accepting that account as true for present purposes, the incident does not advance Thomsen's case in any material respect. To establish a causal link between his protected activity and the adverse actions he complains of, Thomsen must point to conduct connected to the decisions that harmed him. A single confrontation at a public meeting supplies no such connection. The record does not show that the town meeting bore on Thomsen's employment, his compensation, or any tangible term of his position. Nor does it show that Thiesen's directive to leave carried any consequence beyond the moment. An angry word, standing alone, is not an adverse employment action. The temporal and substantive gap compounds the problem. Thomsen identifies no decision by the Board that followed from the September 1994 exchange. He does not tie Thiesen's remark to any vote, resolution, or personnel measure taken afterward. Without that link, the incident amounts to isolated friction between two individuals rather than evidence of a retaliatory motive animating official action. Courts have declined to treat stray hostility as proof that a governing body acted to punish protected conduct, and the reasoning applies with force here. Thomsen also fails to explain how Thiesen's individual conduct is attributable to the Board. Thiesen was one member among several. A retaliation claim against the Board requires evidence that the body, acting through its lawful processes, took action against Thomsen because of his protected activity. The heated words of one member at a meeting do not establish that the Board harbored or acted upon any improper purpose. Thomsen offers nothing to bridge the distance between Thiesen's personal reaction and a collective decision by the Board. Even viewed in the light most favorable to Thomsen, the September 1994 episode lacks the elements that would make it probative. It reflects displeasure, not an employment decision. It involves one person, not the governing body. It occurred at a public gathering, not in the setting where Thomsen's terms of service were determined. These deficiencies are not technical. They go to the heart of what a retaliation claim must prove, and Thomsen has supplied no facts that fill the void. The order to leave a meeting, however abrupt, does not by itself permit an inference that the Board retaliated against Thomsen for exercising his rights. Thomsen must produce more than a recollection of anger. He must show a decision, a decisionmaker acting in an official capacity, and a causal tie to his protected conduct. On this element he has produced none of the three. The contention accordingly does nothing to support his claim, and it cannot carry the burden he must meet to survive summary judgment on this issue.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: Based on the information supplied by Warwick, the police were entitled to act with dispatch. The record reflects that Warwick did not merely provide an anonymous or conclusory accusation; rather, he furnished identifying information sufficient to permit officers in the field to locate the vehicle and its occupants within a short period of time. Acting upon that information, the police radioed ahead, and officers positioned along the anticipated route were able to stop the vehicle occupied by Hunte and Richards. The legality of that stop must be assessed in light of the totality of the circumstances known to law enforcement collectively at the time the seizure occurred, not by isolating the knowledge of the particular officer who activated the emergency lights. Under the collective-knowledge doctrine, an officer may rely on a communication from another officer or agency where the originating information provides an adequate basis for the intrusion. The Constitution does not require the stopping officer personally to possess every fact supporting reasonable suspicion, provided the police team, considered as a whole, had sufficient articulable facts to justify the action taken. Here, Warwick’s report supplied the operative facts, and the radio transmission served as the means by which those facts were communicated to officers capable of intercepting the suspects. That procedure is ordinary, practical, and consistent with Fourth Amendment principles, particularly where delay would risk the disappearance of the vehicle or the loss of evidence. The stop of Hunte and Richards therefore did not rest on a mere hunch. The officers had a particularized basis to believe that the persons in the vehicle were connected to the matter under investigation. The description, timing, direction of travel, and prompt police response reduced the possibility of mistake and distinguished this case from one involving a generalized lookout or unsupported suspicion. The fact that the officers who made the stop acted after receiving a radioed instruction does not diminish the objective justification for the seizure, so long as the instruction itself was grounded in reliable information. Nor is the stop invalid simply because it occurred before the officers had completed every step of their investigation. Terry permits a brief investigatory detention when officers can point to specific and articulable facts suggesting criminal activity may be afoot. The purpose of such a detention is to confirm or dispel suspicion through limited inquiry. Pulling over Hunte and Richards was reasonably tailored to that purpose. The officers did not undertake an indiscriminate dragnet; they stopped a specific vehicle associated with information just relayed by Warwick. Accordingly, the police action was supported by reasonable suspicion, if not more, at the moment the vehicle was stopped. The radio transmission functioned as a lawful extension of Warwick’s information, enabling coordinated police action without offending constitutional limits. Any contrary rule would unduly hamper legitimate law enforcement by requiring each officer in a fast-moving investigation to reacquire firsthand the facts already known to the police. The stop was therefore lawful.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: The district court's finding that Richards requested a co-defendant to keep quiet rests on adequate evidentiary support in the sentencing record. At the hearing, the government introduced testimony describing a conversation in which Richards instructed the co-defendant not to speak with investigators. The court credited that testimony. A sentencing court may resolve factual disputes by a preponderance of the evidence, and its credibility determinations receive deference on review. Richards challenges the finding on two grounds. He first contends that the testimony was uncorroborated and came from a witness with an incentive to cooperate. That argument goes to weight, not admissibility. The court heard the witness, observed the demeanor, and assessed the account against the balance of the record. Nothing in the transcript shows that the court overlooked the witness's motive to curry favor. To the contrary, the court acknowledged the cooperation agreement and still found the account reliable. A reviewing court will not substitute its own reading of a cold record for the trial judge's firsthand appraisal. Richards next argues that the statement, even if made, does not warrant an obstruction enhancement under the Guidelines. That position misreads the governing provision. Section 3C1.1 applies when a defendant willfully attempts to obstruct the administration of justice during the investigation or prosecution of the offense. An instruction to a co-defendant to remain silent falls within the conduct the enhancement targets. The commentary lists threatening, intimidating, or otherwise unlawfully influencing a witness as covered behavior. A request to keep quiet, directed at a person the defendant knows will be questioned, fits that description when the request is intended to impede the inquiry. The record supports the inference of intent. Richards made the statement after the investigation had begun, to a person he knew possessed information about the offense. The timing and target of the request permit the court to conclude that Richards sought to prevent disclosure rather than merely to offer casual advice. The court drew that inference, and it stands on the facts before it. We reject the suggestion that a bare request, unaccompanied by any threat, cannot support the enhancement. The Guidelines do not require coercion. They require a willful attempt to obstruct, and an effort to induce a witness to withhold information satisfies that standard. Whether the request succeeded is immaterial. The enhancement reaches attempts. The court applied the correct legal standard and made findings sufficient to support its application. It identified the conduct, connected that conduct to the investigation, and explained why the conduct amounted to obstruction. That explanation satisfies the requirement that a sentencing court articulate the basis for a contested enhancement. Richards has shown no clear error in the factual finding and no misapplication of the Guidelines. The obstruction enhancement was proper. The finding that Richards requested a co-defendant to keep quiet supports the two-level increase the court imposed, and we affirm that portion of the sentence.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: The Debtor and the Trustee advance a number of practical and equitable reasons why, in their view, it would be incongruous or unwise to permit reimbursement claims for services rendered in connection with the estate. They contend that such claims may dilute distributions to unsecured creditors, invite duplicative participation by parties whose efforts were neither requested nor supervised by the estate fiduciary, and undermine the statutory scheme governing compensation of professionals. Those concerns are not insubstantial. Bankruptcy administration depends upon order, predictability, and adherence to the priority structure Congress has prescribed. A party may not, by characterizing its conduct as beneficial, transform voluntary or self-interested activity into an administrative expense payable ahead of general creditors. Nevertheless, the existence of such concerns does not justify a categorical disallowance where the governing statute permits reimbursement and the record establishes that the services conferred an actual, demonstrable benefit upon the estate. The question is not whether reimbursement for services may sometimes prove inconvenient, nor whether allowance of such claims might require careful scrutiny. It is whether the claimant has satisfied the statutory predicates for allowance. If those predicates are met, the Court’s role is to apply them, not to engraft an additional limitation based upon a generalized apprehension that reimbursement claims are administratively awkward or potentially inconsistent with ordinary estate governance. The distinction is important. The Bankruptcy Code already contains safeguards against the very abuses identified by the Debtor and the Trustee. A claimant must show that the expense was actual and necessary, that the services were not merely duplicative, and that the estate received a concrete benefit rather than an incidental advantage to the claimant. The Court may reduce or deny compensation where the proof is speculative, excessive, inadequately documented, or directed primarily to the claimant’s private objectives. These limitations ensure that reimbursement is reserved for exceptional circumstances and does not become an alternative route for parties to obtain professional compensation without authorization. Nor is the asserted incongruity sufficient to override the equitable principles that inform bankruptcy administration. If a party, acting outside the ordinary compensation framework, materially preserves or enhances the estate under circumstances contemplated by the statute, denying reimbursement solely because the services were not performed by an estate-retained professional may produce its own inequity. The estate should not receive and retain a measurable benefit while disclaiming any obligation to bear the reasonable cost of obtaining it, provided the claimant has carried the burden imposed by law. Accordingly, the Court does not accept the Debtor’s and Trustee’s proposed rule that reimbursement for services should be rejected as inherently inconsistent with the Code’s compensation scheme. Their objections properly bear upon the evidentiary showing, the reasonableness of the amounts sought, and the causal connection between the services and the benefit conferred. They do not, however, warrant a threshold prohibition. The claim must therefore be evaluated under the applicable statutory standard, with close attention to necessity, benefit, reasonableness, and the avoidance of duplication.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit

label: ai
text: In May 1995, the Commission issued a policy statement governing how pipelines should price the cost of new construction. The statement addressed a recurring conflict in natural gas regulation. A pipeline seeking to expand its system often asks existing customers to share the cost through rolled-in rates. Those customers object when the expansion serves new demand and raises their rates without a matching benefit. New shippers, by contrast, resist incremental rates that assign the entire expansion cost to the service they request. The Commission resolved that conflict by adopting a presumption in favor of incremental pricing for new facilities, while preserving discretion to approve rolled-in treatment when the record shows system-wide benefits or no subsidy from existing customers. That approach falls within the Commission’s authority under the Natural Gas Act. The Act requires rates that are just and reasonable. It does not command one method for allocating construction costs. Cost causation remains the controlling principle, but the Commission may apply that principle through presumptions that reflect experience in regulated markets. The 1995 statement rests on a rational premise: customers who cause a pipeline to build facilities should ordinarily bear the cost of those facilities. Existing customers should not fund an expansion merely because the project attaches to the same integrated system. The policy statement also respects the distinction between rulemaking and adjudication. The Commission did not impose an inflexible tariff rule. It announced the criteria that would guide future certificate and rate proceedings. Pipelines remained free to present evidence that an expansion would improve reliability, reduce operating costs, enhance access to supply, or produce other benefits for existing shippers. Opponents remained free to challenge those claims. The Commission therefore gave regulated entities advance notice of its general approach without surrendering its duty to decide cases on their records. Petitioners argue that the policy statement departed from prior rolled-in pricing practice without adequate explanation. That argument understates the Commission’s explanation and overstates the stability of the prior practice. The Commission had approved both rolled-in and incremental pricing in earlier cases, depending on the facts. By 1995, open-access transportation had changed the commercial setting. Expansion projects increasingly served discrete groups of new shippers rather than the captive bundled customers for whom the historic system had been built. The Commission identified that change and explained why a presumption against subsidies better matched the restructured market. The statement does not guarantee lower rates for any class of customers. It sets the burden of justification. A pipeline that seeks rolled-in treatment must show why existing customers should pay part of the expansion cost. That allocation of proof is reasonable. The pipeline controls the project design, the contracts, and the cost evidence. Requiring it to justify a claimed system benefit promotes disciplined investment and protects customers from unexamined cross-subsidies. The Commission’s May 1995 policy statement therefore represents a lawful exercise of administrative judgment under the Natural Gas Act.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: The record establishes that DCPS acted after it learned of the Prinvest incident, not before. That sequence matters. The plaintiffs seek to treat the termination of Robinson’s contract and DCPS’s assumption of control over the Kedar school as proof that DCPS had long possessed knowledge of the same danger that the incident later revealed. The inference does not follow. A remedial response after notice does not, by itself, establish prior notice. It shows that DCPS regarded the incident as serious and that it chose to remove Robinson from the school’s operation once the facts came to light. That conclusion does not absolve DCPS of every possible duty. As the public agency responsible for placing students and overseeing the delivery of educational services, DCPS retained obligations that it could not shed by contract. It had to exercise reasonable care in selecting a contractor, monitoring the contractor’s performance, and responding to credible signs of risk. The question, however, is whether the evidence would permit a factfinder to conclude that DCPS breached those obligations before the Prinvest incident. On this record, the answer depends on proof of what DCPS knew, when it knew it, and what steps a reasonable agency would have taken with that information. The post-incident conduct has limited probative value on that issue. DCPS’s decision to terminate Robinson’s contract may support the view that Robinson could no longer be trusted to operate the school after the incident. It does not show that DCPS had enough information earlier to justify the same action. Agencies often receive new information that changes the risk calculus. The law does not punish an agency for acting promptly once that change occurs. Nor does it convert later corrective action into an admission that earlier oversight fell below the governing standard. The plaintiffs also argue that DCPS’s immediate takeover of the Kedar school demonstrates that DCPS always had the capacity to run the program itself. Capacity is not the same as legal obligation. A public agency may have the power to intervene without having a duty to do so at an earlier time. The duty arises from notice, foreseeable risk, and the agency’s relationship to the students. If DCPS had received warnings comparable to the Prinvest incident and disregarded them, the takeover would carry different significance. The present record does not support that comparison without additional evidence. The court therefore gives the termination and takeover their proper weight. Those actions confirm that DCPS acted decisively after learning of misconduct connected to Robinson. They also define the point at which DCPS’s knowledge became concrete enough to require intervention. But they do not, standing alone, prove that DCPS should have terminated Robinson’s contract sooner or assumed control of the school before the Prinvest incident. Any claim based on earlier negligence must rest on evidence of prior notice and unreasonable inaction, not on the mere fact that DCPS later chose a more protective course.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: The record establishes that the tow, in the configuration selected for the transit, left about 24 feet of clearance on each side when passing through the bridge opening. That measurement controls the court’s analysis because it defines the margin available for steering error, current set, wind effect, and response time. A 24-foot margin on each side does not, by itself, make the transit unlawful. It does, however, require a standard of care suited to a confined passage where a modest lateral movement can produce contact with the bridge structure. The operator had a duty to evaluate the tow’s beam, the bridge width, the river conditions, visibility, speed, and available assistance before committing to the transit. The law does not demand perfect navigation. It demands reasonable seamanship under the conditions present at the time. Where the available clearance narrows to this degree, reasonable seamanship requires early alignment, controlled speed, effective communication among crew members, and continuous observation of the tow’s head and stern. The narrower the margin, the less tolerance exists for delayed correction. The clearance figure also bears on causation. If the tow struck the bridge, the question is not answered by stating that 24 feet remained on either side at the centerline of the opening. That calculation assumes a centered approach and a tow that holds its alignment through the span. A tow angled within the opening consumes the available clearance rapidly. A slight yaw can place a corner of the tow outside the safe envelope even though the nominal side clearance appears adequate on paper. The operator therefore cannot rely on the static measurement as a complete defense. At the same time, the opposing party cannot treat the 24-foot clearance as proof of negligence without more. Bridges on navigable waterways often require precise handling. A tow may lawfully transit a narrow opening when the pilot has experience, the tow responds predictably, the weather permits control, and the crew uses appropriate precautions. The governing inquiry remains practical. It asks whether a competent operator, knowing the bridge dimensions and the tow’s characteristics, would have attempted the passage in the manner used. On this record, the clearance supports a finding that the transit presented a heightened risk. It does not establish liability unless the remaining evidence shows that the pilot failed to account for that risk. Relevant evidence includes the approach angle, engine orders, rudder commands, use of assist vessels, speed over ground, river stage, current direction, and any communications with the bridge or other traffic. These facts determine whether the pilot converted a narrow but manageable passage into an unreasonable hazard. The court therefore gives the 24-foot side clearance substantial weight, but not conclusive effect. The measurement defines the operating margin. It does not excuse poor handling, and it does not condemn careful navigation. Liability turns on whether the operator matched the method of transit to the limited room available at the bridge.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: Defendants do not contest that the matters discussed in the RFI concern public issues. That concession is well founded. The RFI addressed the operation of public programs, the expenditure of public funds, the conduct of public officials and contractors performing governmental functions, and the effect of those activities on residents who depend on the services at issue. Speech on those subjects lies at the center of public concern. The First Amendment gives its highest protection to discussion of government performance because citizens cannot supervise their institutions without access to criticism, inquiry, and competing accounts of official action. The record also confirms that the RFI did more than recount a private commercial dispute. It solicited information bearing on whether public resources were being administered lawfully and effectively. It sought facts about compliance, oversight, staffing, service delivery, and the treatment of affected members of the community. Those topics extend beyond the financial interests of any single participant. They bear on the public’s ability to assess whether government actors, and those working with them, are meeting obligations imposed by law and public trust. Because defendants concede the public-concern element, the Court need not dwell on that threshold point. The concession narrows the dispute to the legal consequences that follow from speech directed to a matter of public concern. In that posture, the challenged statements receive constitutional protection unless plaintiffs can satisfy the governing standards for liability. Plaintiffs may not avoid those standards by characterizing the RFI as a private business communication. Labels do not control. The content, context, and audience of the communication show that the RFI concerned institutional accountability and the use of public authority. Public-concern status also affects the weight assigned to competing interests. Plaintiffs have an interest in protecting reputation and contractual relationships from false statements of fact. That interest remains legitimate. Yet the law requires breathing space for debate about public programs, including debate that proves uncomfortable for those who manage, fund, or benefit from them. If liability could attach whenever a request for information prompted scrutiny of a public-facing program, speakers would have reason to avoid asking questions about government operations. That result would conflict with the principles that protect civic oversight. Nothing in this conclusion decides whether each challenged statement was true, false, actionable opinion, or protected petitioning activity. Those issues require separate analysis under the applicable elements and defenses. Here, the point is narrower. The subject matter of the RFI falls within the recognized category of public concern, and defendants have not placed that issue in dispute. Accordingly, the Court treats the public-concern requirement as satisfied for purposes of the pending motion. Any remaining claim must proceed, if at all, under the heightened limitations that apply when private litigation targets speech about governmental performance and matters affecting the community.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: An applicant cannot obtain remand merely by showing that the Board of Immigration Appeals did not discuss each document in the record. The Board must consider the issues presented and give reasons sufficient to permit judicial review. That duty does not require a catalogue of every affidavit, report, or article submitted by the alien. When the Board identifies the governing standard, addresses the material grounds for relief, and explains why the evidence fails to satisfy that standard, a reviewing court may infer that it considered the record unless the record shows otherwise. Requiring separate findings on each item would convert appellate review into an inventory exercise and would exceed the Board’s statutory role. The relevant question is whether the decision shows reasoned consideration of the claim as a whole.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: The statutory definition of a "qualified individual" performs two distinct functions, and the courts have not always kept them separate. First, the definition identifies who may invoke the Act's protections. An individual must have a disability as defined elsewhere in the statute. Second, and more consequentially, the definition ties eligibility to job performance. The individual must be able to perform the essential functions of the position, either unaided or with reasonable accommodation. That second requirement carries the weight of most disputes under the Act. The phrase "essential functions" directs the inquiry to the core duties of the position, not to marginal or incidental tasks that an employer might assign for convenience. A function is essential when its removal would fundamentally alter the position. The burden of identifying essential functions falls on the employer in the first instance, though the employer's judgment is not conclusive. Written job descriptions prepared before the vacancy carry evidentiary weight, but they do not foreclose a contrary finding where the actual work diverges from the paper. The clause "with or without reasonable accommodation" reorders the analysis in a way that many employers misapprehend. An individual who cannot perform an essential function without help is still qualified if a reasonable accommodation would enable that performance. The question is not whether the individual can do the job today, unassisted, but whether the individual can do it with the adjustments the Act requires the employer to consider. An employer who evaluates capability without accounting for available accommodations applies the wrong standard and risks liability for that error alone. The definition also marks the outer limit of the employer's obligation. Reasonable accommodation does not extend to eliminating an essential function, reallocating it to other workers, or lowering the performance standard the position demands. An accommodation that would require the employer to redefine the job is not reasonable within the meaning of the Act. The line between adjusting how a function is performed and excusing its performance controls the outcome in many cases. Read together, these elements establish a sequence. A court first identifies the essential functions, then determines whether the individual can perform them with any reasonable accommodation. Only an individual who clears both steps qualifies for the Act's protection.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The Sixth Circuit’s formulation states the controlling point for this claim: “[a]t the time she could ‘perform the essential functions’ of her job, she was not disabled for purposes of” the ADA. That reasoning defeats any attempt to splice separate periods of employment into a single prima facie showing. The statute protects a qualified individual with a disability. It does not permit a plaintiff to combine qualification from one period with disability from another and then assemble those distinct facts after the fact. The record must show that the employee was disabled, within the meaning of the Act, at the same time she could perform the essential functions of the position, with or without reasonable accommodation. When she performed the job without limitation, she lacked a statutory disability. When her condition later imposed limitations that prevented performance of the job’s essential functions, she no longer satisfied the qualified-individual requirement. The ADA does not require an employer to disregard that temporal divide. This conclusion follows from the structure of the Act. A plaintiff must establish that she has a disability, that she is otherwise qualified for the position, and that the employer acted against her because of that disability. Each element matters at the time of the challenged employment decision. If the employee had no substantial limitation when she could perform the job, the employer did not act against a disabled employee during that period. If the employee later became substantially limited but could no longer perform the essential duties, the Act did not compel the employer to retain her in the same role. The accommodation inquiry does not alter the result. Reasonable accommodation addresses barriers that prevent a qualified disabled employee from performing essential functions. It does not eliminate essential functions, create a new position, or require indefinite leave when no return to effective performance is shown. An employer may need to consider schedule changes, equipment, reassignment to a vacant position, or other measures supported by the record. But the employee still bears the burden of identifying an accommodation that would permit performance of the essential job duties. Speculation about future capacity cannot satisfy that burden. Nor does this approach narrow the ADA beyond its text. It enforces the statutory balance. Congress protected employees whose impairments substantially limit major life activities, while preserving the employer’s right to define and require the core duties of the job. A contrary rule would convert any later medical decline into proof that an earlier, fully performing employee was disabled, or would convert earlier competence into proof that a later, incapacitated employee remained qualified. The Sixth Circuit rejected that reasoning because it collapses distinct requirements. On this record, the plaintiff cannot show the required overlap. Her evidence establishes periods of satisfactory performance and later periods of incapacity, but it does not establish that she was both disabled and qualified when the adverse decision occurred. The ADA claim therefore fails as a matter of law.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: In Smith v. the central question is not whether the plaintiff suffered an inconvenience, but whether the defendant’s conduct invaded a legally protected interest in a manner cognizable at law. The record establishes that Smith entered into a written agreement, performed the obligations imposed upon him, and thereafter relied upon the defendant’s express representations in arranging his business affairs. When the defendant repudiated those representations without notice, justification, or a legally sufficient reservation of rights, the resulting injury was neither speculative nor remote. It followed directly from conduct the defendant could reasonably foresee would induce reliance. The defendant urges that the agreement should be read narrowly, isolating selected clauses from the instrument as a whole. That argument is unpersuasive. A contract is not construed by dismemberment. Its provisions must be read together, giving effect to each term if reasonably possible and avoiding an interpretation that renders material language idle. Here, the defendant’s proposed construction would deprive the notice provision, the performance schedule, and the remedial clause of practical effect. The more coherent reading is that the parties intended reciprocal obligations, enforceable upon substantial performance and subject only to those limitations expressly stated in the document. Nor does the defendant’s appeal to equitable discretion alter the result. Equity does not exist to relieve a party from the consequences of a calculated breach. It intervenes to prevent injustice where legal remedies are inadequate or where rigid enforcement would defeat the parties’ legitimate expectations. The evidence shows that Smith proceeded in good faith, complied with the agreed conditions, and sought relief only after the defendant refused to honor obligations it had accepted. On these facts, enforcement promotes, rather than undermines, equitable principles. The trial court properly rejected the defendant’s contention that damages were unsupported. Smith presented documentary evidence, contemporaneous communications, and testimony sufficient to establish the nature and amount of his loss with reasonable certainty. The law does not require mathematical precision where the wrong itself has made exact calculation difficult. It requires a rational basis for approximation, and that standard was met. Accordingly, the judgment in favor of Smith rests upon sound principles of contract interpretation, reliance, and remedial fairness. The defendant received the benefit of the bargain and cannot now disclaim the burdens attached to it. The judgment is therefore affirmed.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The Court began its inquiry by noting that the first step in interpreting a statute is to determine whether the language at issue has a plain and unambiguous meaning as applied to the dispute before it. This principle reflects a settled premise of statutory construction. When Congress has spoken with clarity, its words control, and the Court's task ends where the text furnishes an answer. The inquiry into meaning does not proceed in the abstract. It asks whether the operative language resolves the specific question presented, not whether the statute admits of some conceivable uncertainty in a different case. Applying that standard here, the Court examined the phrase on which the parties disagree. Section 4(b) directs that a claimant "shall file" within ninety days of receiving notice. The petitioner reads this command as jurisdictional, barring any late claim regardless of the equities. The respondent treats it as a claim-processing rule subject to tolling. The text answers the question. The verb "shall" imposes a mandatory duty, but nothing in the provision speaks to the consequences of noncompliance, and nothing conditions the tribunal's authority on timely filing. A deadline is not jurisdictional merely because it is firm. The Court declined to import a jurisdictional bar that the statute does not state. Congress knows how to make a filing requirement jurisdictional, and it has done so in neighboring provisions using language that ties the tribunal's power to the timing of the claim. Section 4(b) contains no such link. The absence is telling. Where Congress uses precise language in one section and omits it in another, the Court presumes the omission was deliberate. The plain meaning of the text does not end the analysis where the words themselves invite a broader reading. Here, though, the words are confined. "Shall file within ninety days" describes an obligation resting on the claimant. It does not describe a limit on the forum. Reading the two together, the provision sets a deadline that the claimant must meet, and it leaves open whether that deadline may yield to equitable considerations in an appropriate case. Because the language is clear as applied to the dispute, the Court had no occasion to resort to legislative history or to canons that operate only when the text is ambiguous. Those tools clarify meaning that the words leave uncertain. They do not override meaning that the words supply. The petitioner's reliance on committee reports therefore misapprehends the order of the inquiry. The Court consults such materials only after the text has failed to resolve the question, and the text has not failed here. The Court accordingly holds that Section 4(b) is a mandatory but nonjurisdictional deadline. The claimant's obligation to file within ninety days remains, and the tribunal retains authority to consider a late claim where equitable tolling applies. This reading gives full effect to the words Congress chose and imposes no limit the statute does not contain.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The Second Circuit reasoned that a narrow reading may “permit irrational discrimination as between disabled retirees, some of whom, for whatever reason, could still satisfy a formal condition of eligibility while others, equally disabled and equally separated from the work force, could not.” That concern is not merely rhetorical. It reflects a familiar principle of statutory construction: where Congress has enacted a remedial statute to protect persons from classifications rooted in disability, courts should not adopt an interpretation that allows the protected trait to become the mechanism by which benefits are denied, unless the text clearly requires that result. The employer’s contrary position depends upon treating retirement status as wholly independent of disability, even where the record shows that the retirement category was created or applied in a manner that tracks disability itself. Such formalism is insufficient. A plan may, of course, draw legitimate distinctions among employees and retirees based on tenure, contribution history, age, or other neutral criteria. But a distinction is not neutral merely because it is expressed in the vocabulary of plan administration. If the challenged rule withholds a benefit from those who retire because they are disabled, while preserving it for those who retire under other circumstances, the court must examine substance rather than labels. The broader construction adopted by the Second Circuit better accords with the purpose of disability-discrimination law. The relevant inquiry is not whether every disabled retiree is entitled to identical benefits in all respects, nor whether an employer must disregard every actuarial or financial consideration. The question is whether the employer has imposed a disability-based burden without adequate justification. A cramped interpretation would allow an employer to accomplish indirectly what the statute forbids directly: identifying a class by reference to disabling condition and then attaching inferior terms to that class. Nor is the feared burden on benefit plans persuasive. Recognizing that disabled retirees are protected from irrational discrimination does not convert courts into plan administrators. It simply requires that classifications affecting disabled persons be supported by lawful and rational grounds. Employers remain free to design retirement programs, to negotiate collectively bargained benefits, and to account for cost, risk, and eligibility requirements. What they may not do is rely on disability as a proxy for exclusion while insisting that the resulting disparity is beyond judicial review because it appears within a retirement plan. Accordingly, the Second Circuit’s reasoning gives proper effect to both text and purpose. It avoids an anomalous regime in which protection turns on happenstance: whether a disabled worker could remain nominally employed long enough to qualify under one provision, whether the employer characterized the separation as ordinary retirement rather than disability retirement, or whether administrative terminology obscured the operative discrimination. Statutes directed at equality should not be read to tolerate such arbitrary divisions absent unmistakable command. The more persuasive rule is that disabled retirees, no less than disabled employees, may challenge benefit classifications that single them out because of disability and impose unequal terms without lawful justification.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: In Parker v. Winwood, 938 F.3d 833 (6th Cir. 2019), the Sixth Circuit addressed precisely this issue. There, the plaintiff claimed that the defendants copied a musical composition and urged the court to send the case to a jury based on access and asserted similarities. The court rejected that approach. It held that copyright law protects original expression, not general ideas, familiar devices, or elements common to a genre. A plaintiff therefore must identify protectable similarities that permit a reasonable inference of copying. Similarities that arise from standard musical conventions do not carry that burden. That reasoning controls here. Here, the plaintiff identifies broad features shared by the two works, including tempo, mood, subject matter, and a recurring progression. Those features may create a surface resemblance, but they do not show unlawful copying. The record shows that each challenged element appears throughout the relevant field. The progression follows a common pattern. Lyrical themes draw from ordinary vocabulary. The structure reflects a conventional arrangement used by many works of the same type. Copyright does not allow one author to remove such materials from public use by combining them in a familiar way. Parker also forecloses the plaintiff’s effort to treat access as a substitute for protectable similarity. Access can support an inference of copying only after the plaintiff identifies expression that the law protects. Without that showing, access proves little. A defendant may have heard a work and still remain free to use stock themes, public-domain material, and common techniques. For that reason, the plaintiff’s evidence of access does not create a triable issue by itself. The plaintiff argues that the works must be compared as a whole. That principle is sound, but it does not permit a court to ignore the boundary between protected and unprotected material. Parker requires filtration before comparison. Once commonplace elements are removed, the remaining similarities are too general to support infringement. The protectable choices in the plaintiff’s work concern the particular sequence, phrasing, and selection of expressive details. By contrast, the accused work makes different choices in those respects. Nor does expert testimony change the result. An expert may assist the court in identifying technical features, but the expert cannot expand copyright protection beyond its legal limits. Here, the report lists similarities at a high level and attaches significance to conventions that belong to the genre. Parker rejected the same method because it risks converting ordinary creative tools into private property. As a result, the summary judgment record presents no genuine dispute on substantial similarity. The plaintiff has shown that the works share unprotected building blocks. But the plaintiff has not shown that the defendant copied protectable expression. Under Parker, that failure ends the claim. The defendants are entitled to judgment as a matter of law.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: Second, had Congress intended to control which coverages employers must offer, it would have said so plainly. The presumption against implied mandates is well established. When a statute imposes affirmative obligations on private parties, particularly obligations that dictate the substance of employment benefits, Congress ordinarily specifies the required terms with precision. It identifies the benefits that must be provided, the classes of employees entitled to them, and the standards governing their scope. The absence of that specificity here is telling. The statutory text before us contains no such command. It prohibits certain forms of discrimination in the administration of benefits already offered, but it does not compel any employer to offer a particular coverage in the first instance. The distinction is not a technicality. A rule against discriminatory administration presupposes an existing benefit and regulates how the employer treats it. A rule requiring the benefit itself creates the obligation from nothing. These are different legal duties, and Congress knows how to impose each. Elsewhere in the same body of legislation, Congress demonstrated its capacity to mandate coverage directly when it wished to do so. Where it required employers to provide specific benefits, it enumerated them. Where it left the choice to the employer, it regulated only the manner in which chosen benefits were administered. Reading a coverage mandate into a nondiscrimination provision would erase a line that Congress itself drew and maintained throughout the statute. The consequences of the contrary reading confirm its implausibility. If a duty to provide certain coverages could be inferred from a duty not to discriminate, employers would face liability for failing to offer benefits the statute never named. Courts would be left to determine, without textual guidance, which coverages the mandate reaches and which it does not. That inquiry would proceed through judicial estimation rather than legislative direction. Congress does not delegate questions of that magnitude by silence. The party urging the broader reading responds that a nondiscrimination rule serves little purpose if employers may decline to offer coverage at all. The premise is mistaken. A rule governing administration retains full effect for the benefits an employer chooses to provide. It ensures that those benefits reach all eligible employees on equal terms. The provision does its assigned work without being enlarged into a mandate its language does not support. We therefore decline to read a coverage requirement into text that speaks only to administration. The ordinary meaning of the words governs, and the ordinary meaning imposes no obligation to offer any particular coverage. Congress remains free to require specific benefits by legislation that says so. Until it does, the courts may not supply the command through interpretation. This conclusion rests on the settled principle that significant obligations require clear expression. The presumption protects regulated parties from duties they had no fair notice they bore. Applied here, it forecloses the expansive construction the statute cannot sustain.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: BEEZER, Circuit Judge: Jose Luis Flores-Garcia appeals his conviction for assisting an inadmissible alien felon to enter the United States, in violation of 8 U.S.C. § 1327. He argues that the district court should have entered a judgment of acquittal because the government failed to prove that he knew the passenger in his vehicle had a prior felony conviction that made him inadmissible. He also challenges the court’s instruction on knowledge. We affirm. Border Patrol agents stopped Flores-Garcia’s van shortly after it crossed through an area known for unlawful entries. The van carried three passengers. One passenger, Raul Mendoza, had no permission to enter the United States and previously had sustained a felony drug conviction. The government introduced certified records of that conviction and testimony showing that Flores-Garcia and Mendoza had traveled together before the attempted crossing. Agents also testified that Flores-Garcia gave inconsistent accounts of where he met Mendoza, where they were going, and why they selected the route they used. Section 1327 requires proof both that the defendant knowingly assisted an alien to enter the United States and that he knew facts that made the alien inadmissible under § 1182(a)(2). The statute does not require proof that the defendant recited the Immigration Code or understood the legal consequence of the felony conviction. It requires knowledge of the underlying circumstance that triggers inadmissibility. A contrary rule would convert a knowledge requirement into a demand for legal training and would defeat the statute’s evident purpose. The evidence satisfied that standard. Mendoza testified that he told Flores-Garcia before the trip that he had been imprisoned for a drug offense. The jury did not have to accept Flores-Garcia’s claim that he understood Mendoza to mean only a minor offense. Mendoza described prison time, not a traffic citation or local fine. The route, timing, payment arrangement, and false statements to agents reinforced the inference that Flores-Garcia knew he was transporting a person who could not lawfully enter. The instruction tracked the statute and required the jury to find both that Flores-Garcia knew Mendoza was an alien and that Mendoza had been convicted of a felony drug offense. That formulation protected the mens rea element. Flores-Garcia requested an instruction requiring knowledge that Mendoza was legally inadmissible. The district court properly rejected it because it misstated the law. Nor did the admission of the conviction records create unfair prejudice. The records established an element of the offense. The court limited their use, and the government did not invite the jury to convict Flores-Garcia based on Mendoza’s character or criminal history. A rational jury could find every element beyond a reasonable doubt. The conviction is affirmed.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: If the defendant’s knowledge of the reason why an alien is inadmissible is an element of section 1327, the government would be required to prove more than the defendant’s awareness that the alien lacked permission to enter the United States or was attempting to evade inspection. It would have to establish, beyond a reasonable doubt, that the defendant knew the facts that made the alien inadmissible under the particular statutory ground charged. That requirement follows from the structure of the offense. Section 1327 does not punish assistance to any alien whose entry is unlawful in some general sense; it targets assistance to an alien who is inadmissible on specified grounds. If scienter extends to that circumstance, the government must link the defendant’s state of mind to the disqualifying condition itself. Such a construction would not require proof that the defendant understood the immigration laws, identified the correct subsection of the inadmissibility statute, or appreciated the legal consequence that the alien was “inadmissible.” Criminal statutes ordinarily require knowledge of facts, not knowledge of law, unless Congress clearly provides otherwise. Thus, where inadmissibility rests on a prior aggravated felony, the government would need to show that the defendant knew of the alien’s relevant criminal history, not that he knew the offense qualified as an aggravated felony. Where inadmissibility rests on prior removal, the government would need to prove knowledge of that removal or of facts plainly conveying it. Where the ground concerns a controlled-substance violation, the proof would have to show knowledge of the alien’s drug-trafficking or drug-related conduct, not merely suspicion that the alien preferred clandestine entry. This burden may be satisfied by circumstantial evidence. Direct admissions are not necessary. A jury may infer knowledge from the defendant’s relationship with the alien, prior conversations, planning, payments, concealment measures, route selection, possession of documents, or statements made during the venture. But the inference must be directed to the charged ground of inadmissibility. Evidence that the defendant knew the alien was undocumented, or that the crossing was secretive, may support a smuggling charge under other provisions, but it does not automatically establish knowledge of a separate statutory bar that triggers section 1327. To hold otherwise would collapse the distinct elements of the offense and allow the government to substitute general awareness of illegality for knowledge of the aggravating circumstance Congress selected. This interpretation also preserves the statute’s evident severity. Section 1327 carries consequences aimed at persons who assist the entry of aliens whose presence Congress has deemed especially objectionable. Requiring proof that the defendant knew the operative facts prevents the enhanced punishment from attaching to a driver, guide, or intermediary who knowingly assisted an unlawful entry but had no awareness of the alien’s prior removal, criminal conviction, or other disabling condition.
