The government remains free to prosecute such conduct under statutes requiring only knowledge of unlawful entry. But when it invokes section 1327, it must prove the mental state that corresponds to the feature distinguishing that offense from ordinary alien smuggling.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: ALDISERT, Circuit Judge: The question for decision is whether the federal Assimilative Crimes Act, 18 U.S.C. § 13, incorporates a state drunk-driving statute for prosecution of an offense committed on a military reservation, where Congress has not enacted a parallel federal proscription. We hold that it does, and we affirm the conviction. The Act extends to federal enclaves the criminal law of the state within whose exterior boundaries the enclave lies. Its purpose is to fill gaps in the body of federal criminal law that governs conduct on lands subject to exclusive or concurrent federal jurisdiction. Congress recognized that it could not enumerate every offense a state might punish. Rather than legislate a comprehensive criminal code for each installation, base, and post, it adopted state law by reference, subject to the limitation that no state offense may be assimilated where a federal statute already governs the same conduct. The appellant operated a motor vehicle on the reservation while intoxicated. He was charged under the state statute, assimilated through § 13. He contends that assimilation was improper because federal regulations promulgated for the installation address vehicular operation and thereby occupy the field. We reject the argument. The regulations he cites govern speed limits and registration of vehicles. They do not reach operation while intoxicated. A regulation directed to one hazard does not preempt assimilation of a state law directed to a different one. The test is whether Congress has defined and punished the precise act charged, not whether the general subject of motor-vehicle operation has received federal attention. We find no federal enactment that punishes driving while intoxicated on this enclave. The gap the Act was designed to close is present. The state statute supplies the governing rule of conduct, and its elements were proved. The appellant next urges that assimilation of a state offense carrying a suspended license as a collateral consequence works an improper delegation of federal sentencing authority. The contention misreads the statute. Section 13 assimilates the substantive offense and its penalty. It does not import state administrative sanctions that operate independently of the criminal judgment. The district court imposed only the fine and term of confinement that the state statute authorizes. No license suspension formed part of the federal sentence, and none was ordered. The remaining assignments of error concern the sufficiency of the evidence. The record contains the arresting officer's testimony, the results of a breath analysis, and the appellant's admissions. A rational factfinder could conclude beyond a reasonable doubt that the appellant operated the vehicle while his blood-alcohol concentration exceeded the statutory threshold. The judgment of the district court will be affirmed.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: Although the district court did not address § 232, the issue is properly before us because it was pressed by the parties, turns on a purely legal question, and may be resolved on the existing record. We may affirm on any ground supported by the record, and nothing in the posture of this case requires a remand merely to obtain an initial ruling on a statutory provision whose operation is clear. The omission is therefore not jurisdictional, nor does it foreclose our consideration of the statute’s effect. Section 232 supplies an independent limitation on the relief sought. Its operative language is mandatory, not precatory. When the statutory prerequisites are present, the court is directed to give effect to the prescribed consequence, and equitable considerations cannot enlarge judicial authority beyond that boundary. The appellant’s attempt to characterize § 232 as a discretionary procedural safeguard is inconsistent with both the text and structure of the enactment. The provision does not merely regulate timing or manner; it defines the substantive conditions under which the asserted claim may proceed. The record establishes those conditions. The relevant notice was issued, the administrative determination became final, and the plaintiff did not pursue the exclusive method of review made available by the statute. Having bypassed that avenue, the plaintiff may not obtain the same relief indirectly through this action. To permit such a collateral challenge would defeat Congress’s evident choice to channel disputes through a specific process, with designated standards and deadlines. Courts ordinarily presume that such a scheme is meaningful, particularly where Congress has coupled a right of review with limitations designed to ensure finality. Nor is there merit in the contention that applying § 232 here would work an unfair surprise. The statute was invoked below, the facts bearing on its application were undisputed, and the plaintiff had a full opportunity to respond. The district court’s silence on the point may reflect the sufficiency of its other grounds, but it does not create a waiver by the appellee or an entitlement by the appellant to further proceedings. Appellate review is concerned with the correctness of the judgment, not the completeness of every step in the lower court’s reasoning. The remaining objections are variations on the same theme. The plaintiff argues that § 232 should yield because the complaint alleges constitutional and ultra vires conduct. But artful pleading cannot overcome a statutory bar where the injury alleged is inseparable from the agency action committed to the prescribed review mechanism. The complaint seeks to undo, restrain, or impose liability for the consequences of that action. Section 232 therefore applies according to its terms. Accordingly, even assuming arguendo that the district court’s stated rationale was incomplete, the judgment was correct. Section 232 precludes the relief requested, and the undisputed facts bring this case within its scope. The judgment should be affirmed on that alternative ground.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The Assimilative Crimes Act directs a federal court sitting over an enclave to borrow state criminal law only when federal law leaves a real gap. It does not make the State the primary lawgiver for federal property, and it does not permit displacement of a federal policy choice. But when Congress has not defined the offense at issue, state law supplies the missing rule and gives the United States a charge that otherwise would not exist. That condition exists here. The federal code punishes assault, theft, weapons possession, and many traffic offenses on federal land. It does not address the specific conduct charged here: operating a motor vehicle after the State has suspended the driver's license for refusal to submit to chemical testing. The state statute targets that precise conduct. Its elements do not conflict with any federal offense, and its penalty fits within the Act's limits. Assimilation respects congressional design. It prevents a driver from escaping punishment because the offense occurred inside a national park rather than on the adjacent state road. It also preserves consistent treatment for licenses that the State issues, suspends, and regulates. The indictment properly rests on the state offense that the Act assimilates as federal law.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The Appellants' claims under 42 U.S.C. § 1985(3) fail at their foundation. The statute reaches private conspiracies to deprive persons of the equal protection of the laws, but it does not create a general federal remedy for every conspiracy that works an injury. The Supreme Court has held that a plaintiff must show some racial or otherwise class-based animus behind the conspirators' actions. Absent that showing, the claim cannot proceed, regardless of the harm alleged. The Appellants offered no evidence that the defendants acted from any invidious purpose directed at a protected class. Their complaint recites the elements of a conspiracy in conclusory terms and alleges that the defendants coordinated to injure their business interests. Economic motive, however, is not the animus the statute contemplates. The record contains no proof that the defendants targeted the Appellants because of race, or because of membership in any group the courts have recognized as protected under § 1985(3). The Appellants' own submissions describe a dispute over commercial advantage, not one rooted in discrimination. At summary judgment, the Appellants bore the burden of pointing to specific facts from which a reasonable jury could infer discriminatory intent. They did not carry that burden. Their opposition rested on the assertion that discriminatory purpose could be inferred from the coordinated conduct itself. That inference does not follow. Coordinated conduct establishes concerted action, but concerted action is a separate element from the animus the statute requires. Proof of one does not supply proof of the other. The Appellants conflated the two and left the animus element unsupported. The Appellants also urged that the district court should have permitted the intent question to reach a jury because intent is ordinarily a factual matter. Intent is often a jury question when the record contains evidence from which a factfinder could resolve it either way. Here the record contained no such evidence. A party cannot force a trial on animus by labeling the question factual when it has produced nothing to place the animus in dispute. The district court correctly declined to send an empty question to the jury. Nor does the Appellants' reliance on circumstantial evidence rescue the claim. Circumstantial proof of intent is permissible, and a plaintiff need not produce a defendant's admission of prejudice. The circumstances the Appellants identified, though, point only to a commercial rivalry. Nothing in the timing, the pattern of dealings, or the defendants' statements suggests that the class-based hostility the statute requires played any role. The circumstantial evidence, taken in the light most favorable to the Appellants, still does not reach discriminatory purpose. Because the Appellants failed to establish discriminatory intent, an essential element of any claim under § 1985(3), the district court properly entered judgment against them on this count. We affirm that ruling.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: In my opinion, the retailer may maintain the challenged customer notice if the notice accurately states the source of the information, avoids implying governmental compulsion, and does not misstate the legal effect of Levi Strauss’s position. The practice does not become unlawful merely because Levi Strauss requested it. A retailer may repeat a supplier’s product-related communication when the communication concerns authenticity, warranty status, channel restrictions, labeling, care instructions, or other facts material to a buyer’s decision. The retailer still bears responsibility for the words it publishes at the point of sale. The operative concern is attribution. As a retailer, it may enforce this practice because Levi Strauss requests that customers be notified that Levi Strauss has advised the retailer that certain goods, offers, or sales practices do not meet Levi Strauss’s stated distribution requirements. That formulation identifies Levi Strauss as the source of the assertion. It also preserves the retailer’s separate role. The retailer does not certify the truth of every underlying statement by placing customers on notice that Levi Strauss has given the advice. But if the retailer knows the advice is false, outdated, or misleading in context, continued use of the notice would expose the retailer to ordinary claims for deceptive trade practices. The notice should therefore use restrained language. It may say that Levi Strauss has advised the retailer of a stated position. The notice should not say that the customer has no rights unless the governing warranty, statute, and sales documents support that conclusion. Nor should it describe goods as counterfeit unless the retailer has a factual basis for that charge. It should not state that resale is illegal when the issue is only a contractual distribution preference. Courts distinguish between a truthful channel disclosure and a statement that disparages lawful goods. The retailer also should avoid presenting Levi Strauss’s request as a command that relieves the retailer of judgment. A supplier’s request can explain why the practice exists, but it cannot supply a defense to a misleading point-of-sale representation. The retailer controls its premises, its website, and its customer communications. It selects the language, decides where to display it, and benefits from the customer’s reliance. Those facts support treating the notice as the retailer’s own commercial speech for purposes of consumer protection law. A compliant notice can serve legitimate interests. Customers often care whether a product carries a manufacturer warranty, whether the manufacturer will provide repair support, and whether the item came through authorized channels. The retailer may give that information before sale. Full disclosure reduces later disputes and allows the buyer to weigh price against service limitations. The law favors accurate pre-sale information over silence followed by surprise. For those reasons, I would uphold the practice only in its accurate, attributed form. The retailer may notify customers that Levi Strauss has advised it of Levi Strauss’s stated position. It may not convert that advice into an overbroad legal warning or a factual accusation the retailer cannot substantiate.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: With respect to Section E, the Court is persuaded that the provision must be enforced according to its ordinary meaning, read in the context of the agreement as a whole and in light of the evident commercial purpose the parties sought to achieve. Section E does not stand as an isolated recital or aspirational statement. It assigns a concrete obligation, identifies the circumstances under which that obligation arises, and supplies a standard by which performance may be measured. The fact that the provision is concise does not render it indefinite. Contractual language need not anticipate every possible contingency; it is sufficient if the Court can determine, by accepted principles of construction, what the parties required and whether the challenged conduct conformed to that requirement. The opposing interpretation would deprive Section E of practical effect. Courts do not favor constructions that render negotiated language surplusage, particularly where, as here, the provision appears in a sequence of duties that allocate risk between sophisticated parties. If the parties had intended Section E to be merely discretionary, they could have used permissive language. They did not. The operative terms impose a mandatory undertaking, and the surrounding provisions confirm that the undertaking was material to the bargain. The Court therefore rejects the contention that compliance with Section E was optional or subject solely to one party’s unilateral judgment. Nor is the provision unenforceable for uncertainty. The record reflects a course of performance sufficient to illuminate the parties’ shared understanding. For a substantial period, both sides acted as though Section E required timely disclosure, reasonable cooperation, and documentation adequate to permit verification of performance. That conduct is admissible not to vary the written terms, but to confirm the meaning a reasonable contracting party would have attributed to them at the time of execution. The later assertion of ambiguity, made only after a dispute arose, is entitled to little weight. The Court also concludes that the breach was not merely technical. The failure to comply with Section E impaired the nonbreaching party’s ability to protect its contractual interests and to make informed decisions contemplated by the agreement. A party may not withhold material information, frustrate the agreed process, and then argue that the consequences are speculative because the very omission prevented a complete assessment of harm. While damages must be proven with reasonable certainty, the law does not require mathematical precision where the injury flows naturally from a breach and the evidence supplies a rational basis for calculation. Equitable considerations reinforce, rather than undermine, this conclusion. Enforcement of Section E does not impose a forfeiture or confer an unwarranted windfall. It holds the breaching party to the allocation of responsibilities it accepted. The Court’s role is not to rewrite the agreement to relieve a party from a term that later proved inconvenient, but to give effect to the lawful bargain made. Accordingly, Section E is valid, enforceable, and material, and the established failure to perform under that section supports the relief awarded herein.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The Council brought this action to enjoin Produce, a wholesale distributor, from operating a loading facility within the municipal boundary without a permit. The Council relies on Ordinance 14-7, which requires any commercial enterprise moving perishable goods in volumes exceeding two tons daily to secure a conditional-use license. Produce concedes it operates without such a license but contends the ordinance exceeds the Council's delegated authority. The threshold question is whether the Council possessed power to regulate this class of activity. The enabling statute grants municipalities authority over land use, traffic, and public sanitation. Produce argues that the movement of perishable goods falls under the exclusive jurisdiction of the state agricultural board, and that the Council intruded on a preempted field. That argument fails. The state statute governs grading, inspection, and labeling of produce. It says nothing about where and when a distributor may operate a loading dock. The Council did not attempt to set standards for the goods themselves. It regulated the location and hours of a commercial operation, a matter squarely within its zoning power. Produce next contends that the ordinance is unconstitutionally vague. It points to the phrase "perishable goods" and argues that a reasonable operator cannot know which items trigger the licensing requirement. The ordinance defines perishable goods as any item requiring refrigeration to prevent spoilage within seventy-two hours. That definition supplies an objective standard. Produce's own shipping manifests classify its inventory under refrigeration codes matching that definition. A party whose conduct falls plainly within a statute cannot complain that the statute is vague as applied to marginal cases. The Council must still demonstrate that Produce's operation actually crosses the two-ton threshold. The record contains uncontested manifests showing daily volumes between four and six tons over a six-month period. Produce offered no evidence to the contrary. It instead argued that the threshold should be measured by the weight of goods sold rather than the weight moved through the facility. Nothing in the ordinance supports that reading. The text refers to goods "loaded, unloaded, or staged" at the site. Weight moved, not weight sold, is the operative measure. The remaining issue concerns the remedy. The Council seeks a permanent injunction. Produce argues that an injunction would inflict disproportionate harm because relocation would take months and disrupt existing supply contracts. The balance of hardships does not favor Produce. It operated in knowing violation for over a year after receiving written notice. A party that proceeds in defiance of a valid ordinance cannot invoke the burdens of compliance to defeat enforcement. The court holds that Ordinance 14-7 rests within the Council's authority, that it is sufficiently definite, and that Produce's operation violates its terms. The Council is entitled to injunctive relief. The injunction will take effect in ninety days to allow orderly transition, after which continued operation without a license will subject Produce to the daily penalties the ordinance prescribes. The Council's request for retroactive fines is denied for lack of adequate notice as to the accrual method.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The record does not support Al-Saeed’s claim that Laurie Buck refused his return at the Walker Road store. Al-Saeed identifies Buck as the employee who denied the transaction, but employment records place Buck at a Fred Meyer store in Anchorage, Alaska, on the day of the alleged refusal. That fact defeats the identification. A witness may rely on memory, but memory cannot overcome undisputed scheduling records, payroll entries, and store assignment data showing that the named employee worked hundreds of miles away. Al-Saeed offers no evidence that Buck traveled from Anchorage to the Walker Road location during the relevant shift. He does not identify a flight, a substitute shift, a corrected time entry, or any store record tying Buck to Walker Road that day. Nor does he produce a receipt, return log, register record, or manager note bearing Buck’s name. His assertion rests on his later recollection of the employee’s identity. The court does not disregard that recollection merely because the opposing party disputes it. Rather, the court rejects it because the documentary record squarely contradicts it. This discrepancy matters because Al-Saeed’s claim depends on the conduct of the person who handled the return. If Buck did not interact with him at Walker Road, then the allegation that Buck personally refused the return cannot establish liability. The claim may still turn on what occurred at the store, but it cannot proceed on a theory that assigns the act to an employee who was working in Anchorage at the same time. Al-Saeed argues that the employer’s records deserve little weight because retail scheduling systems can contain errors. That general proposition does not create a factual dispute. A party opposing summary judgment must identify evidence that a particular record contains a particular error. Al-Saeed points to none. The payroll record matches the posted schedule. Anchorage store staffing reports list Buck on duty. No Walker Road document lists her presence. The consistency among separate business records gives them substantial evidentiary force. The court also considers whether a reasonable juror could infer that another employee used Buck’s credentials or name. This record contains no basis for that inference. Al-Saeed did not testify that he saw a name badge bearing Buck’s name. He did not describe a register screen showing her credentials. He did not identify a manager who connected Buck to the incident. Without such evidence, the proposed inference rests on speculation, and speculation cannot defeat a properly supported motion. The court therefore treats Buck’s absence from the Walker Road store as established for purposes of this claim. Al-Saeed may dispute whether the store mishandled his attempted return, but he has not shown that Buck participated in that event. Any claim premised on Buck’s personal refusal fails for lack of evidence connecting her to the alleged transaction.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The declarant's statement, "my wife, she bought me those two," bears directly on the ownership defense raised at trial. The claimant attributes acquisition of the two items to a third party, his spouse, rather than to himself. This attribution matters because title and lawful possession turn on how the goods entered the household. If the spouse purchased the items with her own funds, the claimant holds them as a recipient, not a purchaser, and any lien asserted against his personal accounts fails to reach them. The statement also functions as an admission against the theory that the claimant obtained the goods through the transactions under scrutiny. It anchors the acquisition to a gift, which alters the applicable burden. The party asserting forfeiture must now trace the spouse's payment. Absent that tracing, the claimant retains a colorable interest in both items, and the court should treat his account as credible pending contrary proof.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: PREGERSON, Circuit Judge: Juan Miguel Palafox-Mazon, Carlos Gamboa-Miranda, and Ramon Garcia-Montijo entered the United States on foot, each carrying a backpack that contained approximately forty-seven pounds of marijuana. Border Patrol agents found them in a remote desert area near the border. The government charged each defendant with importation of marijuana and possession of marijuana with intent to distribute. Each defendant pleaded guilty. At sentencing, each requested a reduction for playing a minor role in the offense. The district court denied the requests and imposed sentences based on the full quantity each man carried. The question on appeal is whether the district court applied the Sentencing Guidelines with adequate attention to the defendants’ actual roles. The Guidelines require a sentencing court to measure a defendant’s culpability against the relevant conduct for which he is held accountable and, when the record permits, against other participants in the criminal activity. A courier does not receive a minor role reduction by virtue of being a courier. Nor does the label “courier” end the inquiry. The court must consider what the defendant knew, what he agreed to do, how much discretion he exercised, and how his conduct fit within the larger scheme. The record shows that these defendants performed a narrow function. They carried marijuana across the border. Nothing indicates that they planned the shipment, owned the drugs, recruited others, set the route, negotiated payment, or controlled distribution after entry. Their role exposed them to arrest while leaving the organizers insulated. That fact does not excuse the offense. It bears on punishment because the Guidelines distinguish between principal actors and those who provide limited labor for a criminal venture. The government argues that each defendant carried a substantial amount of marijuana and therefore cannot qualify for a reduction. Quantity matters, but quantity alone cannot decide the question. A person who carries forty-seven pounds of marijuana commits a serious crime. Yet a load bearer may remain less culpable than the persons who supply the drugs, direct the crossing, and receive the proceeds. If the district court treats the weight of the marijuana as dispositive, it fails to make the comparative assessment the Guidelines require. The sentencing transcript does not show that the district court made that assessment. The court emphasized the amount each defendant carried and the danger posed by drug trafficking. Those considerations support punishment, but they do not answer whether these defendants were substantially less culpable than the average participant in the offense. A sentencing court must ground its decision in the facts that bear on role, not in a categorical rule that border couriers are ineligible for relief. We vacate the sentences and remand for resentencing. On remand, the district court shall determine whether each defendant qualifies for a mitigating role adjustment based on the totality of the circumstances. The court may consider the drug quantity, the defendants’ compensation, their knowledge of the scheme, their authority, and their relationship to other participants. The judgment of conviction remains undisturbed. The sentences are vacated.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The record establishes that, when Border Patrol agents apprehended the Defendants, each Defendant was in possession of a backpack, and the agents seized those packs at the time of arrest. The agents also recovered several additional backpacks that had been dropped or left behind by other members of the group who fled into the brush. Defendants contend that the seizure of all backpacks was unlawful and that any evidence derived from them must be suppressed. The Court disagrees. As to the backpacks physically carried by the arrested Defendants, the seizure was reasonable. The arrests occurred in a remote border area during an active interdiction, after agents observed conduct consistent with unlawful entry and coordinated flight. The packs were immediately associated with the arrestees and were within the agents’ control at the moment of detention. Officers effecting a lawful arrest may secure personal effects accompanying an arrestee, both to preserve evidence and to protect officer safety. Even if a warrant later would have been required to search particular containers, the initial act of taking custody of the backpacks was not itself unconstitutional. Nor were agents required to ignore the backpacks abandoned by those who fled. The evidence shows that the additional packs were discarded during flight and left in open terrain. A person who voluntarily abandons property forfeits any reasonable expectation of privacy in it. That principle applies with particular force where, as here, the property was cast aside to facilitate escape from law enforcement. Defendants have not shown that they owned, possessed, or exercised control over the abandoned backpacks; nor have they established that any alleged expectation of privacy in those items remained objectively reasonable after the packs were relinquished. Defendants’ argument also fails for lack of standing as to backpacks not attributed to them. Fourth Amendment rights are personal and may not be vicariously asserted. A defendant seeking suppression must demonstrate that his own rights were violated by the challenged governmental conduct. Mere proximity to property, association with others, or membership in the same traveling group does not confer a privacy interest in every bag recovered from the scene. On this record, each Defendant may challenge only the seizure of his own backpack, and, for the reasons stated, those seizures were lawful. The circumstances confronting the agents further support the reasonableness of their actions. The arrests took place during a fluid encounter involving multiple individuals, some detained and others fleeing. The agents were not required to sort ownership claims in the field or leave containers behind in an unsecured area where evidence could be destroyed, removed, or reclaimed. Securing the backpacks was a limited and practical response to the situation presented. Accordingly, the Court concludes that the seizure of the Defendants’ backpacks incident to their arrests was constitutionally permissible, and that the abandoned backpacks recovered from the area were not protected by any privacy interest Defendants may invoke. Suppression is therefore unwarranted on this ground.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The plaintiff's theory of foreseeability rests on a contingency that the record does not support. She contends that the group's departure was so precarious that any interruption would have prevented them from reaching the crossing when they did, and that the defendant should therefore have anticipated the resulting harm. The argument depends on facts that the plaintiff never developed. There is nothing in the record to indicate that had one of the other persons tripped, fallen, or otherwise become unable to go, the remaining travelers would have altered their route or their timing in any way that bears on the injury. The distinction matters because foreseeability is measured against the circumstances actually shown, not against a hypothetical reconstruction the plaintiff prefers. A party who seeks to hold a defendant liable for failing to guard against a chain of events must place those events before the court through competent proof. The plaintiff offered no testimony from the companions about their intentions, no evidence of their physical condition, and no account of how the group organized its movement. The court cannot infer, from silence, that a stumble by one member would have delayed the others or diverted them from the path they took. We decline to treat the absence of evidence as though it favored the party who bore the burden of production. The plaintiff, not the defendant, needed to establish the causal sequence on which recovery depends. Having failed to do so, she asks the court to supply the missing links by assumption. That we will not do. The law of proximate cause requires a factual foundation, and speculation about what might have happened had events unfolded differently cannot substitute for that foundation. Nor does the general character of the outing rescue the claim. Even if the group traveled together and relied on one another for direction, the record contains no indication that the failure of any one member would have changed the outcome for the rest. The plaintiff's own account describes an ordinary passage, undertaken without incident by the companions who accompanied her. On that record, the injury she suffered cannot be attributed to the defendant's asserted duty to anticipate a disruption that never occurred and that the evidence gives no reason to expect. The trial court correctly recognized this gap. Its ruling did not resolve a dispute of fact against the plaintiff. It held that no dispute existed because the plaintiff produced nothing to create one. We agree. Where the record is barren of proof on an essential element, the defendant is entitled to judgment as a matter of law, and the plaintiff's speculation cannot fill the void.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: Indeed, the only “joint” activity that we can glean from the record is that each Defendant was hired by the same person to carry. That fact, standing alone, does not establish the kind of concerted action necessary to sustain liability on a theory requiring agreement, shared purpose, or coordinated execution. The evidence shows parallel assignments, not a common plan. It shows a common source of employment, not a meeting of the minds. And it shows that the Defendants may have been present in the same broader undertaking, not that they knowingly joined one another in furtherance of an unlawful objective. The distinction is not formalistic. Liability for joint conduct requires proof that each alleged participant embraced the essential nature of the venture and intended to contribute to its success. A person may accept a task from a third party without knowing who else has been engaged, what those others have been asked to do, or whether their efforts are connected in any meaningful way. The law does not permit such gaps to be filled by inference merely because the same intermediary issued similar instructions to multiple persons. To hold otherwise would transform ordinary association, or even mere coincidence of employment, into evidence of complicity. The record contains no communications among the Defendants reflecting coordination. There is no testimony that any Defendant recruited another, directed another, shared proceeds with another, divided responsibilities, or adjusted his conduct in response to another’s role. Nor is there evidence that any Defendant knew the identity, purpose, or intended conduct of the others. The asserted connection thus depends entirely on the identity of the hirer. But the hirer’s unilateral decision to engage multiple people cannot, without more, be attributed to the people hired as proof of their mutual agreement. The Government’s position would require the Court to infer a conspiracy from the architecture of the alleged scheme rather than from evidence of the Defendants’ assent to it. That approach reverses the required analysis. The existence of a larger plan conceived by one person does not establish that every person retained to perform some discrete act became a joint actor with every other person retained for a separate act. Each Defendant’s knowledge and intent must be assessed individually, based on evidence attributable to him. The record, however, supplies no basis to conclude that any Defendant understood himself to be acting in concert with the others, as opposed to performing a limited task at the request of the same individual. Accordingly, the evidence proves, at most, that the Defendants were separately engaged by a common principal. It does not prove that they combined with one another, agreed upon a shared unlawful end, or participated in a coordinated enterprise. Because the alleged joint activity rests on speculation rather than proof, it cannot support the conclusion that these Defendants acted jointly within the meaning required by law.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The government’s reliance on Ogbuehi does not carry the point. In that case, the defendant did more than participate in a criminal venture. He directed the conduct of other persons. After recruiting Teague and Williams, Ogbuehi arranged their travel, instructed them during the trip, and remained the person to whom they looked for direction as they moved toward the United States. His role showed authority over participants, not merely knowledge of a scheme or assistance in its execution. That distinction matters because the enhancement turns on control, organization, or management of people, not on the seriousness of the offense standing alone. The record here assigns that controlling role to Pedro Garcia. He recruited the smugglers, explained the job, and supplied the essential instructions about the load, the route, and the expected delivery. The couriers dealt with Garcia as the person who brought them into the venture and set its terms. Their participation depended on his assurances and directions. When the evidence identifies a particular actor as the one who obtained the couriers, instructed them, and positioned them within the drug-smuggling operation, the court cannot treat another defendant as the organizer by inference. The government points to the defendant’s presence during parts of the operation and to his association with Garcia. Those facts prove participation, but they do not prove a managerial role. A participant may know the purpose of the trip, assist with transportation, or benefit from the offense without exercising authority over another participant. The Guidelines require a concrete showing that the defendant controlled, supervised, or organized at least one other person. The evidence must connect the defendant to decisions about recruitment, assignment, payment, discipline, or direction. It does not do so here. Ogbuehi illustrates the missing proof. Teague and Williams relied on Ogbuehi throughout the journey. He functioned as the guide and source of instruction, and the success of their travel depended on his continuing direction. Here, by contrast, Garcia filled that position. The couriers’ connection to the scheme ran through him. The defendant did not select them, promise them compensation, explain their tasks, or direct their movements in the manner that supported the enhancement in Ogbuehi. This distinction does not minimize the defendant’s criminal liability. The jury’s findings and the undisputed facts establish that he joined the drug-smuggling offense. But sentencing law separates participation from leadership. The government must prove the aggravating role by a preponderance of the evidence, and the proof must identify conduct that places the defendant above another participant in the criminal hierarchy. Because the record shows that Garcia, rather than the defendant, recruited and directed the smugglers, Ogbuehi supports denial of the enhancement rather than its application.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: The plaintiffs in Auer, sergeants employed by the St. Louis Police Department, sought overtime compensation under the Fair Labor Standards Act. Their claim turned on whether they qualified as bona fide executive, administrative, or professional employees exempt from the Act's overtime requirements. The Secretary of Labor's regulations conditioned that exemption in part on a salary basis test, which required that an employee receive a predetermined amount not subject to reduction based on the quality or quantity of work performed. The employer argued that the sergeants met this standard. The sergeants responded that a departmental manual authorizing pay deductions for disciplinary infractions defeated the salary basis, because their compensation was theoretically subject to reduction. The central dispute concerned the meaning of the salary basis regulation, a text the Secretary had promulgated but had not defined with precision as to disciplinary deductions. The Secretary offered an interpretation in an amicus brief filed at the Court's invitation. That interpretation held that an employee is subject to improper deductions, and therefore not paid on a salary basis, only when there exists a clear policy permitting such deductions or an actual practice of imposing them. A theoretical possibility of deduction did not disqualify the employee. The Court adopted the Secretary's reading. Because the regulation was ambiguous on the question presented, the interpretation controlled unless it was plainly erroneous or inconsistent with the regulation itself. The Court found neither defect. The Secretary's construction reconciled the salary basis requirement with the practical realities of public employment, where disciplinary rules of general application often reach every worker in an agency. Reading the regulation to disqualify any employee nominally covered by such a policy would have swept broadly and defeated the exemption's purpose. The plaintiffs objected that the Secretary's position appeared for the first time in litigation and deserved no deference on that account. The Court rejected the objection. An agency's interpretation advanced in a legal brief carries weight when it reflects the agency's considered judgment rather than a post hoc rationalization designed to defend past action. Nothing in the record suggested that the Secretary's reading was contrived for the occasion or inconsistent with prior positions. The holding confirmed the principle that an agency's interpretation of its own regulation receives controlling weight absent plain error or inconsistency. The rationale rests on the agency's presumed familiarity with the purposes and operation of its rules. Applied here, that deference resolved the ambiguity in the salary basis test in the employer's favor and left the sergeants outside the overtime provisions. The judgment upheld the exemption and denied the compensation the plaintiffs claimed.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: LUCERO, Circuit Judge. This appeal requires us to decide whether officers violated the Fourth Amendment when they entered a home without a warrant after receiving a report that a child had been left unattended inside. The district court granted summary judgment to the officers on qualified immunity grounds. We affirm, though on a narrower basis than the district court adopted. The material facts come from the officers’ body camera recordings and from the undisputed portions of the record. A neighbor called 911 after hearing a child crying for nearly thirty minutes in an adjacent apartment. The caller reported that no adult answered repeated knocks. When Officers Martinez and Hale arrived, they heard intermittent crying from inside the unit. They knocked, announced their presence, and waited. No one responded. Through a front window, Officer Hale saw a small child standing near the kitchen counter. The child appeared distressed and did not answer when the officers asked whether an adult was present. The officers then opened the unlocked door and entered. They found the child alone, wearing a soiled diaper, and with a chair pulled against the stove. A burner knob had turned halfway, though no flame appeared. The officers remained inside until paramedics arrived and until the child’s mother returned approximately twelve minutes later. During that interval, Officer Martinez observed narcotics and a firearm on a coffee table. The State later charged the mother, Ms. Rivera, with child neglect and drug offenses. She brought this action under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 after the criminal court suppressed the evidence found in the apartment. We review summary judgment de novo. Qualified immunity protects officials unless the plaintiff shows a constitutional violation and clearly established law at the time of the conduct. The emergency aid exception permits warrantless entry when officers have an objectively reasonable basis to believe that a person inside needs immediate assistance. That rule does not require certainty. It requires facts that would lead a reasonable officer to act without the delay a warrant would cause. Those facts existed here. The officers faced a report of prolonged crying, silence in response to knocking, visual confirmation of a young child alone near a kitchen counter, and no indication that an adult could assist. The Fourth Amendment does not require officers to stand outside while they seek a warrant when a child inside may face immediate danger. Their initial entry therefore complied with the Constitution. The closer question concerns the officers’ continued presence after they found the child. That presence remained tied to the emergency. The child needed supervision and medical evaluation. The officers did not search drawers, containers, or closed rooms. Officer Martinez saw the contraband from a lawful vantage point while securing the child’s safety. The plain view doctrine allowed him to note what he saw. Because the officers did not violate Ms. Rivera’s Fourth Amendment rights, we need not decide whether the asserted right was clearly established. The judgment of the district court is affirmed.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: It is my opinion that the location of the subject lands within the “checkerboard” area of the Eastern Navajo Agency, while also lying within the exterior borders of the State of New Mexico, is not alone determinative of civil or regulatory jurisdiction. The controlling inquiry must proceed parcel by parcel, with attention to the present title status of each tract, the manner by which such title was acquired or reserved, and the federal statutes, treaties, executive orders, patents, and administrative records bearing upon the land. In this region, jurisdiction is frequently fragmented because Navajo trust lands, individual Indian allotments, federal lands, state lands, and privately owned fee lands are interspersed in a noncontiguous pattern. The mere fact that a parcel is geographically surrounded by non-Indian fee land does not divest it of its Indian country character if it is held in trust for the Navajo Nation or for individual Navajo allottees, or if it otherwise falls within the statutory definition of Indian country. For lands held by the United States in trust for the Navajo Nation, tribal and federal interests are at their strongest. State authority over such lands is presumptively limited, particularly where the asserted state regulation would interfere with tribal self-government, impair rights arising under federal law, or burden activities conducted by the Nation or its members on trust land. Similarly, individual allotments that remain in trust or restricted status ordinarily constitute Indian country, and state jurisdiction over those lands and over Navajo members upon them is constrained by federal law. The State’s territorial boundaries do not, by themselves, enlarge state power over Indian country, nor do they extinguish the distinct sovereign interests preserved by Congress. Different considerations may apply to fee lands within the checkerboard, especially where the land is owned by non-Indians and no specific federal restriction remains. Even there, however, the analysis is not mechanical. Tribal authority may still be implicated where conduct on fee land threatens or has a direct effect upon the political integrity, economic security, health, welfare, or internal relations of the Navajo Nation, or where the nonmember has entered consensual relationships with the Nation or its members. Conversely, state authority may be more readily sustained on non-Indian fee land absent a conflicting federal statute, a protected tribal interest, or a demonstrated infringement upon tribal self-government. Accordingly, any legal conclusion respecting these lands must avoid treating the checkerboard area as a uniform jurisdictional category. The proper conclusion depends upon verified land status and the nature of the activity at issue. A title status report, Bureau of Indian Affairs records, county land records, applicable patents, and any governing leases, rights-of-way, or federal approvals should be reviewed before any final jurisdictional determination is made. Subject to that verification, lands in trust or restricted Indian ownership should be treated as Indian country notwithstanding their location within New Mexico, and any state action affecting such lands should be scrutinized for consistency with federal Indian law and the retained sovereignty of the Navajo Nation.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: The framework governing primary enforcement responsibility under the Safe Drinking Water Act assumes a clean division of regulatory authority. In practice, that assumption breaks down where a State and an Indian tribe advance competing claims to the same territory. When each entity insists that the underground injection control program within a disputed area falls under its own jurisdiction, the Administrator confronts a threshold question that neither the statute nor its implementing regulations resolve with any precision. The difficulty is structural. A grant of primacy requires the Administrator to find that the applicant possesses authority over the wells and formations at issue. Where jurisdiction is contested, that finding cannot be made for either party without adjudicating the boundary itself. The Administrator has consistently declined to serve as the arbiter of such disputes, reasoning that questions of tribal territory and State reach turn on treaties, statutes, and land title that lie outside the agency's competence. The result is that both applications stall. The State cannot demonstrate authority the tribe denies, and the tribe cannot demonstrate authority the State denies. This outcome produces a regulatory vacuum. Neither sovereign obtains primacy over the disputed lands, so the federal program applies by default under the Act's backstop provisions. That default is not a neutral placeholder. It shifts operational burden to the Environmental Protection Agency for territory that Congress intended to be administered locally, and it leaves operators uncertain about which permitting regime governs their wells. Uncertainty of that kind discourages compliance and complicates enforcement, because an operator faced with two claimants may reasonably question the authority of either to impose conditions. The prospect that a jurisdictional disagreement can indefinitely suspend the primacy of both a State and a tribe deserves closer attention than it has received. The Act was designed to devolve enforcement responsibility, not to condition that responsibility on the prior settlement of every territorial question. Reading the statute to require resolution of boundary disputes as a precondition to any grant of primacy converts a program about groundwater protection into a forum for litigating sovereignty. That reading serves no one. It delays protection of the resource the Act was enacted to safeguard, and it rewards whichever party benefits from the status quo of federal administration. A more workable approach would permit the Administrator to grant primacy over the undisputed portions of a proposed program while reserving the contested area for separate treatment. Under such an approach, the disagreement would narrow the scope of each grant rather than defeat it. The disputed lands could remain under federal oversight pending resolution in an appropriate forum, and the operators outside the contested zone would gain the certainty that a single enforcing authority provides. Whatever the merits of that solution, the present rule leaves both sovereigns without primary enforcement responsibility whenever they cannot agree, and that is a poor allocation of a shared regulatory task.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: Following EPA Region 6’s August 24, 1995 disapproval of the TAD submission, NMED did not treat the matter as closed or acquiesce in the premise that the jurisdictional and technical deficiencies identified by Region 6 were incapable of correction. Rather, the record reflects that NMED entered into an extended intergovernmental process with EPA Region 9 and the Navajo Nation directed toward resolving the specific objections that had prevented approval. That course was neither perfunctory nor ministerial. It required reconciliation of overlapping regulatory interests, clarification of the geographic and legal reach of state-administered environmental programs, and consideration of the Navajo Nation’s sovereign interests in lands and facilities potentially affected by the proposed authorization. In assessing the legal significance of that process, the salient point is that Region 6’s disapproval was not a final adjudication of all future authority, but an agency determination addressed to the submission then before it. NMED was therefore entitled, and indeed obligated, to pursue further consultation where the disapproval turned on issues susceptible to factual development, interagency coordination, or modification of the proposed program. The subsequent involvement of Region 9 was especially material because that Region possessed federal administrative responsibility for matters involving the Navajo Nation, and its participation provided the appropriate federal forum for addressing tribal concerns that Region 6 could not resolve unilaterally. The Navajo Nation’s participation likewise was not merely advisory. Any lawful resolution required meaningful engagement with the Nation’s governmental position, particularly where the disputed matters implicated tribal lands, tribal members, or regulatory consequences within Indian country. NMED’s decision to proceed through consultation rather than unilateral assertion of authority is consistent with settled principles of cooperative federalism as applied in the environmental context. State implementation authority, even when approved by EPA, does not operate in disregard of tribal sovereignty; nor does the existence of tribal interests categorically foreclose state participation where federal law permits a coordinated approach. The administrative record therefore must be read in light of that balance. The length of the process further supports, rather than undermines, the conclusion that the parties were attempting to cure the grounds of disapproval in a legally durable manner. Delay alone does not establish agency abandonment, waiver, or bad faith. Where the subject concerns the allocation of environmental regulatory responsibility among a state agency, a federal regional office, and a tribal sovereign, a protracted exchange of technical positions, jurisdictional analyses, and proposed revisions is to be expected. The relevant inquiry is whether the process was directed toward the deficiencies identified in the August 24, 1995 decision and whether the participating governments had a reasonable basis for continuing to negotiate. On this record, NMED’s post-disapproval conduct is best understood as a continuing effort to obtain an administratively acceptable resolution, not as an impermissible collateral attack on Region 6’s decision. The engagement with EPA Region 9 and the Navajo Nation was the procedurally proper means of addressing the intergovernmental issues that the disapproval brought to the surface.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: While NMED argues that the relevant final action of which it seeks review, with respect to both properties at issue, is EPA’s action of declining to require further federal response, that formulation does not match the administrative record. The agency did not issue a command, impose an obligation, release a regulated party from liability, or alter NMED’s legal authority over either property. EPA reviewed submissions, stated its technical view of the existing data, and declined to undertake additional federal work at that time. That decision may have practical consequences for NMED’s enforcement strategy, but practical disappointment does not convert every agency communication into final agency action. Under the Administrative Procedure Act, two conditions must be satisfied. A challenged action must mark the consummation of the agency’s decision-making process, and it must determine rights or produce legal consequences. A definitive agency statement can satisfy the first condition. Satisfying the second requires more than a closed file or an unfavorable explanation. NMED identifies no statute, regulation, permit, or order under which EPA’s letters changed the legal status of either property. Nor does NMED show that EPA’s position bound the property owners, barred state action, or foreclosed later federal action if new information justified it. The record instead shows a continuing technical exchange between federal and state officials. EPA described the evidence it found adequate, identified gaps it considered immaterial, and explained why it would not refer the sites for further federal response. Those communications reflected the agency’s present enforcement judgment. Enforcement judgments ordinarily do not create reviewable final action unless the agency adopts a binding legal determination. EPA did not do so here. It left NMED free to investigate under state law, pursue state remedies, request further consultation, or submit new data. NMED’s contrary view would treat an agency’s refusal to act as reviewable whenever a state agency disagrees with the reasons given. That approach would blur the line between final action and ongoing intergovernmental coordination. Congress did not authorize courts to supervise every technical disagreement between EPA and a state regulator. Judicial review attaches when the agency fixes legal rights or duties, not when it explains why it will not allocate federal resources to a site. This conclusion applies to both properties. Although the correspondence differs in detail, its legal character is the same. EPA did not approve a cleanup plan with binding effect, issue a no further action covenant, amend a permit, or preempt state authority. It communicated its present position based on the record before it. Because NMED has not identified a final agency action within the meaning of the APA, this court lacks a basis to review the merits of EPA’s technical conclusions. The petition must therefore be dismissed for want of reviewable agency action.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: The facts of that case concerned the termination of an environmental inspector employed by the Tribal Council to monitor compliance with the Safe Drinking Water Act. The inspector, hired under a written contract of indefinite duration, held responsibility for testing water quality at the reservation's public wells and reporting deficiencies to both the Council and the Environmental Protection Agency. After the inspector filed a report documenting elevated contaminant levels at three well sites, the Council voted to end his employment. He then sued, alleging that his discharge violated the whistleblower protections of the Act. The dispute presented two questions. The first was whether the Council enjoyed sovereign immunity from suit. The second was whether the Act's employee-protection provision applied to a tribal government acting in its capacity as an operator of public water systems. On the immunity question, the court held that the Council retained its sovereign status. The inspector argued that the Council waived immunity by accepting federal grant funds conditioned on compliance with the Act. The court rejected that theory. A waiver of tribal sovereign immunity cannot be implied from the acceptance of funds. It must be expressed clearly, and no such expression appeared in the grant agreements or in any tribal ordinance. The court found that Congress had not abrogated tribal immunity in the text of the Act, and the acceptance of a regulatory obligation does not, standing alone, subject the sovereign to private suit for damages. On the coverage question, the court reached a narrower conclusion. It observed that the Act's whistleblower provision reaches any "employer," a term the statute does not define to exclude tribal governments. The court reasoned that a tribe operating a public water system functions as a regulated entity in the same manner as a municipality or private utility. The protective purpose of the provision, which shields employees who report environmental violations, would be undermined if tribal inspectors could be dismissed for candid reporting without recourse. The court therefore held that the provision applied to the Council as an employer, even though sovereign immunity barred the particular relief sought. The two holdings, read together, left the inspector with a substantive right and no forum to enforce it against the Council itself. The court noted that the inspector's remedy, if any, lay in an administrative complaint to the Secretary or in suit against individual officials under the theory recognized in Ex parte Young. That result reflects the tension between the Act's regulatory reach and the doctrine of tribal immunity. Congress remains free to condition future grants on an express waiver, but the court declined to supply what the political branches had withheld.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: The Tribe contends that the federal trust responsibility requires dismissal of this employee-protection claim because the complaint names a tribal employer. That contention misstates the office of the trust doctrine. The doctrine guides construction when Congress leaves genuine doubt about the reach of a statute affecting Indians. It does not create a freestanding exemption from a statute whose text covers tribal employers and supplies a remedy for retaliation. The Safe Drinking Water Act uses broad terms in its whistleblower provision. It protects an employee who reports conduct concerning drinking-water requirements, participates in enforcement, or refuses to violate the Act. Congress chose language directed at “employers,” and the Act’s definitions bring Indian tribes within the regulated class. When Congress includes tribes in the statutory scheme, a court may not use the trust relationship to subtract them from it. The interpretive canon favoring tribes resolves ambiguity. It does not defeat expressed coverage. Osage Tribal Council v. United States Department of Labor confirms that point. The court there rejected the argument that tribal status barred an SDWA whistleblower proceeding. It treated the claim as a statutory retaliation claim, not as an intrusion into a tribe’s internal political structure. The employee sought the protection Congress attached to reports about drinking-water compliance. The source of the underlying duty mattered because it placed the case within the SDWA. It did not transform the trust doctrine into a jurisdictional shield. This distinction matters. A tribe retains its sovereignty, and courts must respect that sovereignty when Congress has not spoken. But sovereignty does not permit a court to rewrite a statute that speaks to tribes. The trust responsibility binds the United States in its dealings with tribes. It does not give a tribal employer license to discharge an employee for invoking federal environmental protections that Congress made applicable to tribal entities. Nor does the fact that the claim arises in a tribal workplace alter the analysis. Many federal statutes apply to employers whose operations serve governmental purposes. The inquiry turns on Congress’s language and the character of the claim. An SDWA whistleblower suit does not ask the court to supervise tribal membership, elections, domestic relations, or the allocation of traditional authority. It asks whether the employer retaliated against an employee for conduct protected by a federal environmental statute. That question falls within the remedial framework Congress enacted. The Tribe’s rule would make coverage depend on the identity of the defendant rather than the statutory text. It would also leave a gap in enforcement for drinking-water systems operated by tribes, even though Congress included tribes in the Act’s structure. The court has no basis to create that gap. The trust doctrine protects tribal interests in cases of statutory doubt. Here, the SDWA supplies the governing rule, and Osage Tribal correctly recognizes that the doctrine does not bar the whistleblower suit.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: Although we do not determine definitively whether Section 8 is Indian country because the question is not ripe for judicial review, we conclude that the present record is insufficient to sustain the declaratory relief sought, and that the parties’ dispute must await a concrete exercise of jurisdiction or a specific enforcement action. The inquiry whether land qualifies as Indian country is not an abstract exercise in historical classification. It turns upon the source of title, the terms of any reservation or allotment, subsequent congressional action, the character of federal superintendence, and the practical relationship between the land, the Tribe, and the United States. Those considerations may have legal significance only when joined to an actual controversy concerning the authority asserted over particular persons, conduct, or property. Here, the record shows disagreement over the status of Section 8, but it does not show that the challenged designation has yet been applied in a manner producing immediate legal consequences for the parties before us. No prosecution, taxation measure, permitting decision, exclusion order, or regulatory command is presently under review. Nor has any party identified a final agency action fixing rights or obligations with respect to Section 8. In these circumstances, a judicial declaration would risk becoming an advisory pronouncement on a question whose answer may depend upon facts not yet established and governmental positions not yet finalized. This conclusion does not diminish the importance of the issue. If Section 8 is Indian country, significant consequences may follow for criminal jurisdiction, civil regulatory authority, and the allocation of sovereign power among the Tribe, the State, and the United States. If it is not, different consequences may follow. But Article III requires more than the possibility that those consequences may arise. It requires a dispute fit for judicial resolution and hardship sufficiently immediate to justify intervention. Accordingly, we hold only that the Indian-country status of Section 8 is not ripe for adjudication on this record. The judgment is vacated insofar as it purports to resolve that issue, without prejudice to renewal in a proper case presenting concrete facts and a final assertion of authority.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: In support of the proposition that Section 17 does not constitute Indian country, petitioners point to our statements in Yazzie that although subsection 1151(a) reaches land within the exterior boundaries of a reservation, the statute presupposes a formal reservation established through federal action. Section 17, petitioners contend, never received that federal imprimatur. The parcel was set aside by state legislation and later conveyed to the Tribe in fee, without any executive order, treaty, or congressional enactment declaring it reservation land. On petitioners' reading, Yazzie forecloses treating such a parcel as Indian country under any of the three statutory subsections. The argument misreads both Yazzie and the statute it construes. Yazzie addressed whether a right-of-way running through undisputed reservation land lost its status when the Tribe relinquished its beneficial interest. We held that it did not, because the surrounding reservation retained its character regardless of the ownership of individual tracts. Nothing in that holding turned on the mode by which the reservation was first created. The passage petitioners quote describes the operation of subsection 1151(a) in the ordinary case. It does not announce a categorical rule that only reservations born of treaty or executive order qualify. Subsection 1151(a) speaks of land within the limits of any Indian reservation under the jurisdiction of the United States Government, notwithstanding the issuance of any patent. Congress chose that language to capture reservations however established, so long as federal jurisdiction attaches. The requirement is jurisdictional, not genealogical. A parcel acquired by fee and later brought under federal supervision through the trust process can satisfy the subsection even though its origin lies in state conveyance. The controlling question is whether the United States has assumed jurisdiction over the land as a reservation, not whether the reservation traces to a particular founding instrument. Petitioners' reliance on the fee character of the original conveyance also proves too much. The statute itself contemplates patented land, directing that reservation status survives the issuance of any patent. If a patent within an established reservation does not defeat Indian country status, the fee origin of Section 17 cannot supply the categorical bar petitioners seek. The relevant inquiry runs to present jurisdiction and use, not to the form of the initial transfer. We are left, then, with the factual record concerning federal action over Section 17. The Secretary accepted the parcel into trust in 1994, and the Department has since treated it as part of the Tribe's reservation for jurisdictional purposes. Those actions bring the land within subsection 1151(a) as we have construed it. Yazzie does not command otherwise, and petitioners identify no other authority that would strip the parcel of its status. The proposition petitioners advance would convert an observation about the typical reservation into a limit Congress did not impose. We decline to read Yazzie so broadly. Section 17 constitutes Indian country under subsection 1151(a).
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: We do not rest our disposition on the dependent Indian community clause. To do so would require an inquiry substantially different from, and more uncertain than, the analysis necessary here. The question whether the lands at issue constitute a dependent Indian community would require us to examine the continued vitality of the factors articulated in Watchman in light of the Supreme Court’s subsequent decision in Alaska v. Native Village of Venetie Tribal Government. Watchman employed a practical, multi-factor approach, asking whether the area was set apart for Indian use, whether it was under federal superintendence, and whether it functioned as an Indian community. Venetie, however, made clear that the statutory phrase “dependent Indian communities” contains two mandatory requirements: first, the land must have been set aside by the Federal Government for the use of Indians as Indian land; and second, the land must be under federal superintendence. That reformulation may not have eliminated every consideration previously relevant under Watchman, but it plainly narrows the inquiry and changes its center of gravity. Evidence that a community is predominantly Indian, that tribal institutions operate there, or that the residents understand the area as part of the tribal homeland may remain historically and contextually significant. Yet after Venetie, such facts cannot substitute for a federal set-aside and continuing federal superintendence. A court applying the dependent Indian community provision must therefore distinguish between indicia of community life and the jurisdictional predicates Congress incorporated into 18 U.S.C. § 1151(b), as construed by the Supreme Court. The present record would force us into precisely that difficult terrain. The parties dispute not merely the factual character of the community, but the legal significance of federal actions taken over time, including the extent to which those actions were directed to the land itself rather than to tribal members or tribal programs. They also disagree about whether federal regulation, funding, or recognition amounts to superintendence over the relevant territory, or instead reflects the ordinary government-to-government relationship that may exist even outside Indian country. Those questions are substantial. They implicate the boundary between federal protection of tribal self-government and the creation or preservation of territorial jurisdiction. Nor is it evident that Watchman can be applied mechanically after Venetie. To the extent Watchman treated the existence of a cohesive Indian community as an independent or weighty factor, Venetie counsels caution. To the extent Watchman understood federal set-aside and superintendence in a broader, functional manner, Venetie may require a more exacting showing. Resolving those tensions would demand a careful examination of precedent, history, federal land status, and the nature of governmental control. Because this case can be resolved on narrower grounds, we decline to decide whether the lands constitute a dependent Indian community. Prudence favors avoiding an unnecessary holding that would either expand or contract Indian country jurisdiction without a fully developed need to do so. Our decision therefore leaves for another case the precise effect of Venetie on the Watchman framework.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: Even under the broadest possible construction of “grounds arising,” the latest ruling in those cases, the New Mexico Court of Appeals’ summary dismissal, does not supply a new ground for relief here. That dismissal ended that appeal. It did not alter the operative facts, revise the governing statute, or announce a rule changing the legal character of the prior proceedings. A litigant cannot convert an adverse disposition into a fresh predicate for collateral attack by assigning significance to the fact of dismissal itself. The phrase “grounds arising” reaches events that come into existence after the earlier proceeding and that bear materially on the right asserted. It does not reach later judicial refusals to revisit claims already presented, or claims that could have been presented with diligence. The record in those cases shows continuity, not novelty. Those parties raised the same essential objections, challenged the same judicial acts, and sought the same practical relief. The Court of Appeals declined to disturb the result. That ruling confirms the finality of the prior adjudication. It does not reopen it. The argument also misconceives the function of a summary dismissal. Such a disposition may carry preclusive effect as a final appellate act, depending on the procedural setting. By its form alone, however, it does not create substantive law favorable to the losing party. The absence of a written merits discussion gives the movant no advantage. A court’s decision to dispose of an appeal summarily reflects its judgment that the appeal does not warrant further review under the applicable standards. It does not create an unresolved question for later litigation. Nor does fairness require a different result. The doctrine limiting successive litigation protects the opposing party and the courts from repeated attempts to relitigate the same dispute. That concern has full force here. The asserted “latest ruling” arose from the same litigation path that produced the earlier adverse decisions. Treating each appellate refusal as a new ground would defeat finality. Every denial would become the seed of another petition, and no judgment would end the controversy. The proper inquiry remains whether the asserted ground depends on facts or law that did not exist, and could not have been invoked, when the prior challenge proceeded. The New Mexico Court of Appeals’ summary dismissal fails that test. It added no new factual circumstance and announced no intervening change in law. That disposition left the parties where the prior judgments placed them. For that reason, even the most generous reading of “grounds arising” cannot bring the present claim within the permitted exception. The claim rests on repetition, and repetition is not a ground arising after judgment.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: The district court held that under United States v. Booker, the Sentencing Guidelines operate in an advisory capacity, and that its earlier treatment of the Guidelines as binding constituted procedural error requiring resentencing. We agree with the court's reading of Booker, though we part company with its application to the record before us. Booker severed the provision that made the Guidelines mandatory and left the remainder of the sentencing statute intact. A district court must still calculate the applicable Guidelines range and consult it as the starting point for its analysis. That range no longer dictates the outcome. The court retains discretion to impose a sentence outside the range so long as it grounds that decision in the factors set out in 18 U.S.C. 3553(a) and explains its reasoning on the record. The district court understood this framework. It calculated the range at 63 to 78 months, acknowledged that the range was advisory, and stated that a sentence at the low end would serve the statutory purposes. Where the court erred was in its assumption that Booker compelled a downward variance. Nothing in Booker requires a sentence below the range. The decision removed the floor and the ceiling that the mandatory scheme once imposed, but it did not create a presumption favoring leniency.
