A court that treats a variance as obligatory misunderstands the discretion Booker restored. The record confirms the error. The court remarked that, "after Booker, I am no longer required to sentence within the range, and I therefore reduce the term to 48 months." That statement reveals a mistaken premise. The reduction rested on the court's belief that Booker itself justified departure from the range, rather than on any of the 3553(a) factors applied to this defendant. The court identified no characteristic of the offense, no feature of the defendant's history, and no consideration of deterrence or public safety that supported a term below the range. The variance floated free of the statutory anchors that must support it. We do not hold that the sentence imposed was substantively unreasonable. A term of 48 months may well be justified on this record. The defect lies in the reasoning, not necessarily in the result. When a district court varies from the Guidelines, it must articulate reasons tied to the statutory factors sufficient to permit meaningful appellate review. A variance announced as the automatic consequence of Booker forecloses that review, because it substitutes a legal misconception for the individualized judgment the statute demands. The proper course is remand. On resentencing, the district court should begin with the correctly calculated range, consider the 3553(a) factors, and impose whatever sentence those factors warrant. If the court again concludes that a term of 48 months is appropriate, it may impose one, provided it explains why the statutory factors support that choice. What it may not do is treat Booker as a mandate to sentence below the range. We vacate the sentence and remand for resentencing consistent with this opinion.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Tenth Circuit

label: ai
text: Section 1. Legislative findings. 1. The Legislature finds that the denial by some employers of the right of employees to organize for common representation burdens lawful industry and impairs the flow of commerce. When employees cannot act through representatives of their own choosing, individual bargaining lacks equal force. Wages remain unsettled, and disputes over pay or working time more often interrupt production. 2. The Legislature further finds that the refusal by some employers to accept collective bargaining causes recurring labor conflict. Collective bargaining provides an orderly method for employees and employers to resolve employment disputes through written agreements that support stable operations. An employer who refuses that method leaves employees without a practical means to present common demands and increases the risk of strikes, lockouts, or related stoppages affecting commerce. 3. The policy of this Act is to protect the exercise by employees of full freedom of association and self-organization, including the designation of representatives. The Act seeks to remove sources of industrial unrest by requiring employers to meet with employee representatives and to bargain in good faith about compensation, working time, and other workplace matters. Section 2. Employee rights. 1. Employees have the right to form or join a labor organization, and they may assist such an organization. They have the right to bargain collectively through representatives they choose. They also have the right to engage in concerted activity for collective bargaining or for mutual aid related to employment. 2. No employer may interfere with the exercise of these rights. No employer may discharge, discipline, threaten, blacklist, or otherwise discriminate against an employee because the employee forms, joins, supports, or assists a labor organization, or because the employee seeks collective bargaining. Section 3. Duty to bargain. 1. An employer must recognize and bargain with the representative selected by a majority of employees in an appropriate bargaining unit. The duty to bargain requires the employer to meet at reasonable times and confer in good faith, including by making a sincere effort to reach agreement on employment matters. 2. The duty to bargain does not compel either party to agree to a proposal or make a concession. It does require each party to give the other party a fair opportunity to present proposals, to state objections, and to consider counterproposals. 3. An employer violates this Act if the employer refuses to meet with the employees’ representative, bypasses that representative to bargain directly with employees on collective matters, unilaterally changes a subject under negotiation, or uses delay to defeat the bargaining process. Section 4. Enforcement. 1. The labor board may investigate charges alleging denial of organizational rights or refusal to bargain. If the board finds a violation, it may order reinstatement, back pay, recognition, bargaining, posting of notices, and other relief needed to restore the protected process. 2. Courts shall enforce lawful orders of the board and may grant temporary relief when necessary to prevent continuing interference with employee rights or the collective bargaining procedure.

label: ai
text: It shall be an unfair labor practice for an employer, by any officer, agent, supervisor, representative, or person acting directly or indirectly in its interest, to interfere with, restrain, or coerce employees in the exercise of rights secured by this Act, including the right to self-organization, to form, join, assist, or refrain from assisting any labor organization, to bargain collectively through representatives of their own choosing, and to engage in concerted activities for the purpose of collective bargaining, mutual aid, protection, or other lawful workplace objectives. Interference, restraint, or coercion includes, but is not limited to, threatening loss of employment, reduction of hours, closure, transfer, demotion, discipline, denial of benefits, adverse scheduling, blacklisting, surveillance, or other reprisal because an employee has exercised, or is believed to have exercised, any such right. It also includes promising, granting, or withholding wages, benefits, promotions, assignments, or other terms or conditions of employment for the purpose of influencing, discouraging, or penalizing the exercise of such rights. An employer shall not question an employee concerning union membership, union sentiment, protected concerted activity, the identity or views of other employees, or the selection of representatives, where the circumstances, including the time, place, manner, authority of the questioner, or presence of threats or reprisals, tend reasonably to restrain or coerce the employee. An employer shall not maintain, enforce, or promulgate any rule, policy, agreement, handbook provision, confidentiality requirement, electronic communications restriction, no-solicitation rule, no-distribution rule, or social media directive that has the purpose or reasonably foreseeable effect of preventing or chilling employees from discussing wages, hours, working conditions, labor organizations, grievances, or other matters protected by this Act, except to the extent narrowly tailored to a substantial and legitimate business need and not applied discriminatorily. An employer shall not spy upon, photograph, record, monitor, or create the impression of surveillance of employees’ protected activities, whether on employer premises, through electronic systems, or elsewhere, when such conduct would tend to deter a reasonable employee from engaging in protected activity. No employer shall solicit grievances, conduct polls, convene captive-audience meetings, or communicate statements of preference in a manner accompanied by threats, promises, misrepresentations of material fact, or conduct reasonably tending to deprive employees of free choice. The expression of views, argument, or opinion shall not constitute an unfair labor practice under this subsection if it contains no threat of reprisal or force, no promise of benefit, and no coercive conduct. The burden shall be upon the employer to show that a challenged communication or policy falls within this protection. Remedies may include cease-and-desist orders, rescission of unlawful rules, notice posting, reinstatement of privileges, make-whole relief, and such other affirmative action as will effectuate the purposes of this Act.

label: ai
text: In making a finding under this section, the Board shall consider, among other relevant factors, the practices and customs of labor organizations in the particular industry. The Board shall determine whether the challenged conduct conforms to established patterns of representation, dues collection, membership admission, and internal governance that prevail among comparable organizations operating in the same trade or sector. The Board shall receive evidence of industry practice from any party and may take official notice of practices documented in prior proceedings before it. Such evidence may include collective bargaining agreements, constitutions and bylaws of labor organizations, historical records of grievance handling, and testimony from persons with direct knowledge of the trade. A party asserting that a particular practice is customary bears the burden of establishing its prevalence and duration within the relevant industry. In weighing the practices and customs of an industry, the Board shall give greater consideration to practices that are longstanding, widely adopted, and applied without material variation across employers and employee groups. A practice confined to a single organization or adopted only recently carries diminished weight. The Board shall not treat conformity to industry custom as dispositive where the custom itself conflicts with a right secured under this chapter. The Board shall define the relevant industry with reference to the nature of the work performed, the market in which employers compete, and the character of the labor supply on which those employers draw. Where an organization represents workers across more than one industry, the Board shall consider the practices of each industry as they bear on the conduct at issue. The Board may consult classifications used by other agencies of the United States when defining industry boundaries, but such classifications do not bind the Board. Evidence of industry practice serves to inform the Board's judgment on whether the conduct in question was reasonable, whether it served a legitimate representational purpose, and whether it imposed a burden disproportionate to that purpose. The Board shall explain in its findings how the practices and customs it considered affected its conclusion. A finding that fails to address probative evidence of industry practice offered by a party is subject to remand on review. Nothing in this section requires the Board to defer to industry practice that operates to exclude workers from representation on grounds prohibited by law. Where a custom, however established, produces a result the Board determines to be unlawful, the Board shall so find and shall order relief without regard to the prevalence of the custom. The Board retains authority to determine that a practice, though common, does not reflect a considered judgment of the organizations that follow it and therefore merits reduced weight. The considerations set out in this section apply to all proceedings in which the Board must assess the conduct of a labor organization against the standards of this chapter.

label: ai
text: For purposes of this chapter, a work is “created” when the work is fixed in a copy or phonorecord for the first time. Fixation occurs when the author, or a person acting with the author’s authority, embodies the work in a material object. The embodiment must permit the work to be perceived, reproduced, or communicated for more than a transitory period, either directly or with the aid of a machine or device. A fixed embodiment need not be final, complete, public, or commercially exploited. If a work is prepared over a period of time, each portion that becomes fixed at any time constitutes the created work as of that time. The date of creation for that portion is the date on which fixation first occurs. Completion of later portions does not alter the date of creation of an earlier fixed portion, except to the extent that a later portion contains new authorship fixed for the first time. If a work reaches completion in successive versions, each version constitutes a separate created work to the extent that the version contains original authorship not fixed in an earlier version. A revision, continuation, adaptation, arrangement, or other modified form receives its own date of creation only for the authorship first fixed in that form. Material carried forward from an earlier version retains the creation date that applied when that material first became fixed. A work may be created even if no copy or phonorecord has been distributed, published, registered, transferred, or deposited. Creation depends on fixation, not on public availability, commercial use, notice, or the author’s intent to complete the work. An unfinished draft, sketch, recording, notation, digital file, or other embodiment may constitute a created work if it satisfies the requirements of fixation. A performance, recitation, transmission, or display that has not been fixed in a copy or phonorecord does not create a work under this section. If the performance, recitation, transmission, or display is fixed at the same time that it occurs, the work is created at the time of that fixation. If a transmission produces a fixed copy or phonorecord under the author’s authority, the work is created when that fixed embodiment first exists. A party asserting a date of creation bears the burden of proving fixation on that date by a preponderance of the evidence. A court or other deciding authority may consider drafts, files, recordings, metadata, deposit materials, testimony, business records, and other competent evidence bearing on the existence and timing of fixation. Absence of publication or registration does not defeat proof of creation, but the evidence must identify the authorship claimed and show that the claimed authorship had become fixed.

label: ai
text: An alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States shall not be regarded as seeking an admission into the United States for purposes of the immigration laws unless that alien: (i) has abandoned or relinquished that status; (ii) has been absent from the United States for a continuous period in excess of 180 days; (iii) has engaged in illegal activity after departing the United States; (iv) has departed from the United States while under legal process seeking removal of the alien from the United States, including removal proceedings under this chapter and extradition proceedings; (v) has committed an offense identified in section 1182(a)(2) of this title, unless since that offense the alien has been granted relief under section 1182(h) or 1229b(a) of this title; or (vi) is attempting to enter at a time or place other than as designated by immigration officers or has not been admitted to the United States after inspection and authorization by an immigration officer. An alien described in this subparagraph shall be inspected in accordance with the procedures otherwise applicable to arriving aliens under this section.

label: ai
text: Sec. 101. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, to employ a manipulative or deceptive method through the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, the mails, or both. This prohibition applies in connection with the offer, purchase, or sale of any security, commodity, digital asset offered as an investment, or other financial interest under federal jurisdiction. A person violates this section if the person, with intent to deceive or with reckless disregard for the truth, does any of the following: (1) makes a material statement that is false when made; (2) omits a material fact necessary to make a statement, in the circumstances in which the person made it, not misleading; (3) creates a false appearance of active trading, market depth, ownership, demand, price support, or liquidity; (4) enters, places, cancels, routes, or coordinates an order or transaction for the purpose of misleading another person about price, value, supply, demand, or execution interest; (5) obtains money or property through a statement, omission, practice, or course of conduct that operates as a deception on a purchaser, seller, counterparty, or investor; or (6) causes another person to engage in conduct prohibited by this section while knowing the facts that make the conduct unlawful. A fact is material if a reasonable investor would consider it important in deciding whether to buy, sell, hold, lend against, or value the financial interest. Proof of reliance by a particular person is not required in an action brought by the United States or by the Commission under this section. Interstate commerce includes the internet, telephone networks, electronic messaging, payment systems, wire services, national securities exchanges, alternative trading systems, broker networks, bank channels, and any other facility that crosses a state line or affects commerce among the States. The Securities and Market Integrity Commission may adopt rules that define prohibited methods, prescribe disclosure duties, require preservation of transaction records, and establish controls reasonably designed to prevent violations of this section. By rule or order, the Commission may exempt a class of persons, transactions, or instruments if the exemption serves investor protection and does not impair market integrity. United States district courts have jurisdiction over actions to enforce this section. In a civil action, the court may order an injunction, disgorgement, restitution, a civil penalty, officer or director bars, trading restrictions, and any other equitable remedy needed to prevent future violations. A person who willfully violates this section commits an offense and, on conviction, shall be fined under title 18, imprisoned for not more than 20 years, or both.

label: ai
text: Subject to sections 107 through 122, and except as otherwise provided in this title, the owner of copyright in a work has the exclusive right to do, and to authorize another person to do, any of the following acts: to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies or phonorecords; to prepare derivative works based upon the copyrighted work; to distribute copies or phonorecords of the work to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership, or by rental, lease, or lending; in the case of literary, musical, dramatic, and choreographic works, pantomimes, motion pictures, and other audiovisual works, to perform the work publicly; in the case of literary, musical, dramatic, and choreographic works, pantomimes, and pictorial, graphic, or sculptural works, including individual images of a motion picture or other audiovisual work, to display the work publicly; and, in the case of sound recordings, to perform the work publicly by means of a digital audio transmission.

text: This Sixth Supplemental Agreement is dated 15 April 2019. The parties to it are MGM China Holdings Limited, a company incorporated in the Cayman Islands with limited liability, and MGM Grand Paradise, S.A., a company incorporated in Macau. The parties enter into this agreement to supplement existing financing arrangements relating to the MGM Macau business and the MGM Cotai business. They also record agreed changes to the obligations of MGM Grand Paradise, S.A. as borrower and as operating company. With effect from the Effective Time, each reference in the Finance Documents to the Principal Agreement will mean the Principal Agreement as amended by this Sixth Supplemental Agreement. Except as changed by this agreement, the Principal Agreement continues to bind each party. No waiver arises from this agreement unless it appears in an express written clause. A failure or delay in enforcing any provision of the Principal Agreement or of this agreement does not prevent later enforcement. MGM Grand Paradise, S.A. confirms that its payment obligations under the Principal Agreement remain valid and enforceable. MGM China Holdings Limited confirms that each support obligation it has given in connection with the Principal Agreement remains in place. Each such obligation continues to secure the liabilities of MGM Grand Paradise, S.A. as changed by this agreement. Each party confirms that it has taken all corporate action required to approve entry into this agreement, perform its obligations, and authorise the person signing this agreement on its behalf. The amendments in this agreement become effective when MGM China Holdings Limited receives evidence, in form and substance satisfactory to it, that MGM Grand Paradise, S.A. has obtained all internal approvals required for this agreement. MGM China Holdings Limited must also receive evidence, satisfactory to it, that no default is continuing under the Principal Agreement. MGM China Holdings Limited may waive any condition by written notice to MGM Grand Paradise, S.A. The date on which the last condition is satisfied or waived is the Effective Time. From the Effective Time, MGM Grand Paradise, S.A. must apply all proceeds, accounts, and operating cash subject to the Principal Agreement in accordance with the amended payment waterfall set out in Schedule 1. It must not create security over any charged asset, incur financial indebtedness outside the permitted limits, or dispose of a material asset except as allowed by the Principal Agreement. MGM China Holdings Limited may request reasonable information to confirm compliance. MGM Grand Paradise, S.A. must provide that information within ten business days after request. Each party must pay its own costs arising from the negotiation and signing of this agreement. However, MGM Grand Paradise, S.A. must pay any filing, registration, or notarisation cost required in Macau in connection with the amendments. This agreement may be signed in counterparts. Each counterpart, when signed, forms part of the same instrument. This agreement and any non-contractual obligation arising from it are governed by Hong Kong law. The courts of Hong Kong have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute arising from this agreement.

text: This Amended and Restated Credit Agreement, dated as of June 17, 2019, is entered into among LONESTAR RESOURCES AMERICA INC., a Delaware corporation (the "Borrower"), the guarantors party hereto, the lenders from time to time party hereto (collectively, the "Lenders"), and Citibank, N.A., as administrative agent for the Lenders (in such capacity, the "Administrative Agent"). This Agreement amends, restates, and supersedes in its entirety that certain Credit Agreement dated as of May 24, 2016, as previously amended (the "Existing Credit Agreement"). The Borrower has requested that the Lenders extend credit under a senior secured reserve-based revolving facility. The Lenders are willing to do so on the terms set forth in this Agreement. In consideration of the mutual covenants contained herein, the parties agree as follows. The initial Borrowing Base as of the date hereof is $290,000,000, subject to redetermination in accordance with Section 2.07. The Borrowing Base reflects the loan value assigned by the Lenders to the proved oil and gas properties of the Borrower and its Restricted Subsidiaries evaluated in the most recent Reserve Report delivered to the Administrative Agent. The Borrower acknowledges that the Borrowing Base is subject to scheduled semi-annual redeterminations on or about April 1 and October 1 of each year, and to interim redeterminations as permitted under this Agreement. Each Loan made on or after the date hereof shall bear interest at a rate per annum equal to, at the Borrower's election, the Alternate Base Rate plus the Applicable Margin or the Adjusted LIBO Rate plus the Applicable Margin. The Applicable Margin shall be determined by reference to the Borrowing Base Utilization Percentage then in effect, as set forth in the pricing grid attached as Annex I. Interest on each Eurodollar Loan is payable on the last day of each Interest Period applicable thereto, and interest on each ABR Loan is payable on the last day of each calendar quarter. The obligations of the Borrower under this Agreement are secured by first-priority liens on not less than 85% of the total value of the proved oil and gas properties of the Borrower and the Guarantors, granted under the Security Instruments described in Section 8.14. Each Guarantor jointly and severally guarantees the prompt payment of all Secured Obligations when due, whether at stated maturity, by acceleration, or otherwise. The representations and warranties of the Borrower set forth in Article VII are true and correct in all material respects as of the date hereof, except to the extent they relate to an earlier date. The Borrower confirms that no Default or Event of Default has occurred and is continuing as of the date of this Agreement. This Agreement is governed by the laws of the State of New York. The parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts sitting in the Borough of Manhattan, City of New York, for any action arising out of this Agreement, and each party waives any right to trial by jury.

label: ai
text: This Agreement is made as of May 21, 2015, among TWIN RIVER MANAGEMENT GROUP, INC., a Delaware corporation, the affiliates that sign counterparts to this Agreement, and the owners of equity interests listed on the signature pages. The parties enter into this Agreement to establish the authority of TWIN RIVER MANAGEMENT GROUP, INC. to provide management and operational services for the covered businesses, with related administrative and financial support, and to allocate the corresponding fees, duties, and decision rights. TWIN RIVER MANAGEMENT GROUP, INC. shall manage the ordinary course operations of the covered businesses in a manner consistent with approved budgets and the written policies adopted by the applicable governing body, and in compliance with applicable law, including gaming regulatory requirements. Its authority includes personnel supervision and financial reporting, negotiation of vendor arrangements, coordination of compliance matters, and implementation of capital projects approved under this Agreement. TWIN RIVER MANAGEMENT GROUP, INC. may not sell a material asset, incur debt outside an approved budget, amend an organizational document, settle litigation above $250,000, or enter into a transaction with an affiliate unless the required approval has been obtained. Each covered business shall pay TWIN RIVER MANAGEMENT GROUP, INC. a monthly management fee equal to the amount set forth in the annual budget approved for that business. The fee accrues on the first day of each month and is payable within ten business days after receipt of an invoice. Reimbursable expenses must relate to services provided under this Agreement and must be supported by reasonable documentation. No party may charge a markup on shared expenses unless the governing body has approved that charge in writing. TWIN RIVER MANAGEMENT GROUP, INC. shall keep complete books covering all services, fees, allocations, and reimbursements under this Agreement. Each party may inspect the related records during normal business hours after reasonable notice. Financial records must be retained for at least seven years, or longer if a regulator requires a longer period. Each party shall protect confidential business information received under this Agreement and may use that information only to perform or enforce this Agreement, comply with law, or respond to a lawful request from a regulator or court. A party receiving a request for confidential information shall give prompt notice to the affected party when law permits notice. This Agreement begins on May 21, 2015, and continues until terminated under this section. A party may terminate this Agreement after a material breach if the breaching party fails to cure the breach within thirty days after written notice. TWIN RIVER MANAGEMENT GROUP, INC. may resign as manager on ninety days’ written notice, subject to any required regulatory approval. Termination does not affect accrued payment obligations, confidentiality duties, record retention duties, or claims arising from conduct before the effective termination date. Delaware law governs this Agreement. Each party consents to exclusive jurisdiction in the state and federal courts located in Delaware for any dispute arising from this Agreement.

label: ai
text: Subject to Section 6 hereof and the satisfaction or waiver of each of the conditions precedent set forth herein and in the Credit Agreement, the Third Incremental Term Loan Lender hereby agrees that it will provide the Third Incremental Term Loan Commitments and, on the Third Incremental Effective Date, make Third Incremental Term Loans to the Borrower in an aggregate principal amount not to exceed the amount of such Third Incremental Term Loan Commitments set forth opposite such Lender’s name on the applicable commitment schedule delivered to the Administrative Agent. The Borrower hereby requests, and the Third Incremental Term Loan Lender hereby commits, to fund such Third Incremental Term Loans in immediately available funds to the account designated by the Administrative Agent, for further credit to the Borrower or as otherwise directed in accordance with the funds flow memorandum approved by the Administrative Agent. Upon the making of the Third Incremental Term Loans, such loans shall constitute “Loans,” “Term Loans,” and “Incremental Term Loans” under the Credit Agreement for all purposes thereof and shall be entitled to the benefits of the Loan Documents on a pari passu basis with the existing Term Loans, except as expressly provided herein. The Third Incremental Term Loan Commitments shall terminate automatically and without further action upon the earlier of the funding of the Third Incremental Term Loans on the Third Incremental Effective Date and the date on which this Agreement is terminated in accordance with its terms. No Third Incremental Term Loan Lender shall have any obligation to make any Third Incremental Term Loan unless all conditions applicable to such funding have been satisfied or waived in accordance with the terms hereof. The Borrower acknowledges and agrees that the Third Incremental Term Loans shall have the interest rate, amortization schedule, maturity date, fees, prepayment provisions, and other economic terms set forth in this Agreement and, to the extent not inconsistent herewith, the Credit Agreement. From and after the Third Incremental Effective Date, each reference in the Credit Agreement or any other Loan Document to the “Credit Agreement,” “thereunder,” “thereof,” or words of like import shall be deemed to refer to the Credit Agreement as modified hereby, and each reference to a “Lender” shall include the Third Incremental Term Loan Lender in its capacity as holder of the Third Incremental Term Loans. The Third Incremental Term Loan Lender, by its execution hereof, confirms that it has received and reviewed such documents and information as it has deemed appropriate to make its own credit decision and agrees to be bound by the provisions of the Credit Agreement applicable to a Lender. The Borrower shall use the proceeds of the Third Incremental Term Loans solely for the purposes permitted under the Credit Agreement and this Agreement, including payment of fees, costs, and expenses incurred in connection with the transactions contemplated hereby.

text: This Note Purchase Agreement and Security Agreement forms an exhibit to the Purchase Agreement, as amended. It states the agreement by and among Bricktown Brewery Restaurants LLC, as Borrower, the purchasers named in the signature pages, and each Subsidiary that signs this Agreement. The Borrower agrees to issue and sell to each Purchaser the promissory notes described in the schedule of purchased notes. Each Purchaser agrees to purchase those notes from the Borrower. Each note will accrue interest and will mature and be payable as stated in its form. The Borrower shall use the purchase price only for restaurant operations, permitted capital expenditures, approved transaction expenses, and other purposes that the Purchasers approve in writing. Each Subsidiary joins this Agreement as both a grantor and a guarantor of the Borrower’s obligations under the notes and the related documents. The Borrower and each Subsidiary grant to the Purchasers a first priority security interest in all Collateral to secure payment and performance of the secured obligations. Collateral includes accounts, inventory, equipment, fixtures, deposit accounts, investment property, general intangibles, commercial tort claims identified in writing, books, records, and proceeds of the foregoing. Each grantor shall execute and deliver financing statements, account control agreements, landlord access agreements, and any other instruments that the Purchasers reasonably require to perfect or protect the security interest. The Borrower represents that it is a limited liability company in good standing. The Borrower also represents that it owns or leases the restaurant assets used in its business and has authority to enter into this Agreement. Each Subsidiary makes the same representations as to itself. No grantor shall incur additional debt, create a competing lien on the Collateral, sell a material portion of its assets outside the ordinary course, or make distributions to equity holders, except as the Purchasers permit in writing or as this Agreement expressly allows. An Event of Default occurs if the Borrower fails to pay any amount when due, any grantor breaches a material covenant, any representation proves false in a material respect, a bankruptcy event occurs, or any material license needed for the operation of the restaurants terminates and remains unrestored after the applicable cure period. After an Event of Default, the Purchasers may accelerate the notes, enforce their security interest, collect Collateral proceeds, and exercise all remedies available under Article 9 of the Uniform Commercial Code. The remedies are cumulative. A waiver of one default does not waive any later default.

label: ai
text: This Seventh Loan Modification Agreement ("Agreement") is made this 28th day of June, 2018 to be effective as of June 1, 2018 and is entered into by and among Meridian Community Bank, a national banking association ("Lender"), Cornerstone Development Partners, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company ("Borrower"), and the guarantors identified on the signature pages hereto ("Guarantors"). Lender and Borrower previously entered into a Loan Agreement dated March 15, 2011, as amended by six prior loan modification agreements (collectively, the "Loan Agreement"), evidencing a term loan in the original principal amount of $8,500,000. The parties now wish to further modify the terms of the Loan Agreement as set forth below. 1. Definitions. Capitalized terms used but not defined in this Agreement have the meanings given to them in the Loan Agreement. 2. Outstanding Balance. As of the effective date, the outstanding principal balance under the Loan Agreement is $6,240,318.44, together with accrued and unpaid interest of $31,204.09. Borrower acknowledges and confirms this balance without offset, defense, or counterclaim of any kind. 3. Maturity Date. The Maturity Date is extended from July 1, 2018 to July 1, 2021. All references to the Maturity Date in the Loan Agreement and the other Loan Documents are amended accordingly. 4. Interest Rate. Commencing on the effective date, interest accrues on the outstanding principal balance at a fixed rate of 5.75% per annum. Section 2.3 of the Loan Agreement is deleted and replaced with the rate stated in this Section. 5. Payment Terms. Borrower shall make consecutive monthly payments of principal and interest in the amount of $41,782.16, due on the first day of each calendar month, beginning July 1, 2018. The entire remaining balance of principal and accrued interest is due and payable on the Maturity Date. 6. Modification Fee. In consideration of the accommodations granted under this Agreement, Borrower shall pay Lender a modification fee of $18,720.00, due upon execution of this Agreement. 7. Reaffirmation of Guaranty. Each Guarantor reaffirms its obligations under the Guaranty executed in connection with the Loan Agreement and agrees that the Guaranty remains in full force and effect and applies to the Loan Agreement as modified by this Agreement. 8. No Novation. This Agreement modifies the Loan Agreement. It does not constitute a novation, release, or discharge of the indebtedness or the Loan Documents. Except as expressly modified here, the Loan Agreement and the other Loan Documents remain unchanged and continue in full force and effect. 9. Governing Law. This Agreement is governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles. 10. Counterparts. This Agreement may be executed in counterparts, each of which is deemed an original and all of which together constitute one instrument. The parties have executed this Agreement as of the date first written above.

text: This agreement is dated 24 October 2019. It is entered into by Sapphire Aviation Finance I (UK) Limited as existing lessor, Wilmington Trust SP Services (Dublin) Limited, not in its individual capacity, but solely as security trustee, and each other person that signs this agreement as a party. The Existing Lessor confirms that it has full power to enter into this agreement, to perform its obligations under it, and to transfer the interests described in it in the manner contemplated by the transaction documents. By this agreement, the Existing Lessor assigns to the Security Trustee, for the benefit of the secured parties, all of its right, title, and interest in the assigned lease rights. The assignment also covers the related rent, the maintenance reserves, the security deposit, and each related claim against the lessee that arises on or after the effective time. No obligation of the Existing Lessor that accrued before the effective time is released by the assignment unless the relevant beneficiary gives a written release. Acting solely in its capacity as security trustee, the Security Trustee accepts the assignment and incurs no personal liability for any covenant, representation, or payment obligation of any other party. Each party must sign and deliver any document, notice, acknowledgement, or instruction that the Security Trustee reasonably requests to give effect to this agreement. The Existing Lessor must notify the lessee of the assignment in the form approved by the Security Trustee. That notice must direct the lessee to make each payment to the account specified in the notice. If the Existing Lessor receives any assigned amount after the effective time, it must hold that amount for the Security Trustee and transfer it to the specified account within two business days after receipt. As at the date of this agreement, the Existing Lessor represents that no prior assignment, security interest, or transfer made by it conflicts with the assignment granted under this agreement. It also represents that, to its knowledge, no default by the lessee has occurred and remains continuing under the lease, except as disclosed in writing to the Security Trustee before signing. This agreement binds each party and its permitted successors. A party may not assign its obligations under this agreement without the prior written consent of the Security Trustee, except for an assignment permitted under the finance documents. English law governs this agreement and any non-contractual obligation arising from it. The courts of England have jurisdiction, and each party accepts that jurisdiction for any dispute connected with this agreement.

text: Effective as of June 26, 2019, the parties acknowledge and agree that the term “Properties,” as used in this Agreement and in each certificate, schedule, exhibit, notice, consent, assignment, estoppel, closing statement, and ancillary instrument executed or delivered in connection herewith, shall mean and include, collectively, the following retail shopping center assets located in the City of Alexandria, Commonwealth of Virginia: (i) Bradlee Shopping Center, commonly known as 3600 King Street, Alexandria, Virginia 22302, together with all land, buildings, structures, improvements, parking areas, access drives, fixtures, appurtenances, easements, rights, privileges, licenses, permits, and other real property interests used or held for use in connection therewith; and (ii) Shoppes at Foxchase, commonly known as 4500-4600 Duke Street, Alexandria, Virginia 22304, together with all land, buildings, structures, improvements, parking areas, access drives, fixtures, appurtenances, easements, rights, privileges, licenses, permits, and other real property interests used or held for use in connection therewith. Each Property shall be deemed to include, without limitation, all landlord interests under leases, licenses, occupancy agreements, amendments, guarantees, security deposits, rent claims, tenant reimbursements, common area maintenance charges, tax and insurance pass-throughs, service contracts, warranties, plans, specifications, surveys, environmental reports, governmental approvals, utility rights, signage rights, trade names to the extent assignable, and tangible personal property located upon or exclusively serving such Property. No street address, tax parcel designation, legal description summary, site name, or other identifying reference set forth herein shall be construed to limit the scope of the conveyance, assignment, encumbrance, representation, covenant, indemnity, or other provision applicable to the Properties, it being the intent of the parties that all interests owned, claimed, or controlled by the applicable owner and relating to the operation, maintenance, use, leasing, management, repair, replacement, or development of the foregoing shopping centers be included except to the extent expressly excluded in a written schedule executed by all parties. The parties further agree that any reference to matters affecting the Properties shall include matters affecting either or both of Bradlee Shopping Center and Shoppes at Foxchase, whether arising before, on, or after June 26, 2019, including real estate taxes and assessments, zoning and land use restrictions, recorded covenants and easements, tenant rights, reciprocal easement obligations, maintenance obligations, insurance requirements, casualty and condemnation matters, environmental conditions, governmental notices, violations, and claims of third parties. All representations, warranties, covenants, closing obligations, post-closing obligations, indemnities, survival periods, and remedies concerning the Properties shall apply separately to each Property and jointly to the Properties as the context requires. Delivery of documents, notices, approvals, or due diligence materials with respect to one Property shall not be deemed delivery with respect to the other unless the applicable communication expressly so states or the materials are by their nature applicable to both Properties.

text: The obligations described in this Section become operative during March 2028 and govern the parties' conduct for that calendar month. On or before March 5, 2028, the Supplier shall deliver to the Purchaser a written production schedule covering the full month. The schedule must identify each shipment date, the quantity assigned to that date, and the destination facility. The Purchaser shall confirm the schedule within three business days of receipt. If the Purchaser does not respond within that period, the schedule takes effect as submitted. The Supplier shall complete no fewer than four shipments during March 2028. The first shipment must depart on or before March 8, 2028, and the final shipment must depart on or before March 28, 2028. Each shipment shall contain at least twelve thousand units unless the parties agree in writing to a smaller quantity for a specific date. Payment for goods delivered in March 2028 falls due on April 15, 2028. The Purchaser shall remit the full invoiced amount by wire transfer to the account designated in Exhibit C. Interest accrues on any unpaid balance at the rate of one percent per month, calculated from April 16, 2028, until the Purchaser pays in full. If a force majeure event prevents performance during March 2028, the affected party shall give notice within forty-eight hours of the event's onset. The notice must describe the event, estimate its duration, and state which obligations the party cannot perform. Performance suspended by force majeure resumes when the event ends. No suspension extends beyond March 31, 2028, without the written consent of both parties. The Purchaser may inspect any shipment on arrival. The Purchaser shall notify the Supplier of any nonconforming units within five business days of delivery. Units the Purchaser does not reject within that period are accepted. The Supplier shall replace rejected units within ten business days of receiving the rejection notice, and the replacement units count against the monthly minimum only if they arrive on or before March 31, 2028. Either party may terminate this Section for the March 2028 period if the other party fails to cure a material breach within seven days after written notice. Termination under this paragraph does not relieve either party of obligations that accrued before the termination date. Amounts owed for goods delivered before termination remain payable under the schedule stated above. All notices required for March 2028 performance shall be sent to the addresses listed in Section 14 and are effective on the date of actual receipt. A notice delivered after 5:00 p.m. local time at the recipient's address is treated as received on the next business day. The rights and duties set out in this Section apply only to March 2028. They do not modify the parties' obligations for any other period, and they expire on April 30, 2028, except for payment and interest obligations that remain outstanding on that date.

text: This First Amended and Restated Master Lease Agreement (this “Agreement”), dated as of July 30, 2018, is made by and between Wells Fargo Equipment Finance, Inc., as lessor, together with its successors and assigns (“Lessor”), and the lessee identified on the signature pages hereto (“Lessee”), and amends, restates, supersedes, and replaces in its entirety the prior master lease agreement between the parties, as amended or supplemented before the date hereof. From and after the effective date of this Agreement, all schedules, riders, certificates of acceptance, purchase orders, amendments, and other instruments executed under or in connection with the prior agreement shall, unless expressly provided otherwise therein, be deemed issued under and governed by this Agreement. Subject to the terms and conditions set forth herein, Lessor may from time to time lease to Lessee, and Lessee may from time to time lease from Lessor, certain items of equipment, together with all accessions, attachments, replacements, substitutions, parts, additions, manuals, records, and related software or embedded technology, as more particularly described in one or more lease schedules executed by the parties. Each schedule shall constitute a separate, distinct, and independent lease of the equipment described therein, but all schedules shall be governed by and construed in accordance with this Agreement. In the event of any conflict between the terms of this Agreement and the terms of a schedule, the schedule shall control solely with respect to the equipment and transaction described therein. Lessee acknowledges and agrees that Lessor is financing the acquisition or use of the equipment at Lessee’s request and is not the manufacturer, supplier, vendor, installer, or servicer of the equipment. Lessee has selected each item of equipment and each supplier based upon its own judgment and has not relied upon any representation or warranty of Lessor concerning the equipment’s condition, merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, design, capacity, durability, compliance, or suitability. To the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, each lease shall be a net lease, and Lessee’s obligation to pay rent and all other amounts due under this Agreement and each schedule shall be absolute, unconditional, and not subject to abatement, reduction, setoff, counterclaim, recoupment, defense, or deferment for any reason. Lessee shall, at its sole cost and expense, receive, inspect, install, use, operate, maintain, repair, and preserve the equipment in good order, condition, and working repair, ordinary wear and tear excepted, and in compliance with all applicable laws, regulations, insurance requirements, manufacturer specifications, and the terms of this Agreement. Lessee shall bear all risk of loss, theft, damage, destruction, seizure, or condemnation of the equipment from the date specified in the applicable schedule or acceptance certificate until the equipment is returned to Lessor or purchased by Lessee in accordance with the applicable schedule. No such event shall relieve Lessee of its payment or performance obligations hereunder.

text: This Contract becomes effective on the date on which both Parties have completed signature and seal by their respective legal representatives or authorized representatives. Each Party shall cause its legal representative or authorized representative to sign in the signature block designated for that Party and shall affix its official seal or contract seal in the place designated for seal. A signature without the required seal, or a seal without the required signature, does not satisfy the condition for effectiveness unless the other Party confirms acceptance in a written instrument signed and sealed in the same manner. If the Parties sign and seal this Contract on different dates, the effective date shall be the later of the two dates. The date written next to each signature shall serve as evidence of the time of signing. If a date is omitted, the date on which the last Party completes both signature and seal shall control, as shown by delivery records, signing records, or other reliable written evidence. Neither Party may assert an earlier effective date based solely on negotiations, drafts, purchase orders, emails, meeting minutes, or partial performance. Each person signing this Contract on behalf of a Party represents that the person is that Party's legal representative or has received authorization sufficient to bind that Party to this Contract. Upon request, a Party shall provide the other Party with reasonable evidence of such authority, including a power of attorney, board approval, internal authorization document, or comparable written confirmation. Failure to provide such evidence after a reasonable request entitles the requesting Party to suspend performance until the evidence is provided, without liability for delay during the suspension period. Before the effective date, this Contract creates no obligation to perform the main commercial duties stated in it, except for obligations that by their nature govern formation, authority, confidentiality, costs of preparation, dispute resolution for formation issues, and protection of information exchanged during negotiation. Any act taken before the effective date shall be treated as preparatory unless the Parties expressly state in a signed and sealed writing that the act constitutes performance under this Contract. The Parties may sign and seal this Contract in separate counterparts. Each counterpart that bears the signature and seal of one Party shall form part of the same Contract once the other Party has signed and sealed its counterpart. Copies transmitted by courier record, scanned copy, or an agreed electronic signing system may evidence execution, provided that each Party shall deliver the original signed and sealed counterpart if the other Party reasonably requests it for filing, registration, audit, or enforcement purposes. After the effective date, this Contract binds both Parties according to its stated provisions. No amendment, waiver, renewal, or termination shall take effect unless made in a written instrument signed and sealed by the legal representatives or authorized representatives of both Parties, unless this Contract expressly permits another method.

text: This Amendment No. 3 (this "Amendment") is entered into as of March 14, 2024, by and among Meridian Holdings LLC, a Delaware limited liability company (the "Borrower"), the lenders party to the Credit Agreement (the "Lenders"), and Coastal Trust Bank, N.A., as administrative agent (the "Agent"). This Amendment modifies that certain Credit Agreement dated June 1, 2021, as previously amended by Amendment No. 1 dated October 5, 2022, and Amendment No. 2 dated April 30, 2023 (as so amended, the "Credit Agreement"). Capitalized terms used but not defined in this Amendment have the meanings given to them in the Credit Agreement. The parties agree as follows. Section 1.01 of the Credit Agreement is amended by deleting the definition of "Applicable Margin" in its entirety and replacing it with the definition set forth in Exhibit A attached to this Amendment. Section 2.03(b) is amended by replacing the figure "$50,000,000" with "$75,000,000." The revised Commitment schedule appears in Exhibit B. Section 6.11 (Financial Covenants) is amended to require the Borrower to maintain a Consolidated Leverage Ratio not exceeding 3.50 to 1.00 as of the last day of each fiscal quarter ending on or after March 31, 2024. The prior threshold of 4.00 to 1.00 no longer applies to any test period beginning on that date. The effectiveness of this Amendment is subject to the following conditions. The Agent must receive counterparts of this Amendment signed by the Borrower and the Required Lenders. The Borrower must deliver a certificate of a Responsible Officer confirming that the representations in Article 5 remain true in all material respects and that no Default exists. The Borrower must pay all fees and expenses due under Section 9.03, including the reasonable fees of the Agent's counsel invoiced on or before the closing date. The Borrower represents that it has full power to execute this Amendment and that this Amendment constitutes its legal, valid, and binding obligation enforceable in accordance with its terms, subject to applicable bankruptcy and equitable principles. Except as expressly modified by this Amendment, the Credit Agreement remains in full force and effect. This Amendment does not waive any right or remedy of the Agent or the Lenders under the Credit Agreement, and it does not establish a course of dealing. This Amendment is a Loan Document. It is governed by the laws of the State of New York. This Amendment may be executed in counterparts, each of which constitutes an original and all of which together constitute one instrument.

text: This Amendment No. 2 amends the Services Agreement dated March 1, 2023, between Meridian Data Group, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company, and Northstar Retail Systems, Inc., a New York corporation. The parties agree that this amendment becomes effective on January 1, 2026. Capitalized terms used in this amendment have the meanings given to them in the agreement, unless this amendment gives a different meaning. The parties amend Section 2 of the agreement to add the following services. Meridian will provide monthly data reconciliation, exception reporting, and user access reviews for Northstar’s production environment. Each monthly report will be delivered no later than the tenth business day after the end of the applicable month. Each report must identify open exceptions, assigned owners, aging, and recommended corrective steps. Northstar will review each report within five business days after receipt and will notify Meridian of any disputed item with reasonable detail. The parties amend Section 4 of the agreement to revise the fees. Beginning with the January 2026 invoice, Northstar will pay Meridian a fixed monthly service fee of $38,500. Meridian may also charge approved pass-through expenses if Northstar approves the expense in writing before Meridian incurs it. Invoices will be issued monthly in arrears. Northstar must pay each undisputed invoice within thirty days after receipt. If Northstar disputes an invoice, Northstar must pay the undisputed portion on time and provide a written description of the disputed amount. The parties amend Section 6 of the agreement to include additional security obligations. Meridian must maintain administrative, technical, and physical safeguards designed to protect Northstar data against unauthorized access. Access to Northstar data must be restricted to personnel who need that access to perform the services. Meridian must notify Northstar within forty-eight hours after confirming unauthorized access to Northstar data. The notice must describe the known facts, affected systems, categories of data involved, and steps taken to contain the incident. The parties amend the agreement to extend the term through December 31, 2027. Either party may elect not to renew the agreement by giving written notice at least ninety days before the end of the then-current term. Termination will not affect payment obligations that accrued before the termination date or obligations that, by their nature, continue after termination. If this amendment conflicts with the agreement, this amendment controls to the extent of the conflict. Except as changed by this amendment, the agreement remains in effect. No course of performance, purchase order, invoice term, or online term changes this amendment unless both parties sign a written amendment that identifies the changed provision. Each party states that it has authority to enter into this amendment and that the person signing for that party has authority to bind it. This amendment may be signed in counterparts. A signature delivered by electronic means has the same effect as an original signature. New York law governs this amendment, without regard to conflict of law rules.

text: This Amended and Restated Software License and Maintenance Agreement (this “Agreement”) is made as of October 1, 2019 (the “Effective Date”). Meridian Data Systems, Inc., a Delaware corporation with its principal office at 1200 Harbor Drive, Suite 400, San Diego, California 92101, is “Licensor.” Northstar Benefit Administrators, LLC, a New York limited liability company with its principal office at 88 Madison Avenue, New York, New York 10016, is “Licensee.” Licensor and Licensee previously entered into a Software License and Maintenance Agreement dated June 15, 2016, as amended before the Effective Date (the “Prior Agreement”). The parties now desire to amend and restate the Prior Agreement in its entirety. Beginning on the Effective Date, this Agreement supersedes the Prior Agreement. It governs all access to and use of the Software, all maintenance services, all support services, and all related fees. Rights and obligations that accrued under the Prior Agreement before the Effective Date remain enforceable, including payment obligations, confidentiality duties, audit rights, indemnity obligations, and limits on liability. Subject to Licensee’s payment of the fees required by this Agreement and Licensee’s compliance with this Agreement, Licensor grants Licensee a limited, nonexclusive, nontransferable license during the Term. The license permits Licensee to install and use the object-code version of the Software solely for Licensee’s internal business operations. Licensee may allow its employees, contractors, and authorized agents to use the Software on Licensee’s behalf, provided that Licensee remains responsible for their acts and omissions. Except for a reasonable number of backup copies, Licensee shall not copy the Software. Without Licensor’s prior written consent, Licensee shall not modify the Software, create derivative works, reverse engineer the Software, remove proprietary notices, rent the Software, or use the Software to process data for any unaffiliated third party. Licensor shall provide maintenance and support for the Software during any period for which Licensee has paid the applicable maintenance fees. Maintenance includes error corrections, updates, and generally released enhancements that Licensor makes available to similarly situated licensees without a separate license charge. Support includes remote assistance during Licensor’s standard support hours for reproducible errors reported through Licensor’s designated support channel. Licensor does not have any duty to support Software that Licensee has altered, used with unsupported hardware or software, or operated outside the Documentation. Licensee shall pay the license fees, maintenance fees, and other charges stated in the applicable order form or invoice. Unless the applicable order form states otherwise, invoices are due thirty days after the invoice date. Overdue amounts accrue interest at one percent per month or the highest rate allowed by law, whichever is lower. Licensee shall reimburse Licensor for taxes arising from this Agreement, other than taxes based on Licensor’s net income. Each party shall protect the other party’s confidential information using at least the same care it uses for its own similar information, and no less than reasonable care. This Agreement may be amended only by a writing signed by both parties.

text: This Manufacture and Supply Agreement ("Agreement") is made and entered into on May 26, 2017 by and between BioVectra Inc., a corporation organized under the laws of the Province of Prince Edward Island, with its principal place of business at 11 Aviation Avenue, Charlottetown, Prince Edward Island (the "Manufacturer"), and the counterparty identified on the signature page (the "Purchaser"). The Manufacturer and the Purchaser are referred to individually as a "Party" and collectively as the "Parties." The Manufacturer operates facilities for the development and production of active pharmaceutical ingredients, intermediates, and related compounds. The Purchaser wishes to engage the Manufacturer to produce and supply the products described in Schedule A (the "Products"), and the Manufacturer wishes to accept that engagement, on the terms set out below. The Manufacturer shall produce the Products in accordance with the specifications recorded in Schedule A, as those specifications may be amended in writing by the Parties from time to time (the "Specifications"). The Manufacturer shall carry out all manufacturing at the facility identified in Schedule B and shall not relocate production to any other facility without the prior written consent of the Purchaser, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld. The Manufacturer shall perform its obligations in conformity with current Good Manufacturing Practices as defined by applicable regulatory authorities and with all laws governing the manufacture, packaging, labeling, and storage of the Products. The Manufacturer shall maintain the licenses, permits, and registrations required to perform under this Agreement and shall promptly notify the Purchaser of any suspension, revocation, or material adverse change affecting them. The Purchaser shall submit firm purchase orders in the form set out in Schedule C. Each purchase order shall state the quantity of Products, the requested delivery date, and the destination. The Manufacturer shall confirm or reject each order within ten business days of receipt. An order rejected for reasons other than nonconformity with this Agreement shall be treated as accepted if the Manufacturer fails to respond within that period. Title to and risk of loss in the Products shall pass to the Purchaser upon delivery in accordance with the delivery terms recorded in Schedule A. The Manufacturer shall package and ship the Products so as to protect them against damage and deterioration during transit and storage. The Purchaser shall pay the prices set out in Schedule A. Each invoice is payable within thirty days of the invoice date. Amounts not paid when due shall bear interest at the rate of one percent per month or the maximum rate permitted by law, whichever is lower. Each Party shall keep confidential the technical, commercial, and financial information disclosed to it by the other Party in connection with this Agreement and shall use that information solely to perform its obligations. This obligation survives termination of the Agreement for a period of five years. The Parties have executed this Agreement as of the date first written above.

text: This Amendment, dated as of March 29, 2019 (this “Amendment”), amends that certain Distributor Motor Fuel Agreement dated as of January 23, 2018 (the “Agreement”), between the supplier identified in the Agreement (“Supplier”) and the distributor identified in the Agreement (“Distributor”). Capitalized terms used in this Amendment and not defined in it have the meanings given to them in the Agreement. The parties amend Section 2 of the Agreement to extend the Term through March 31, 2022, unless the Agreement ends earlier under its express termination provisions. Distributor shall purchase from Supplier, during each Contract Year after the date of this Amendment, at least eighty-five percent of the monthly average gallons purchased during the twelve-month period ending February 28, 2019. Supplier shall measure compliance on a calendar-quarter basis. If Distributor fails to meet the required volume for a quarter, Supplier may invoice Distributor for a shortfall charge equal to three cents per gallon multiplied by the difference between the required gallons and the gallons actually purchased for that quarter. Supplier’s acceptance of a shortfall charge does not waive Supplier’s right to enforce future volume requirements. The parties amend the pricing provision of the Agreement to state that the dealer tank wagon price, rack price, taxes, freight, brand fees, environmental charges, and other pass-through amounts will apply as shown on Supplier’s invoice for each delivery. Distributor shall pay each invoice by electronic funds transfer within ten days after the invoice date. If Distributor disputes an invoice, Distributor shall give Supplier written notice within five business days after receipt, identify the disputed amount, and state the basis for the dispute. All undisputed amounts shall be paid by Distributor when due. Supplier and Distributor shall work in good faith to resolve any timely invoice dispute within fifteen days after Supplier receives the notice. Distributor shall maintain all licenses, permits, insurance policies, and brand approvals required for the sale and distribution of motor fuel at each location covered by the Agreement. Distributor shall comply with all applicable laws governing motor fuel storage, transportation, sale, signage, payment card processing, environmental protection, and consumer disclosures. Upon any release, suspected release, governmental inquiry, suspension, or threatened suspension affecting any covered location, Distributor shall promptly notify Supplier. Supplier may suspend deliveries to any location if Distributor fails to pay an undisputed invoice when due, loses a required license, exceeds approved credit limits, violates brand standards, or creates a safety risk relating to receipt or storage of motor fuel. Supplier shall give notice of the suspension as soon as practicable under the circumstances. Suspension of deliveries does not terminate the Agreement unless Supplier gives a separate termination notice under the Agreement. Except as amended by this Amendment, the Agreement remains in effect. If this Amendment conflicts with the Agreement, this Amendment controls. Each party represents that the person signing this Amendment for that party has authority to bind that party. The parties may sign this Amendment in counterparts, and electronic signatures have the same effect as manual signatures.

text: ARTICLE I DEFINITIONS Section 1.1 Defined Terms. As used in this Agreement, the following terms have the meanings set forth in this Section 1.1, effective as of October 1, 2019. "Affiliate" means, with respect to any Person, any other Person that directly or indirectly controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with that Person. For this purpose, "control" means the power to direct the management and policies of a Person, whether through ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise. "Agreement" means this instrument, together with all Exhibits and Schedules attached to it, as amended from time to time in accordance with Section 9.4. "Business Day" means any day other than a Saturday, Sunday, or day on which commercial banks in New York, New York are authorized or required by law to close. "Closing Date" means the date on which the transactions contemplated by this Agreement are consummated under Article III. "Confidential Information" means all non-public information disclosed by one party to the other, whether disclosed orally, in writing, or by any other means, that a reasonable person would understand to be confidential given the nature of the information and the circumstances of disclosure. "Effective Date" means October 1, 2019. "Governmental Authority" means any federal, state, local, or foreign government, any political subdivision of a government, and any agency, court, or instrumentality exercising executive, legislative, judicial, regulatory, or administrative functions. "Material Adverse Effect" means any change or effect that is materially adverse to the business, financial condition, or results of operations of a party, taken as a whole. A change resulting from general economic conditions does not constitute a Material Adverse Effect unless it affects the party disproportionately relative to others in its industry. "Person" means any individual, corporation, partnership, limited liability company, trust, or other entity, and includes any Governmental Authority. Section 1.2 Interpretation. References to Articles, Sections, Exhibits, and Schedules are to those parts of this Agreement unless otherwise stated. The words "include" and "including" are not limiting. Headings are for convenience only and do not affect the construction of this Agreement. A reference to any statute includes any amendment to that statute and any regulation issued under it as of the Effective Date.

text: EXECUTION VERSION TRANSITION SERVICES AGREEMENT This Transition Services Agreement (this “Agreement”) is made and entered into this 26th day of August, 2019, by and between the service provider identified on the signature page hereto (“Provider”) and the recipient identified on the signature page hereto (“Recipient”). Provider and Recipient are sometimes referred to herein individually as a “Party” and collectively as the “Parties.” WHEREAS, pursuant to that certain transaction agreement dated as of the date hereof, the Parties have consummated a separation, divestiture, acquisition, or related corporate transaction; and WHEREAS, in connection therewith, Recipient desires to obtain from Provider, and Provider is willing to provide to Recipient, certain transitional administrative, operational, information technology, finance, human resources, facilities, procurement, regulatory, and other support services for a limited period following the closing of such transaction, all upon the terms and conditions set forth herein. NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements contained herein, and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the Parties agree as follows: Provider shall provide, or cause its affiliates or designated subcontractors to provide, the services described in the schedules attached hereto, as such schedules may be amended by mutual written agreement of the Parties (the “Services”). The Services shall be performed in a manner substantially consistent with the manner, scope, quality, frequency, and level of care with which such services were provided to the applicable business during the twelve-month period immediately preceding the closing date, except as otherwise expressly stated in the applicable schedule. Recipient shall use the Services solely for the conduct of the business transferred or otherwise supported in connection with the transaction and shall not resell, assign, sublicense, or provide the benefit of the Services to any third party except with Provider’s prior written consent. Recipient shall provide reasonable cooperation, access, information, approvals, and personnel as Provider may reasonably request in order to perform the Services. In consideration for the Services, Recipient shall pay Provider the fees, charges, and reimbursements set forth in the applicable schedule, together with any documented out-of-pocket costs, taxes, or third-party charges incurred in accordance with this Agreement. Provider shall invoice Recipient monthly in arrears unless otherwise specified, and Recipient shall pay undisputed amounts within thirty days after receipt of invoice. The term of each Service shall commence on the closing date and continue for the period specified in the applicable schedule, unless earlier terminated in accordance with this Agreement. Recipient may terminate any Service upon reasonable prior written notice, provided that Recipient shall remain responsible for all fees and costs accrued through the effective date of termination. Neither Party shall be liable for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential, or punitive damages arising out of this Agreement, except to the extent resulting from fraud, willful misconduct, breach of confidentiality, or payment obligations.

text: This First Amendment to Asset Purchase Agreement, dated as of March 27, 2019, is entered into by and among Harbor Medical Holdings, LLC, a Delaware limited liability company, as buyer, Northstar Surgical Assets, Inc., a Minnesota corporation, as seller, and Northstar Health Group, LLC, solely for the limited purposes stated in Section 6. Buyer and Seller are parties to that certain Asset Purchase Agreement dated February 12, 2019, under which Buyer agreed to purchase specified assets used in Seller’s ambulatory surgery center business. The parties desire to amend the Agreement to revise the closing mechanics, adjust the purchase price for identified excluded assets, and confirm certain transition obligations. For consideration each party considers adequate, the parties agree as follows. The definition of Purchased Assets in Section 1.1 of the Agreement is amended to include all prepaid service contracts listed on Schedule A to this First Amendment, all assignable warranties relating to surgical equipment, and all transferable permits required for operation of the acquired business after Closing. The Purchased Assets exclude the receivables identified on Schedule B, Seller’s cash on hand, and the employee benefit plans maintained by Seller or its affiliates. Section 2.2 of the Agreement is amended to provide that the purchase price shall equal $2,850,000, subject to the working capital adjustment set forth in Section 2.4. At Closing, Buyer shall pay $2,550,000 by wire transfer to the account designated by Seller at least two business days before Closing. Buyer shall deposit $300,000 with Lakeside Escrow Services, Inc. under the escrow agreement signed by the parties on the Effective Date. The escrowed amount shall secure Seller’s indemnification obligations for twelve months after Closing, except for claims based on fraud or taxes. The Closing shall occur on April 5, 2019, unless the parties agree in writing to another date. At Closing, Seller shall deliver a bill of sale, assignment agreements for the assumed contracts, evidence of lien releases, and a certificate confirming that the representations in the Agreement remain accurate in all material respects. Buyer shall deliver the purchase price, an assumption agreement for the Assumed Liabilities, and evidence of its authority to complete the transaction. Seller shall provide transition support for ninety days after Closing. That support shall include access to billing records, vendor contacts, equipment maintenance files, and reasonable assistance with permit transfers. Buyer shall reimburse Seller for documented out-of-pocket expenses approved in advance by Buyer. Seller shall not charge any management fee for such support. Except as amended by this First Amendment, the Agreement remains in effect. Any conflict between this First Amendment and the Agreement shall be resolved in favor of this First Amendment. Capitalized terms used but not defined in this First Amendment have the meanings given in the Agreement. Each party represents that it has authority to sign and perform this First Amendment and that its signature creates a binding obligation enforceable under its terms. This First Amendment may be signed in counterparts, and electronic signatures have the same effect as original signatures.
