text: This Cerner Executive Severance Agreement, effective as of December 14, 2017 (the "Effective Date"), supplements and amends the terms of employment between Cerner Corporation (the "Company") and the executive identified on the signature page (the "Executive"). This Executive Severance Agreement governs the compensation, benefits, and obligations that apply upon a qualifying termination of the Executive's employment. Where this Executive Severance Agreement conflicts with any prior agreement between the parties, the terms set forth here control as to the subjects addressed. The Executive remains an at-will employee. Nothing in this Executive Severance Agreement grants the Executive a right to continued employment for any fixed term, and either party may terminate the employment relationship at any time, with or without cause, subject to the payment obligations described below. The Company will provide severance benefits if it terminates the Executive's employment without Cause, or if the Executive resigns for Good Reason, in each case as those terms are defined in Section 3. Severance benefits are not payable if the Company terminates the Executive for Cause, if the Executive resigns without Good Reason, or if the employment ends because of the Executive's death or disability. The Company's obligation to pay severance benefits is conditioned on the Executive's execution and non-revocation of a release of claims in the form the Company provides, delivered within the period stated in Section 4. Upon a qualifying termination, the Company will pay the Executive a severance amount equal to the sum specified in Schedule A, payable in accordance with the schedule set out there. The Company will also pay the Executive any earned but unpaid base salary through the termination date and reimburse any business expenses incurred before that date and submitted under the Company's expense policy. These amounts are subject to applicable tax withholding. The Executive's rights under this Executive Severance Agreement are personal and may not be assigned. The Company may assign its rights and obligations to any successor to its business. This Executive Severance Agreement binds and benefits the parties and their respective successors and permitted assigns. The parties agree that this Executive Severance Agreement is governed by the laws of the State of Missouri, without regard to its conflict-of-laws principles. If any provision is held unenforceable, the remaining provisions continue in effect, and the unenforceable provision is modified to the minimum extent necessary to make it enforceable. This Executive Severance Agreement may be amended only by a writing signed by both parties.

text: May 18, 2017 Re: Employment Agreement Dear Sheila: I have recently reviewed the terms and conditions of your employment and am pleased to provide this letter agreement setting forth the principal terms under which you will continue to be employed by the Company. Effective as of May 18, 2017, you shall serve in the position assigned to you by the Company from time to time, reporting to such officer or manager as the Company may designate, and you shall devote your full working time, attention, skill, and best efforts to the faithful performance of your duties. You agree to comply with all lawful policies, procedures, codes of conduct, confidentiality requirements, and reasonable directions of the Company, as amended from time to time. Your compensation shall consist of a base salary payable in accordance with the Company’s regular payroll practices, less applicable withholdings and deductions. You shall also be eligible to participate in such benefit plans, bonus programs, paid time off policies, and other employee arrangements as the Company may make available to similarly situated employees, subject in each case to the terms, conditions, eligibility requirements, and amendment or termination provisions of the applicable plan or policy. No bonus, incentive payment, or other discretionary compensation shall be deemed earned unless and until approved in writing by the Company and paid, and the Company may modify or discontinue any discretionary program at its sole discretion. During and after your employment, you shall not use, disclose, copy, remove, or permit access to any confidential, proprietary, or trade secret information of the Company except as required in the proper performance of your duties. All documents, records, data, materials, devices, work product, inventions, discoveries, developments, writings, designs, processes, and other property created, obtained, or used by you in connection with your employment shall be and remain the exclusive property of the Company, and you hereby assign to the Company all right, title, and interest therein to the extent permitted by law. Nothing in this letter shall be construed as a promise of employment for any specific period. Your employment is at will and may be terminated by either you or the Company at any time, with or without cause or notice, subject only to the payment of earned wages and any rights required by applicable law. This agreement supersedes prior inconsistent understandings concerning the matters addressed herein and may be amended only by a written instrument signed by you and an authorized representative of the Company. Your employment with State Street will end on the Separation Date stated in the signature block. Until that date, you will remain employed in your current position or in another transition role assigned by State Street. During the transition period, you will perform requested transition duties, respond promptly to reasonable business requests, protect State Street information, and comply with all policies that apply to active employees. State Street may end the transition period earlier if you resign, fail to perform assigned duties, violate policy, or engage in conduct that harms State Street’s business interests. After you sign this Agreement and the revocation period expires, State Street will provide the separation benefits described in the schedule attached to this Agreement. Those benefits exceed what you would otherwise receive. State Street will withhold required taxes and deductions from all taxable payments. No payment under this Agreement changes the amount, timing, or status of any vested benefit under a State Street benefit plan. The governing plan documents control those benefits. In exchange for the separation benefits, you release State Street, its current and former officers, its current and former employees, its current and former directors, its benefit plans, and its plan fiduciaries from all claims arising on or before the date you sign this Agreement. This release includes claims relating to your employment, compensation, benefits, performance reviews, leave, termination, or any alleged discrimination, retaliation, wage violation, contract breach, tort, or statutory violation. It does not include claims that the law does not allow you to release, claims to enforce this Agreement, claims for vested benefits, or claims arising after you sign this Agreement. You will not keep copies of State Street information except personal employment records and documents needed to enforce this Agreement. Your confidentiality, intellectual property, non-solicitation, and other continuing obligations remain in effect. Nothing in this Agreement prevents you from filing a charge, giving truthful information, or cooperating with any government agency. Disclosures protected by law are also permitted. You may not, however, recover personal monetary relief based on a released claim, except where an agency program does not allow that restriction. This Agreement does not admit wrongdoing by you or State Street. It contains the complete agreement concerning your transition and separation, except for obligations that this Agreement expressly preserves. Any amendment must appear in a written document signed by you and an authorized State Street representative.

Before accepting this Stock Option, and before taking any action with respect to it, the Participant has had the opportunity to consult an independent tax adviser. Upon any exercise of this Stock Option, the Company may require the Participant to satisfy all tax withholding requirements in a manner permitted by the applicable equity plan and by the Company’s administrative procedures. To satisfy those requirements, the Company may withhold from wages or other amounts payable to the Participant, require a cash payment, retain shares otherwise deliverable on exercise, or use another method that the Company approves. No provision of the equity plan, the award agreement, any notice of grant, or any related communication shall be interpreted to classify this Stock Option as an incentive stock option. If any administrative form or communication describes this Stock Option inconsistently with this Section, this Section controls. The Participant shall not claim that the Company, any parent or subsidiary, or any of their directors, officers, employees, or agents promised incentive stock option treatment for this Stock Option. Nor shall the Participant seek damages, indemnification, reimbursement, or other payment from any such person or entity based on the absence of incentive stock option treatment. This allocation of tax responsibility forms a material part of the Company’s decision to grant this Stock Option.

text: The pawn shop clerk’s testimony supplied competent evidence of Turns’s knowledge and intent. At trial, the clerk identified Turns as the person who brought the rifle to the shop, negotiated its sale, and described its operation. He also related that “he [Turns] told me that the gun was” capable of firing continuously when the trigger remained depressed. That statement did more than place Turns near the weapon. It showed that he understood the rifle’s automatic character before he relinquished possession of it for money. Turns argues that the clerk’s testimony should carry little weight because the government did not recover the rifle from him and because the sale occurred outside the presence of law enforcement. That argument fails. Knowledge need not be proved through direct observation by an officer. A defendant’s own description of a firearm may establish awareness of its characteristics, especially when the description concerns the feature that brings the weapon within the statute. Nothing in the record gave the clerk an apparent reason to invent the statement. His account also fit the surrounding facts. Turns did not present the rifle as an ordinary hunting weapon. He discussed how it fired, accepted payment, and left it with a commercial buyer who later surrendered it to authorities. The statement also was admissible as an opposing party’s statement. Turns made the remark during the transaction at issue, and the government offered it against him. The district court did not need to treat the statement as hearsay. Nor did the quoted phrasing create a confrontation problem. The witness appeared at trial, testified from personal recollection, and faced cross-examination about his memory, his dealings with police, and his interest in avoiding trouble for the shop. Jurors had the tools needed to decide whether to credit him. Other evidence in the record reinforced the same conclusion. An examiner testified that the rifle fired more than one round by a single function of the trigger. Pawn shop records tied the transaction to Turns. The clerk’s identification was firm, and Turns offered no persuasive innocent explanation for his possession of a fully automatic firearm. On this record, a rational juror could find beyond a reasonable doubt that Turns knowingly possessed and transferred a machine gun. The district court also acted within its discretion in refusing to strike the testimony after defense counsel emphasized the incomplete wording of the clerk’s quoted statement. The clerk did not claim uncertainty about the substance of what Turns said. His phrasing reflected ordinary speech, not a gap in proof. The court properly allowed counsel to argue that point to the jury while declining to remove relevant evidence from the case. The conviction therefore rests on more than suspicion arising from proximity. It rests on possession, sale, mechanical testing, business records, and Turns’s own words about the rifle’s automatic firing capability. Because the clerk’s testimony was a central part of that proof, the district court committed no reversible error in admitting it.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit

label: ai
text: The third claim rests on the allegation that an employee at Fred Meyer’s Raleigh Hills store refused to sell a pair of jeans to Al-Saeed. Taking the pleaded facts in the light most favorable to Al-Saeed, the claim describes a discrete retail transaction in which a customer selected merchandise offered for sale to the public, presented himself for purchase, and was denied completion of that purchase by store personnel. Such an allegation, if supported by competent evidence that the refusal was because of a protected characteristic, may implicate the right to contract for goods and services on equal terms and may also state a claim under applicable public-accommodation principles. The difficulty is not that the alleged conduct is legally insignificant. A refusal to sell ordinary merchandise in a retail store is not a trivial inconvenience when it is imposed selectively. Retail stores open to the public may not reserve the benefits of their inventory for favored customers while excluding others on impermissible grounds. The purchase of clothing is a routine commercial transaction, but the statutory protections governing such transactions do not depend on the price of the item or the social importance of the goods. If a cashier or sales associate refused to process Al-Saeed’s purchase because of his race, national origin, religion, ethnicity, or other protected status, the refusal would be actionable even if another employee later could have corrected it. The record, however, must contain more than Al-Saeed’s conclusion that the denial was discriminatory. He must identify facts from which a reasonable trier of fact could infer that the employee’s refusal was motivated by an unlawful reason rather than by confusion, store policy, register error, suspected loss-prevention concern, lack of authority, or some other nondiscriminatory circumstance. Relevant facts might include statements made by the employee, disparate treatment of similarly situated customers, departure from normal sales procedures, the absence of any legitimate explanation, or evidence that the store had a pattern of denying service to customers sharing Al-Saeed’s protected characteristics. The allegation that the item was jeans, standing alone, does not supply the necessary inference; the legal significance arises from the asserted refusal to transact and the reason for that refusal. Nor is Fred Meyer automatically insulated from liability merely because the alleged refusal was carried out by a single store employee. A retailer acts through its agents in the ordinary course of business, and an employee assigned to assist customers or complete sales may bind the business for purposes of public-accommodation and contracting obligations. Still, liability requires proof that the challenged act occurred within the relevant commercial setting and that the denial of service was sufficiently connected to the employee’s duties. Accordingly, the third claim is viable only to the extent Al-Saeed can establish that he was ready and able to purchase the jeans, that a Fred Meyer employee refused to complete the sale, and that the refusal was substantially motivated by a prohibited basis. Without evidence supporting that causal link, the claim cannot proceed beyond allegation.

text: (F) Interaction of Medicaid and CHIP payment policies with health care delivery generally.— (1) In carrying out this section, the Secretary, in consultation with States, providers, managed care entities, beneficiaries, and other interested parties, shall evaluate the manner in which payment policies under title XIX and title XXI affect access to covered items and services, the adequacy and stability of provider networks, the availability of primary, specialty, behavioral health, dental, pharmacy, and long-term services and supports, and the capacity of delivery systems to furnish care in a timely, culturally competent, and geographically accessible manner. (2) Such evaluation shall include consideration of whether payment rates, supplemental payments, value-based purchasing arrangements, capitation methodologies, directed payments, quality incentives, cost-sharing requirements, prior authorization policies, and other utilization management practices, individually or in combination, promote or impede beneficiary access to medically necessary care. The Secretary shall give particular attention to the effect of such policies on children, pregnant and postpartum individuals, individuals with disabilities, older adults, individuals residing in rural or underserved areas, individuals with limited English proficiency, and persons with chronic, complex, or behavioral health conditions. (3) A State plan, State child health plan, waiver, demonstration project, or managed care contract may not be approved or continued in effect if the Secretary determines, after notice and opportunity for corrective action, that the payment and delivery policies established thereunder are likely to result in a material reduction in access to covered services, an unreasonable delay in furnishing such services, or a provider network that is insufficient to meet the needs of enrolled individuals. In making such determination, the Secretary may consider beneficiary complaints, encounter and claims data, appointment wait times, provider participation levels, service utilization patterns, quality measures, and any other reliable information. (4) Nothing in this subparagraph shall be construed to require uniform payment rates across providers or geographic areas, or to prohibit States from adopting alternative payment models, integrated delivery systems, risk-based arrangements, or care coordination initiatives, if such policies are consistent with efficiency, economy, quality of care, actuarial soundness where applicable, and equal access to covered services. Payment policies shall be designed and administered so as not to discriminate against providers serving a disproportionate share of low-income, medically complex, or otherwise underserved beneficiaries. (5) The Secretary shall issue guidance establishing standards and methods for measuring the relationship between payment policy and access to care, including minimum data elements, reporting intervals, beneficiary and provider engagement procedures, and criteria for identifying access concerns requiring State remediation. A State shall submit such information as the Secretary may require and shall make publicly available, in a form and manner specified by the Secretary, summaries of access findings and any corrective action plan. (6) If the Secretary determines that a State has failed to maintain payment and delivery policies consistent with this subparagraph, the Secretary may require modification of rates, contracts, network standards, utilization controls, or other program terms, and may withhold or defer Federal financial participation to the extent permitted by law until compliance is achieved.

text: The signal introduced by "See, e.g.," performs a distinct evidentiary function within legal citation, and its misuse produces avoidable confusion about the strength of a proposition. When a writer deploys "See, e.g.," she represents two things at once. First, the authority cited supports the stated proposition indirectly, requiring an inferential step the reader must supply. Second, the citation is one of several available sources, offered as a representative example rather than an exhaustive catalog. A reader who understands both signals can gauge the depth of support without pulling every case from the reporter. Courts and commentators sometimes treat the combined signal as interchangeable with a bare "See" or a bare "e.g.," but the distinction matters. A bare "See" tells the reader that the cited authority supports the proposition through inference alone. A bare "e.g." tells the reader that other authorities also stand for the point and could be marshaled if needed. Joining the two signals communicates that the cited source supports the proposition inferentially and that additional sources exist. Dropping either component understates the actual state of the authority. The practical consequence appears most sharply in appellate briefing. A litigant who writes "See, e.g.," implicitly concedes that no single authority establishes the proposition directly. Opposing counsel may seize on that concession, arguing that the position rests on inference stacked upon inference and that the cited example does not compel the result urged. A drafter should therefore reserve the combined signal for propositions genuinely supported by a body of decisions, not for a lone case dressed up to appear representative of a broader consensus. A further problem arises when the "e.g." promise goes unfulfilled. If a brief signals that the cited case is merely illustrative, the reader reasonably expects that other decisions align. Should the drafter know of contrary authority, or should the cited case prove to be an outlier, the signal misleads. The obligation of candor to the tribunal does not evaporate because the drafter selected a permissive citation form. Rule 3.3 governs representations of fact and law regardless of the citation signal chosen, and a misleading "e.g." can violate that duty as readily as a misquoted holding. Editors reviewing draft opinions should confirm that each "See, e.g.," citation reflects a considered judgment about both the inferential distance and the breadth of support. The signal is not decorative. It carries meaning that trained readers rely upon, and careless use erodes the reliability of the entire citation apparatus. When the drafter can cite an authority that supports the proposition directly, the plain "See" or no signal at all serves better. When the drafter genuinely relies on one representative case among many, "See, e.g.," states that reliance honestly. The choice should track the record of authority, not the drafter's rhetorical preference for the appearance of abundant support.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit

label: ai
text: Whether ERISA imposes on health maintenance organizations a fiduciary duty to disclose the compensation arrangements they maintain with treating physicians presents a question this court has not before decided. The answer turns on the interaction between the fiduciary obligations codified at 29 U.S.C. § 1104 and the structural realities of managed care, where the entity administering plan benefits often shares in the financial consequences of the medical decisions made under it. The plaintiffs contend that when an HMO functions as a plan fiduciary, its duty of loyalty compels affirmative disclosure of any incentive structure that rewards physicians for limiting care. They frame the withheld information as material to a beneficiary's evaluation of the treatment she receives and, more broadly, to her decision to enroll or remain enrolled. The defendant responds that the compensation scheme concerns the business of insurance rather than the administration of the plan, and that ERISA's fiduciary provisions reach the latter alone. We begin with the statutory text. A fiduciary owes its duties only when, and to the extent that, it exercises discretionary authority over plan management or the disposition of plan assets. An HMO wears two hats. It contracts with physicians and structures payment, an insurer's function, and it also decides which claims to pay and which services the plan covers, an administrator's function. The fiduciary duty attaches to the second role, not the first. The design of a capitation or bonus arrangement is a decision about how the HMO conducts its enterprise. It is not itself an exercise of discretion over a particular beneficiary's benefits. That distinction does not resolve the disclosure question. A duty to disclose can arise even where the underlying conduct lies outside fiduciary responsibility, if silence about that conduct would render the fiduciary's own representations misleading. The controlling inquiry is therefore whether the HMO, in the course of administering benefits, made statements or created expectations that its compensation practices rendered materially incomplete. Where a plan describes itself as furnishing medically necessary care while omitting that its physicians are paid to withhold such care, the omission may deceive. We hold that ERISA imposes no freestanding, categorical duty on an HMO to disclose physician compensation. The Supreme Court's guidance in Pegram v. Herdrich counsels against reading the statute to convert every incentive arrangement into a per se fiduciary breach, given that Congress legislated against a background in which such arrangements were understood and, in some measure, encouraged. A rule requiring blanket disclosure would import into ERISA a policy judgment Congress declined to make. The narrower duty survives. When an HMO's affirmative representations about the care it provides are rendered materially misleading by an undisclosed incentive to ration that care, the fiduciary must speak. Because the record before us does not establish that the defendant made such representations, we decline to find a breach on this record, and we remand for findings on that question alone.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit

label: ai
text: The second element of the tripartite framework, which I designate here as element B, warrants closer scrutiny than the majority affords it. The majority treats causation as a formality once breach is established. That treatment mistakes the burden the plaintiff must carry. Element B requires the plaintiff to show that the defendant's conduct was a but-for cause of the injury. The record does not support that showing. The plaintiff's expert conceded on cross-examination that the same harm would have followed even absent the defendant's omission. That concession is fatal. Where the injury would have occurred regardless of the breach, causation collapses, and no amount of proof on duty or breach can revive the claim. The majority responds that a jury could reasonably infer causation from the sequence of events. I disagree. Temporal sequence is not causation. The plaintiff points to the fact that her losses accrued after the defendant failed to issue the warning. Post hoc reasoning of that kind has never sufficed under our precedents. In Harmon v. Delacourt, we rejected an identical inference and held that a plaintiff must connect the breach to the harm through evidence, not chronology. There is a further problem the majority overlooks. Even if the plaintiff cleared the but-for hurdle, element B also demands proximate cause. The chain here runs through two intervening decisions by third parties, each of them independent, and each unforeseeable to the defendant at the time of the alleged breach. An intervening act of that character severs liability. The defendant could not have anticipated that a supplier would falsify its records or that a regulator would act on those false records without verification. Foreseeability marks the outer boundary of responsibility, and this conduct falls beyond it. I recognize the appeal of allowing the claim to reach a jury. Sympathy for the plaintiff is understandable given the magnitude of her losses. Sympathy does not supply the missing causal link. Our task is to apply the elements as the law defines them, not to relax them when the result seems harsh. If element B is to mean anything, it must screen out claims where the plaintiff cannot trace the injury to the defendant's act. The proper disposition is judgment for the defendant on causation. That result does not absolve the defendant of the breach the majority correctly finds. It reflects the plain truth that breach without causation yields no liability. I would reverse and enter judgment accordingly. Because the majority holds otherwise, I respectfully dissent from Part III of the opinion, while joining the court's treatment of duty in Part II.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit

label: ai
text: Having used words of art to describe areas eligible for opt-in, Congress chose a legal classification rather than a geographic guess. That choice matters. A legislature that adopts technical language takes the settled consequences of that language, even when members do not pause over every application. The statute therefore asks whether the area fits the legal category Congress named, not whether a floor manager could have identified the area on a map. Respondents read the opt-in provision as if Congress first listed a set of places in ordinary speech and then expected agencies to refine the list through policy judgment. The text does the opposite. It uses terms that already carried legal content. Those terms supplied the boundary of eligibility. When Congress borrows such terms, courts presume that Congress borrowed their established meaning unless the statute shows a contrary design. No such contrary design appears here. The surrounding provisions confirm that Congress expected classification to follow law, not administrative preference. They refer to status, jurisdiction, and governing authority in the same technical register. That pattern leaves little room for a free-floating inquiry into congressional expectation. Nor does the supposed ignorance of Congress change the analysis. Even a Congress unaware of every affected area would have understood the effect of using a legal category. If some areas turned out, after applying settled doctrine, to possess the attributes described by the statutory terms, those areas could opt in. If other areas lacked those attributes, they could not. That result does not frustrate the statute. It implements the mechanism Congress selected. Legislatures often enact rules whose full reach becomes clear only when facts are tested against legal standards. Courts do not treat that ordinary feature of legislation as a drafting failure. The contrary rule would make eligibility depend on speculation about what Congress subjectively pictured. That approach would produce unstable results. One court might infer that Congress contemplated only populous areas. Another might infer that Congress focused on areas with a particular history of regulation. A third might deny eligibility because the area presents an unusual institutional form. None of those limitations appears in the enacted text. The judicial role does not permit adding them under the label of probable expectation. This reading also preserves the function of the opt-in scheme. Congress created a channel through which qualifying areas could assume the statutory framework by choice. It did not require Congress to identify each qualifying area in advance. The statutory design therefore accommodates later determinations of status. An area that satisfies the words of art may invoke the opt-in procedure, subject to the statute’s express conditions.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: The opt-in mechanism turns on a phrase that resists confident application. The statute permits opt-in for "any area in the State classified," but it does not identify the taxonomy that supplies the classification. Three candidates present themselves. The first is the zoning schedule adopted under Chapter 12. A second is the environmental designation regime administered by the Department. A third is the census-based tract system referenced elsewhere in the same title. Each yields a different map of eligible areas, and the differences are not trivial. Under the zoning reading, roughly a quarter of the incorporated territory qualifies. Under the environmental reading, the figure drops below ten percent. The legislature's silence on this point is not an accident that a court may cure by preference. Drafting history shows that an earlier version read "any area classified under Section 4," and that the cross-reference was struck in committee. A reference deliberately removed cannot be restored through interpretation. The better reading treats "classified" as incorporating whatever classification the enacting body was administering at the time, which was the zoning schedule. That schedule was in force, was published, and was the only classification the statute elsewhere assumes. This conclusion carries a cost worth stating plainly. Municipalities that relied on the environmental designation to structure their opt-in resolutions have proceeded on a mistaken premise, and their resolutions are vulnerable to challenge. The remedy is not judicial rewriting but legislative correction or fresh resolutions grounded in the zoning schedule. Courts should resist the temptation to select the classification that produces the widest coverage on the theory that broader opt-in serves the statute's purpose. Purpose does not override a text that fixes eligibility by an identifiable classification. The practical instruction for counsel is direct. Before advising a client that an area qualifies, confirm its status under the zoning schedule as it stood on the effective date. A later reclassification does not retroactively confer eligibility, and an area removed from the schedule loses its standing to opt in.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: The text of the statute is dispositive of the question presented. Section 503(b)(4) provides for the allowance of "reasonable compensation for professional services rendered by an attorney or an accountant of an entity whose expense is allowable under" the enumerated subparagraphs of Section 503(b)(3). Among those enumerated provisions is subparagraph (D), which encompasses the actual, necessary expenses incurred by a creditor, an indenture trustee, an equity security holder, or a committee representing creditors or equity security holders (other than a committee appointed under Section 1102) in making a substantial contribution to a case under Chapter 9 or Chapter 11. A straightforward reading of the statute, therefore, authorizes reasonable allowances for attorney's fees or other professional fees incurred by a member of a committee, provided that the underlying expense of the committee itself is one for which the statute permits recovery. We decline the invitation to read into this framework any limitation not appearing on the face of the enacted language. The structure of the two provisions operates in tandem: subparagraph (D) governs the allowability of the entity's expense, and paragraph (4) governs the compensation of professionals engaged by that entity. Where the antecedent expense qualifies under subparagraph (D), the professional fees follow as a matter of statutory course, subject only to the requirement of reasonableness that Congress expressly imposed. Nothing in the text conditions the recovery of professional fees upon a showing distinct from that required to establish the allowability of the entity's own expenses. To hold otherwise would require us to graft additional prerequisites onto a provision that Congress drafted with evident care and internal cross-reference. The interpretive canons counsel against such an approach. When the words of a statute are unambiguous, the judicial inquiry is complete, and we must enforce the provision according to its terms. The reasonableness limitation supplies a meaningful and administrable constraint; it ensures that allowances are commensurate with the value and necessity of the services rendered without permitting recovery of fees that are excessive, duplicative, or unrelated to the substantial contribution for which the underlying expense is compensable. We recognize that the substantial-contribution requirement itself imposes a demanding threshold, and we do not diminish it here. That threshold governs whether the committee's expenses are allowable in the first instance. Once that predicate is satisfied, however, the attendant professional fees are not subject to some further, unwritten test of independent contribution. The professional need not demonstrate a substantial contribution separate from that of the committee it served.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit

label: ai
text: The plaintiff urges us to read the phrase "actual delivery" as ambiguous, and the invitation is not without its appeal. An ambiguity would open the door to the statute's legislative history, and that history, drawn from committee reports and floor statements, favors the construction the plaintiff advances. But the door does not open merely because we would prefer to walk through it. We may resort to legislative history only when the statutory text will not resolve the question before us. Here the text resolves it. The word "actual" modifies "delivery" for a reason. Congress used "delivery" elsewhere in the same section without the modifier, and we presume that the added word does work the unmodified term does not. Where a statute distinguishes between "delivery" and "actual delivery," the latter requires a physical transfer of possession rather than a constructive or documentary one. The plaintiff's reading would collapse that distinction and render the modifier surplusage. We decline to treat as meaningless a word the drafters chose to include. The plaintiff responds that "actual" can bear a looser sense in ordinary speech, and that this looseness supplies the ambiguity we need. Ordinary looseness is not statutory ambiguity. A term is ambiguous when the text permits two readings that a competent reader could adopt after considering the provision as a whole. The plaintiff has identified no second reading that survives contact with the surrounding language. That a word admits shades of meaning in casual usage does not license a court to select the shade that helps the party invoking it. We recognize the practical consequences of this holding. A rule keyed to physical possession will burden transactions that the parties intended to complete by documentary transfer, and the plaintiff contends that Congress could not have meant so inconvenient a result. Inconvenience is not ambiguity either. When the language is plain, the remedy for an unwise result lies with the legislature, not with a court willing to manufacture doubt as a pretext for consulting the record it would rather follow. Our task is to apply the statute Congress wrote, and the statute Congress wrote speaks to physical delivery. The temptation to find ambiguity grows stronger as the stakes rise, because the legislative history so often supplies a more comfortable answer than the enacted text. That temptation is precisely what the plain meaning rule guards against. If we could conjure an ambiguity whenever the history pointed our way, the rule would protect nothing. We therefore hold the statute unambiguous as applied to these facts, and we do not consult the legislative history. The judgment below, which rested on a contrary reading, cannot stand. We reverse and remand for entry of judgment consistent with this opinion.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit

label: ai
text: There is no principled way to read the language of § 503(b)(4) to allow recovery of attorney’s and accountant’s fees “of an entity whose expense is allowable” under § 503(b)(3) when the entity itself has no allowable expense under that provision. The statute ties professional compensation to a qualifying entity. It does not create an independent right to fees for professionals who serve a party that fails to satisfy § 503(b)(3). Congress chose a dependent formulation, and courts must give that choice effect. Section 503(b)(3) identifies the entities that may receive administrative expenses for specified conduct, including a creditor, an indenture trustee, an equity security holder, or a committee that makes a substantial contribution in a case under chapter 9 or chapter 11. Section 503(b)(4) then permits “reasonable compensation for professional services rendered by an attorney or an accountant of an entity whose expense is allowable under” those listed subparagraphs. The sequence matters. First, the entity must hold an allowable expense under § 503(b)(3). Second, the attorney or accountant of that entity may seek reasonable compensation. A court that skips the first step rewrites the statute by treating § 503(b)(4) as if it referred to an attorney or accountant whose services produced a benefit to the estate. It says no such thing. The contrary reading also drains the phrase “whose expense is allowable” of work. If a professional may recover whenever the professional’s own services helped the case, the entity’s allowable expense becomes irrelevant. That approach converts a limiting clause into surplusage. Bankruptcy statutes distribute estate assets under strict priorities. Administrative expense status carries special force because it reduces the recovery available to other creditors. A court should not expand that priority through implication when Congress placed a condition directly in the text. The statutory design confirms the point. Section 503(b)(3) does not reward every useful act. It covers discrete categories and imposes requirements, including the substantial-contribution standard where applicable. Section 503(b)(4) follows that gatekeeping scheme. It compensates professionals only when they serve an entity that passed through the gate. The professional’s work may have value, but value alone does not establish priority. Congress could have authorized compensation for any attorney or accountant whose services benefited the estate. Instead, it limited compensation to professionals “of” an entity with an allowable expense. Nor does policy justify a different result. Parties often participate in bankruptcy cases for their own economic reasons, and their lawyers may advance positions that incidentally aid the estate. That circumstance does not warrant administrative priority. The Code balances incentives for participation against preservation of the estate. Section 503(b)(4) reflects that balance by linking professional fees to the entity’s qualification under § 503(b)(3). Courts lack authority to loosen that link based on equitable preference. The proper rule is direct. An attorney or accountant may recover under § 503(b)(4) only if the client is an entity whose expense qualifies under § 503(b)(3). Without that predicate, the fee request fails.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit

Let's go with a Fourth Amendment case involving digital privacy and cell-site location information (CSLI) to give it a modern, substantive feel. Or a qualified immunity case. Let's do a Fourth Amendment digital privacy case. * *Case name (implied):* United States v. [Defendant] or [Plaintiff] v. [City]. * *Core holding:* Warrantless acquisition of historical cell-site location information violates the Fourth Amendment because it infringes on a reasonable expectation of privacy. 3. **Drafting - Section by Section (Aiming for ~495 words):** * *Introduction:* State the issue and the holding. * *Factual Background:* Briefly describe the warrantless acquisition of data. * *Legal Standard:* Reasonable expectation of privacy (Katz test) and the carpenter doctrine. * *Application:* Apply the standard to the facts. Distinguish the government's arguments. * *Conclusion:* Reverse the lower court. 4. **Drafting the Text (Iterative word count check):** The principal question presented in this appeal is whether the warrantless acquisition of historical cell-site location information by state law enforcement officers violates the Fourth Amendment's protection against unreasonable searches and seizures. The defendant, Mark Tolan, moved to suppress evidence derived from twelve days of location data obtained directly from his wireless carrier without a warrant. The district court denied the motion, concluding that the third-party doctrine extinguished any reasonable expectation of privacy in the records. We must respectfully disagree. Under the established framework of Katz v. United States, a search occurs when the government violates a subjective expectation of privacy that society is prepared to recognize as reasonable. The third-party doctrine, as articulated in Smith v. Maryland and United States v. Miller, stands for the proposition that an individual has no legitimate expectation of privacy in information voluntarily conveyed to a third party. However, the sweeping nature of modern digital surveillance requires us to reassess the mechanical application of this doctrine to technologies unimaginable at the time of those decisions. Cell-site location information presents a profound challenge to traditional Fourth Amendment analysis. Unlike the discrete numbers dialed from a rotary telephone, historical cell-site location data provides the government with a comprehensive, detailed, and deeply personal chronicle of an individual's physical movements. By simply carrying a cell phone—a practical necessity in contemporary society—a user continuously broadcasts location data to service providers. To characterize this continuous transmission as a voluntary assumption of risk strains the foundational logic of the third-party doctrine.

However, it is a well-established principle of judicial restraint that courts should not formulate rules of constitutional or statutory interpretation in a factual vacuum. Accordingly, we do not pass on what sort of disclosure, if any, that Section 404 might require given a specific inquiry from a plan member. The factual circumstances surrounding such an inquiry are dispositive to the inquiry's resolution, and any attempt to delineate the boundaries of the disclosure duty absent a concrete, developed record would be inherently speculative and advisory. *Paragraph 2: The importance of factual context.* Section 404 imposes a statutory duty of loyalty and prudence upon plan fiduciaries, mandating that they discharge their responsibilities solely in the interest of plan participants and beneficiaries. While it is broadly understood that this fiduciary framework encompasses a general duty of honesty and transparency, the translation of this general duty into a specific affirmative obligation to disclose information in response to a participant's query is highly context-dependent. The nature of the requested information, the sophistication of the inquiring member, the timing of the request, and the fiduciary's knowledge at the time of the inquiry all intersect to shape the contours of any potential disclosure obligation.

text: The claimant testified that on occasion the hearing protection prevented him from perceiving verbal instructions, warning signals, and equipment sounds essential to the safe performance of his duties. When that occurred, he removed the protection so that he could hear well enough to continue working. This admission carries significant weight in assessing both the extent of his noise exposure and the credibility of his account of the working environment. The testimony cuts in two directions. On one hand, it corroborates the claimant's assertion that the workplace generated noise at levels demanding auditory attention. A worker does not discard hearing protection to catch a whisper. He discards it because the surrounding sound obscures information he must receive. The claimant's conduct therefore supports a finding that ambient noise interfered with ordinary communication, which is consistent with the audiometric evidence submitted at hearing. On the other hand, the employer contends that any resulting harm flows from the claimant's own decision to remove the equipment provided to him. That argument overstates the record. The relevant question is not whether the claimant could have kept the protection in place at all times, but whether the employer furnished a work environment in which the protection functioned as intended. Where the protection itself impaired the claimant's capacity to do the job safely, the choice presented to him was illusory. He could either wear the device and lose the ability to hear instructions and hazards, or remove it and expose himself to the very noise the device was meant to attenuate. An employer cannot satisfy its obligations by supplying equipment that cannot be used during the tasks the job requires. The evidence establishes that the claimant's removal of the protection was neither reckless nor idiosyncratic. It was a rational response to a genuine conflict between two demands the employer imposed at the same time. The duty to hear and respond to workplace communication was a condition of continued employment, while the duty to guard against hearing loss was a condition of long-term health. The employer structured the workplace so that these duties could not both be met. Responsibility for that structural defect rests with the employer, not the worker. The employer controlled the selection of protective equipment, the noise levels on the floor, and the means by which instructions were delivered. Each of these was within its power to modify. It could have provided protection engineered to admit speech while attenuating harmful frequencies. Alternatively, it could have reduced noise at the source. It did neither. The claimant's candor about removing the protection strengthens rather than undermines his position. He described the reality of his working conditions without embellishment. That reality was one in which safe performance and hearing conservation stood in direct tension. The employer created that tension and must answer for its consequences.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit

text: The question is whether OPM may give operative content to a statutory scheme that identifies the relevant benefit, assigns OPM administrative responsibility, and leaves unresolved the method for applying that benefit in recurring cases. The answer is yes. Congress did not supply every implementing detail. It created a framework and entrusted OPM with administration of the federal personnel program. OPM has filled this gap with its final rule at 5 C.F.R. part 890, and that rule falls within the authority Congress conferred. The statute does not speak with the precision the challengers demand. It establishes eligibility standards, and it directs OPM to administer the program, but it does not prescribe the exact timing, documentation, and coordination rules that determine how eligibility operates in practice. Those matters require an administrative rule. Without one, similarly situated employees and annuitants would face different results based on agency practice, carrier discretion, or after-the-fact adjudication. Congress did not require that disorder. It assigned the subject to OPM so that a uniform rule would govern federal agencies and affected individuals. OPM’s final rule reflects a permissible construction of the statute. The agency identified the statutory silence, considered the practical consequences of competing approaches, and adopted a rule that tracks the program’s structure. The regulation does not alter the class Congress protected. It explains how that class proves entitlement and how agencies must administer enrollment, notice, and related obligations. That distinction matters. An agency may not revise Congress’s judgment about who qualifies for a statutory benefit. It may specify the administrative steps needed to carry that judgment into effect. The challengers argue that silence bars regulation unless Congress speaks directly to the precise procedure at issue. That position would disable the administrative scheme Congress enacted. A benefits program cannot function through eligibility nouns alone. It needs deadlines, records, forms, and rules for resolving overlapping claims. When Congress delegates administration to an agency with OPM’s role and expertise, it authorizes regulations that make the program workable, provided those regulations remain faithful to the statute. This rule meets that standard. The rule also satisfies the requirement of reasoned decisionmaking. OPM explained the problem the rule addresses and tied its chosen solution to the statutory design. The agency did not rely on considerations outside the statute. It did not treat administrative convenience as an independent source of power. It used experience with federal personnel systems to select a uniform method for applying Congress’s policy. That is the type of administrative judgment the law permits. Nor does the rule impose a new substantive burden inconsistent with the statute. The obligations it creates serve verification, coordination, and implementation. They regulate the process by which statutory rights are administered. A claimant who satisfies the statute retains the benefit Congress granted, subject to compliance with rules that allow OPM and employing agencies to confirm eligibility and administer coverage in a consistent manner.

Department of Homeland Regulation (fictional). * *Issue:* Whether a regulatory agency violated the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) by failing to adequately consider reliance interests when repealing a prior rule. * *Holding:* Yes, the agency's action was arbitrary and capricious. 4. **Drafting - Step-by-Step (monitoring word count ~489 words):** * *Introduction:* State the issue and holding. * *Facts:* Briefly describe the agency's reversal. * *Standard of Review:* APA arbitrary and capricious. * *Analysis:* Apply the *State Farm* reliance interest doctrine. * *Conclusion:* Remand. *(Drafting text - counting words internally)* The question presented in this appeal is whether the Department of Homeland Regulation violated the Administrative Procedure Act when it rescinded the longstanding Petitioners' Benefit Rule without adequately addressing the reliance interests of affected parties. We conclude that it did. For over two decades, the Petitioners' Benefit Rule allowed qualifying small enterprises to apply for expedited regulatory review. Relying on this framework, thousands of businesses structured their compliance operations around the expedited process. In 2022, the Department issued a final rule repealing the Petitioners' Benefit Rule, citing "administrative inefficiencies" and a need to consolidate review protocols. The Department’s concise explanation spanned merely three pages of the Federal Register, dismissing the prior regime as outdated. Under the APA, a court must set aside agency action that is "arbitrary, capricious, an abuse of discretion, or otherwise not in accordance with law." It is well established that an agency must examine the relevant data and articulate a rational connection between the facts found and the decision made. When an agency rescinds a prior rule, it must, at a minimum, address the reliance interests that have developed around the prior regulatory framework. As the Supreme Court instructed in Motor Vehicle Mfrs. Ass'n v. State Farm, an agency must provide a reasoned analysis for its change in policy, which includes an assessment of the consequences of abandoning the old rule. The Department's explanation falls woefully short of this standard. The agency acknowledges that the Petitioners' Benefit Rule was widely utilized but offers only conclusory statements regarding the burden of maintaining dual review tracks. Crucially, the Department entirely failed to address the significant reliance interests at stake. Small enterprises invested substantial capital in software, personnel, and compliance infrastructure specifically designed to meet the expedited review criteria.

text: Petitioners also rely upon Bennett v. Produce, but that decision does not carry the weight they assign to it. Bennett arose from a dispute over the delivery of perishable goods under a supply contract that expressly conditioned payment on inspection at the point of receipt. The court there held that the buyer's failure to reject the shipment within the contractual window barred any later claim for defective quality. That holding turned on the specific inspection clause and the buyer's silence, neither of which is present here. The contract before us contains no inspection clause. Petitioners cannot point to any provision that conditioned their obligation on a right of examination, and the record shows no comparable window for rejection. Bennett therefore addresses a question this case does not present. Its reasoning depends on the buyer's opportunity to inspect and its decision to remain silent. Strip away that opportunity, and the rationale collapses. Petitioners respond that Bennett stands for a broader principle, namely that a party who accepts performance forfeits later objections to its quality. The opinion does not support so sweeping a reading. The court in Bennett confined itself to the contractual terms, and it said as much in rejecting the seller's request for a general waiver rule. A decision that declines to announce a broad principle cannot later be cited for one. The distinction matters to the outcome. Respondent's claim rests on a latent defect that no inspection would have revealed at delivery. Bennett assumed a defect discoverable on receipt, which is why the failure to reject proved fatal. Where the defect is hidden, the logic of forfeiture has no purchase, because the accepting party had no way to know of the flaw when it took delivery. Petitioners' argument would punish respondent for failing to detect what could not be detected. Even if Bennett reached the broader holding petitioners describe, we would decline to follow it here. The governing statute in this jurisdiction preserves a buyer's remedy for latent defects notwithstanding acceptance, and it does so in terms that leave no room for a contrary common law rule. Bennett interpreted a different statutory scheme, one that lacked the latent-defect provision on which respondent relies. A case construing another statute offers little guidance where our own legislature has spoken directly. For these reasons, Bennett does not control. Its facts differ, its holding is narrow, and the statutory framework it applied does not match ours. Petitioners' reliance on the case reflects a reading it will not bear. The court declines to extend Bennett beyond the inspection dispute it decided.

DataStream argues that this provision violates the Dormant Commerce Clause by discriminating against interstate commerce. *Analysis:* The district court upheld the statute. It found the law served a legitimate local interest in data privacy. That court erred. The statute imposes a direct burden on interstate commerce. Under Pike v. However, the cited passages at 62,222-23 articulate a highly consequential refinement of this balancing test, effectively establishing a threshold requirement that an applicant must first demonstrate that the proposed facilities are, or will be, required by the present or future public convenience and necessity before the Commission engages in a comprehensive weighing of benefits versus harms. label: ai
text: The matter before the court concerns the appeal brought by the State of Wisconsin, Department of Workforce Development, challenging the decision of the Administrative Law Judge which approved the disability benefits claim of respondent Carl Thomsen and, more contentiously, ordered that such award be made retroactive to the last day of his employment with the Wisconsin Department of Transportation. Having reviewed the record in its entirety, including the transcript of the administrative hearing, the medical documentation submitted by the claimant, and the pertinent statutory framework governing disability determinations within this jurisdiction, this court finds that the retroactive award was both legally sound and factually justified. The factual underpinnings of this dispute are largely undisputed. Thomsen was employed as a maintenance worker by the Wisconsin Department of Transportation from March 14, 2005, until his separation from service on September 22, 2020. During the latter eighteen months of his tenure, Thomsen experienced a progressive deterioration of his lumbar spine, ultimately resulting in a diagnosis of degenerative disc disease with bilateral radiculopathy. The record reflects that Thomsen initially attempted to continue performing his essential job functions despite increasing pain and decreased mobility. It was not until September 22, 2020, when he could no longer physically sustain the demands of his position, that he ceased working entirely. His application for disability benefits was filed on November 5, 2020, approximately six weeks following his departure from employment. The appellant agency contends that the retroactive award to the date of separation constitutes an abuse of discretion, arguing that the proper effective date should correspond to the date of filing, consistent with general administrative practice. The agency further asserts that Thomsen failed to demonstrate by a preponderance of evidence that he was wholly disabled as of his last day of employment, citing the absence of a contemporaneous physician's statement expressly rendering such a determination on that specific date. This court finds both arguments without merit. Regarding the effective date, Wisconsin Statutes section 36.51(4)(b) provides that disability benefits shall commence on the date the claimant establishes a total and permanent incapacity to engage in substantial gainful activity. Nothing within the statutory language mandates that such date must coincide with the date of application. Where, as here, the evidence demonstrates that the claimant's disability had in fact rendered him unable to work as of his final day of employment, the retroactive recognition of that reality serves the remedial purposes of the legislation. To hold otherwise would penalize a claimant for the administrative latency inherent in the application process itself. Regarding the sufficiency of medical evidence, the record contains the treating physician's longitudinal notes documenting a clear trajectory of functional decline. Dr. Morrison's entries from August and September of 2020 unequivocally document that Thomsen's condition had advanced to the point where he could no longer meet the physical requirements of his occupational classification. The absence of a formal declaration on the precise date of separation does not diminish the cumulative weight of this evidence.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

text: The tribunal examined the applicant's submissions regarding alleged discriminatory treatment and found them wanting. The applicant presented two categories of evidence: general reports documenting conditions in Croatia and circumstantial proof derived from his family's troubles. Neither category satisfies the evidentiary burden required to establish a prima facie case of persecution. General country conditions evidence, while relevant, cannot substitute for specific proof of individualized risk. The submitted reports describe societal attitudes and institutional practices in Croatia that may disadvantage certain groups. However, these documents establish only that adverse conditions exist in the abstract. They do not demonstrate that the applicant himself faces a particularized threat. The legal framework demands a nexus between the general environment and the claimant's specific circumstances. Here, that nexus remains unproven. The applicant's reliance on his family's difficulties compounds this evidentiary shortfall. The record contains testimony that several relatives experienced harassment, employment termination, and property damage. These incidents, while regrettable, constitute circumstantial proof at best. The applicant attempted to extrapolate from his family's experiences to his own presumed fate. This logical leap lacks sufficient grounding. Several factors undermine the probative value of the family evidence. First, the record does not establish that the family members were targeted for the same reasons the applicant claims he would be targeted. Second, the intensity and nature of the harm suffered by the relatives varies considerably, suggesting localized disputes rather than systematic persecution. Third, the applicant left Croatia years before several of the documented incidents occurred, which severs the temporal connection between his departure and subsequent events. The tribunal recognizes that direct evidence of persecution is often unavailable to claimants who flee their home country. Circumstantial evidence can support a valid claim when it permits reasonable inferences about future risk. Yet the inference the applicant urges here depends on speculation. The family's troubles, viewed in isolation, could stem from personal conflicts, criminal activity, or random misfortune. Without additional evidence linking those troubles to a protected ground, the tribunal cannot conclude that the applicant faces a similar threat. The applicant also failed to explain why he did not seek redress through available domestic mechanisms. Croatia maintains judicial institutions capable of addressing discrimination complaints. The record contains no indication that the applicant attempted to invoke these protections before departing. This failure further weakens the claim because it leaves the tribunal unable to assess whether the state is unwilling or unable to provide protection. The distinction between general conditions and specific risk is fundamental. Reports describing widespread discrimination establish background context but cannot bear the weight of an individual claim without corroborating particulars. Similarly, a family's adverse experiences, standing alone, generate sympathy but not legal entitlement. The applicant needed to bridge these gaps with evidence personal to his situation, and he did not do so. Accordingly, the tribunal concluded that this evidence of general conditions in Croatia and the circumstantial proof of discrimination based on his family's troubles were insufficient.

Even if the physical office is a place of public accommodation, and even if the policy content could theoretically be scrutinized under Title III, UNUM's actual decision-making process (the underwriting/claims decision) is fundamentally a proprietary act of risk assessment, not a discriminatory denial of physical access or provision of goods. text: 1. **Analyze the Request:** * **Subject:** Q: Are you being as honest as you can be? A: Yes, I swear to that. * **Word count:** Approximately 499 words. * **Format:** Clean prose only. No markdown, preamble, title, commentary, or explanation. * **Register/Tone:** Substantive legal document, not a generic essay (e.g., an opinion, brief, or affidavit excerpt). 2. **Deconstruct the Subject & Tone:** * The subject is a direct exchange about honesty under oath/affirmation ("I swear to that"). * A "substantive legal document" opinion passage needs to sound like a judicial opinion, a formal legal memorandum, or a findings of fact/conclusions of law section. * Themes to address: Credibility, burden of proof, the sanctity of an oath, subjective versus objective honesty, the legal standard of truthfulness, evaluation of witness testimony. 3. **Drafting - Section by Section (targeting ~499 words):** * *Introduction/Context:* Establish the framework of evaluating the assertion ("Yes, I swear to that"). Define the legal stakes of such a declaration. * *Analysis of the Oath:* Discuss the legal weight of an oath. It transforms a mere assertion into a formal declaration subject to perjury. However, an oath alone does not establish factual veracity. * *Subjective vs. Objective Honesty:* "As honest as you can be" implies a subjective standard. The law requires objective truth to the extent it can be ascertained. A witness may sincerely swear to a false memory. Thus, the sincerity of the oath ("I swear to that") must be distinguished from the factual accuracy of the testimony. * *Credibility Assessment:* How does the trier of fact evaluate this? Through corroborating evidence, consistency, demeanor. The absolute declaration ("Yes") and the invocation of an oath ("I swear") are typical markers of emphasis, but legally insufficient without substantiation. * *Conclusion/Opinion:* The ultimate finding. The oath binds the deponent morally and legally, but the court must look beyond the mere invocation of sincerity to the evidentiary record. 4. **Drafting and Word Count Management:** The inquiry presented—whether the declarant is being as honest as possible, to which the response is an unequivocal affirmation under oath—strikes at the foundational tension between subjective sincerity and objective truth within the adjudicative process. When a witness asserts, "Yes, I swear to that," the court is confronted not merely with a statement of fact, but with a performative invocation of legal and moral consequence. An oath is not a talismanic utterance that transmutes unverified assertions into established fact; rather, it is a juridical instrument that binds the declarant to a standard of truthfulness subject to penal sanction. Consequently, the analysis must bifurcate the sincerity of the declarant from the veracity of the underlying proposition. The phrase "as honest as you can be" inherently implies a subjective limitation upon the declarant's capacity for truth. It acknowledges a boundary to their transparency, whether imposed by cognitive limitation, selective memory, or an unconscious propensity for self-preservation. The law, however, demands objective veracity to the maximum extent ascertainable by the evidentiary record.

(I must include "this "Employment Agreement"" as required by the prompt, but avoid other parentheticals). * core/no-passive-overuse: Active voice where possible. "The Company employs the Executive" instead of "The Executive is employed by the Company". * core/no-repetitive-openers: Vary sentence beginnings. * core/no-robotic-transitions: No "Moreover," "Furthermore,". * core/no-rule-of-three: No dense triplets. Use two items instead of three where possible. * core/no-semicolons: No semicolons. Let's try to integrate them without parentheses if possible, or use minimal standard legal definitions. The Executive will serve as the Chief Operating Officer. The Executive will report directly to the Chief Executive Officer.
