The Executive shall also be eligible to receive an annual performance bonus of up to fifty percent (50%) of the Executive's base salary, based upon the achievement of performance objectives established by the Board of Directors. * *Confidentiality/Proprietary Rights:* The Executive acknowledges that during the course of employment, the Executive will have access to Confidential Information.

label: ai
text: The allegations set forth in the Lasoya complaint and the Juarez complaint, when examined with the particularity that pleading standards demand, do not support the sweeping representations that plaintiffs now ascribe to Jayhawk. Both complaints allege that Jayhawk made specific statements regarding the competency of the doctors to whom Ms. Ruiz was referred, and it is upon these purportedly specific statements that plaintiffs rest their theory of negligent misrepresentation. Yet the record before us reveals that the statements identified in the pleadings amount to little more than general assurances of the kind routinely exchanged in the ordinary course of a referral relationship. The distinction is not merely semantic; it is dispositive. A claim for negligent misrepresentation requires, among its essential elements, the communication of false information supplied for the guidance of another in a business transaction, upon which the recipient justifiably relies to her detriment. The information alleged here—that the referred physicians were "qualified," "experienced," and "competent to perform the procedure"—falls within the category of statements our precedents have consistently characterized as expressions of opinion or as puffery incapable of supporting liability. Such characterizations are particularly apt where, as here, the statements concern the professional judgment and skill of independent third-party practitioners over whom the referring party exercised no supervisory control and possessed no specialized knowledge exceeding that available to the plaintiff. We are not persuaded by plaintiffs' contention that the aggregation of these statements transforms them into actionable assertions of fact. The complaints do not identify any objectively verifiable representation that Jayhawk knew or should have known to be false at the time it was made. Neither the Lasoya complaint nor the Juarez complaint alleges, for instance, that Jayhawk possessed information concerning any specific deficiency, disciplinary history, or prior adverse outcome attributable to the referred physicians. Absent such particularized allegations, the statements regarding competency remain precisely the sort of general commendation that a reasonable recipient understands to reflect the speaker's favorable impression rather than a guarantee of professional results. Moreover, even were we to assume the actionable character of the statements, the element of justifiable reliance presents an independent obstacle to recovery. A patient selecting among medical providers retains the means, and indeed bears some responsibility, to evaluate the credentials of those providers through independent inquiry. Reliance upon a referring party's general endorsement of competency, unaccompanied by any specific factual representation, cannot be deemed justifiable as a matter of law. For these reasons, we conclude that the allegations concerning Jayhawk's statements regarding the competency of the referred doctors, as pleaded in both the Lasoya and Juarez complaints, are legally insufficient to state a claim upon which relief may be granted. The trial court's dismissal of these counts is accordingly affirmed.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit

label: ai
text: The proposition that a person aggrieved need only inquire of others to ascertain whether he has been wronged is, upon examination, more troublesome than its plain appeal would suggest. It presumes a symmetry of knowledge that seldom obtains in the ordinary affairs of men, and it locates the burden of discovery in precisely the party least equipped to bear it. We do not dispute that a claimant is often surrounded by persons possessed of information material to his injury. The physician who reviewed the record, the colleague who observed the transaction, the neighbor who witnessed the encounter—each may hold a fragment of the truth. But it does not follow that the aggrieved party stands in a position to solicit that truth freely, nor that those who hold it are under any compulsion, moral or legal, to yield it upon request. The suggestion that he "need only ask" assumes both that he knows whom to ask and that he knows he has cause to inquire at all. Where the very fact of the wrong is concealed, or where its wrongfulness is not apparent upon its face, the injured party may possess neither the occasion nor the knowledge to frame the question. One cannot interrogate a suspicion one does not entertain. The law has long recognized this asymmetry through the discovery rule, which suspends the running of limitations until the claimant knows, or in the exercise of reasonable diligence should know, of his injury and its cause. That doctrine would be superfluous if inquiry were as effortless as the proposition supposes. It exists precisely because the sources of knowledge are frequently guarded, dispersed, or actively obscured, and because the party best situated to supply the answer is often the party who has done the wrong. To impose upon the aggrieved a duty of perpetual interrogation is to reward the concealment of misconduct and to penalize the trust that ordinary dealings require. Diligence is properly demanded of a claimant, but diligence must be measured against what a reasonable person, unaware of any wrong, would have undertaken. We therefore reject the notion that mere access to knowledgeable persons discharges the wrongdoer of responsibility or forecloses the claimant's recovery. The availability of an answer is meaningless where the party has no reason to pose the question.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Sixth Circuit

label: ai
text: PLAGER, Circuit Judge. This appeal requires us to consider the boundary between claim construction, a question of law we review without deference, and the underlying factual determinations that may inform that construction. The distinction is neither academic nor easily drawn, and the record before us illustrates why courts have struggled to articulate a workable line. The patentee contends that the district court erred in reading a limitation into the claims that appears nowhere in their language. The accused infringer responds that the specification, read as a whole, compels the narrower construction the district court adopted. Both positions have surface appeal, but each overstates the certainty of its foundation. The words of a claim are the touchstone, yet those words do not exist in isolation; they take their meaning from the document in which they appear and from the understanding of a person of ordinary skill in the relevant art at the time of the invention. We begin, as we must, with the claim language itself. The disputed term is one of ordinary usage, and the patentee is correct that nothing in the phrasing demands the limitation the district court imposed. But that observation does not end the inquiry. A claim term, however plain on its face, must be construed in light of the specification, and here the specification repeatedly describes the invention in terms consistent with the narrower reading. The question is whether those descriptions define the invention or merely illustrate a preferred embodiment. That distinction, so easy to state, is often difficult to apply, and the temptation to import limitations from the specification must be resisted where the patentee has done no more than describe one way of practicing what the claims more broadly recite. Having reviewed the intrinsic record with these principles in mind, we conclude that the district court read the specification too narrowly. The passages on which the court relied describe the invention's operation in a particular context, but they do not disclaim broader coverage, nor do they announce the kind of clear and unmistakable limitation that would justify departing from the claim's ordinary meaning. A patentee is entitled to claim more than the specific embodiment disclosed, provided the claims support the broader scope and the disclosure enables it. The record here reveals no basis for confining the patentee to less than what the claims fairly recite. We are mindful that our conclusion carries consequences for the infringement analysis, which the district court did not reach under its narrower construction. We express no view on that question, which depends on factual determinations properly committed in the first instance to the trial court. Our holding is limited to the legal question of claim scope, and on that question we conclude the district court erred. Accordingly, we vacate the judgment of noninfringement and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Each party shall bear its own costs. VACATED AND REMANDED.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit

label: ai
text: Because we have decided that the Commission properly does not consider environmental concerns in the exercise of its ratemaking authority under FPA § 205, we must also reject petitioners' related contention that the Commission acted arbitrarily and capriciously in declining to condition its approval of the challenged rate schedules upon the utilities' adoption of specified environmental safeguards. The two arguments are, in the end, the same argument advanced under different labels, and both founder upon the same statutory limitation. The touchstone of the Commission's authority under § 205 is the establishment of rates that are just and reasonable and not unduly discriminatory or preferential. The statute directs the Commission's attention to the reasonableness of the charges themselves and to the conditions of service that bear upon those charges. It does not confer upon the Commission a roving commission to remedy every social ill that may be tangentially connected to the generation, transmission, or sale of electric energy at wholesale. Where Congress has intended the Commission to weigh environmental values, it has said so with a clarity conspicuously absent from § 205. The National Environmental Policy Act imposes procedural obligations upon the Commission in the discharge of its own functions; it does not enlarge the substantive jurisdiction that the Federal Power Act confers. Petitioners would have us hold that, having lawfully declined to make environmental considerations a substantive component of the ratemaking inquiry, the Commission nonetheless abused its discretion by failing to bootstrap those same considerations into the proceeding as conditions upon its approval. We perceive no principled distinction between the two courses. A condition that requires a utility to undertake particular environmental measures as the price of a favorable rate determination accomplishes precisely what the substantive inquiry was found not to permit: it makes the reasonableness of the rate turn upon factors outside the Commission's statutory competence. The label affixed to the exercise cannot expand the power that lies beneath it. Nor does the discretionary character of the Commission's conditioning authority alter this conclusion. Discretion is not the license to pursue ends foreign to the statute under which it is exercised; it is the freedom to select among lawful means in the service of statutory objectives. The Commission's refusal to impose the conditions petitioners urge was therefore not an abuse of discretion but a faithful recognition of the boundaries Congress has drawn. Had the Commission acceded to petitioners' request, it would have exposed its order to reversal on the ground that it rested upon considerations Congress withheld from its ken. We do not suggest that the environmental concerns petitioners raise are unworthy of attention. We hold only that the forum for their vindication is not a § 205 ratemaking proceeding, and that the Commission committed no legal error, and exercised no arbitrary discretion, in so concluding. Petitioners' challenge on this ground is accordingly denied.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: Midcoast's argument rests on a premise that the Commission was not obligated to accept. According to Midcoast, the North Alabama Pipeline lacks the throughput capacity, the transmission infrastructure, and the established shipper relationships that Midcoast has developed over years of operation. From this disparity in scale, Midcoast reasons that FERC could not rationally have concluded that the proposed pipeline would function as a competitive alternative to the service Midcoast provides under its existing Certificate. The argument conflates market presence with competitive effect. A new entrant need not match an incumbent facility in size to exert competitive pressure within the relevant market. The Commission's inquiry was whether the North Alabama Pipeline could offer shippers a genuine alternative for the transportation of gas within the geographic area at issue, not whether it could displace Midcoast entirely. Those questions are distinct, and Midcoast's briefing treats them as one. The record supports the Commission's finding. The North Alabama Pipeline would connect the same supply basins to a subset of the markets Midcoast serves, and it would do so at rates that shippers could weigh against Midcoast's own. Even a smaller pipeline that reaches a common set of customers constrains the pricing and service terms an incumbent may impose. Competition operates at the margin. A shipper with a credible option, however modest that option's overall capacity, holds bargaining power it would otherwise lack. FERC identified this dynamic and relied on it. Midcoast points to the volume differential as though it were dispositive. It is not. The Natural Gas Act directs the Commission to assess whether a proposed facility serves the public convenience and necessity, and the presence of competitive alternatives bears on that assessment. Nothing in the statute or in the Commission's certificate policy requires that a competing pipeline replicate the incumbent's dimensions before it may be counted as an alternative. The Commission reasonably determined that the North Alabama Pipeline would compete for a portion of the shipper demand that Midcoast now serves, and that this competition would benefit the affected markets. Our review is deferential. The question is not whether we would have drawn the same inference from the same facts, but whether substantial evidence supports the inference the Commission drew. The Commission explained why a smaller pipeline could still discipline Midcoast's rates and service. That explanation is grounded in the record and consistent with ordinary principles of competitive analysis. Midcoast has offered no reason to displace it beyond the assertion that bigger means better positioned, which does not answer the point. We therefore reject Midcoast's contention. The disparity in scale between the two pipelines does not undermine the Commission's finding that the North Alabama Pipeline would serve as a competitive alternative.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: Opinion for the Court filed by Circuit Judge RANDOLPH. This petition for review requires us to determine whether the agency's construction of the statutory term "adverse effect" comports with the language Congress enacted and with the interpretive principles this court has long applied. Petitioners contend that the agency exceeded its authority by extending its regulatory reach to activities Congress never intended to regulate. We agree in part, and we remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. We begin, as we must, with the text of the statute. The familiar framework of Chevron U.S.A. Inc. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc., 467 U.S. 837 (1984), governs our review. At the first step, we ask whether Congress has directly spoken to the precise question at issue. If the intent of Congress is clear, that is the end of the matter, for we must give effect to the unambiguously expressed intent of Congress. Only if the statute is silent or ambiguous do we proceed to consider whether the agency's answer rests on a permissible construction. The agency reads "adverse effect" to encompass any incidental consequence, however attenuated, flowing from a regulated activity. But the statute does not stand alone. Its neighboring provisions confine the agency's jurisdiction to effects that are "direct and substantial," and we do not lightly assume that Congress employed the phrase in one section only to abandon its meaning in the next. Statutory terms are not to be read in isolation but in the context of the whole enactment. When the surrounding provisions are consulted, the agency's expansive reading collapses under its own weight. The agency responds that its interpretation deserves deference because the statute is ambiguous. We are not persuaded. Deference is not a talisman that transforms every plausible reading into a permissible one. An agency may not, under the guise of interpretation, rewrite a limitation Congress deliberately imposed. Where, as here, the statutory structure supplies a limiting principle, the agency must respect it. The doctrine of deference presupposes fidelity to the boundaries Congress has drawn; it does not license an agency to erase them. Nor does the agency's reliance on its longstanding practice carry the day. A consistent administrative construction is entitled to weight, but consistency in error is not a virtue. The agency has never squarely confronted the tension between its reading and the "direct and substantial" limitation, and an interpretation that has escaped scrutiny cannot claim the sanction of considered judgment. We hold that the agency's construction of "adverse effect" is inconsistent with the statute as a whole. The petition for review is granted, the agency's order is vacated, and the matter is remanded for the agency to reconsider its rule in light of the statutory limitations we have identified. We express no view on what conclusion the agency should reach on remand, provided it acts within the bounds Congress established. So ordered.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit

label: ai
text: KANNE, Circuit Judge. The plaintiff appeals the district court's grant of summary judgment on his claim under the Fair Labor Standards Act. He contends that the hours he spent donning and doffing protective equipment before and after his shifts constitute compensable work. In holding otherwise, the district court found those tasks integral to the workday only in a colloquial sense, not the statutory one. We review the grant of summary judgment de novo, and we affirm. The record establishes the relevant facts without meaningful dispute. A line operator at a meatpacking facility, the plaintiff, before clocking in, retrieved and put on a helmet, gloves, an apron, and boots. He performed the reverse sequence at the end of each shift. His employer did not pay for this time, relying on a longstanding provision in the collective bargaining agreement that treated such activities as noncompensable. The statute controls the outcome. Section 203(o) of the FLSA permits an employer and a union to exclude from compensable hours any time spent "changing clothes" at the beginning or end of the workday, provided the exclusion rests on the terms of a bona fide collective bargaining agreement. The parties here bargained over exactly that question and reached exactly that result. The plaintiff cannot now recover for time his union agreed to forgo. The plaintiff's principal argument turns on the meaning of "clothes." He insists that his protective gear falls outside the ordinary understanding of the word because it guards against workplace hazards rather than the elements. The Supreme Court rejected a materially identical argument in Sandifer v. United States Steel Corp. There the Court held that "clothes" encompasses items designed and used to cover the body and worn during the workday, including protective garments. The gear at issue in this case fits that definition. A helmet, apron, gloves, and boots cover the body and serve the plaintiff throughout his shift. He resists that conclusion by pointing to the safety function of his equipment, but function does not remove an item from the statutory category. Sandifer drew the line at devices that are not commonly regarded as articles of dress, such as respirators and earplugs. The plaintiff wore no such devices. His gear, whatever its protective purpose, remains clothing under the governing standard. The remaining contention fails for a similar reason. The plaintiff argues that even if his activities count as changing clothes, the time is compensable because the equipment is integral and indispensable to his principal duties. That test derives from Steiner and its successors, and it governs claims outside the reach of Section 203(o). Once a bargained exclusion applies, the integral-and-indispensable inquiry drops away. The union spoke, and its agreement resolves the matter. The plaintiff spent measurable time on these tasks. His remedy, if any, lies at the bargaining table rather than in federal court. The judgment of the district court is AFFIRMED.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: The record before us compels the conclusion that the defendant's conduct, taken as a whole, discloses a lack of acceptance of responsibility sufficient to warrant the denial of the reduction contemplated under the applicable sentencing provisions. Acceptance of responsibility is not a formality to be satisfied by rote acknowledgment at the moment of sentencing; rather, it is a substantive demonstration, evidenced through the totality of a defendant's words and actions, that he genuinely comprehends the wrongfulness of his conduct and accepts the consequences that flow from it. The defendant here has failed to make that showing. We begin with the well-settled principle that the burden rests upon the defendant to establish, by a preponderance of the evidence, that he is entitled to the adjustment. A guilty plea, while entitled to considerable weight, does not automatically entitle a defendant to a reduction as a matter of right. The sentencing court remains free to examine the defendant's conduct both before and after the entry of that plea, and to weigh whether the plea reflects true contrition or mere pragmatic calculation in the face of overwhelming evidence. The defendant's conduct falls into the latter category. Throughout the proceedings, he engaged in a pattern of minimization and deflection, attributing his offense to the actions of others and characterizing his own participation as incidental or coerced. When afforded the opportunity to speak in allocution, he offered no unqualified admission of the essential elements of his conduct, but instead advanced a version of events materially at odds with the facts established in the presentence report and to which he had ostensibly agreed at the time of his plea. Such frivolous denial of relevant conduct, when contradicted by the record, is properly considered by the sentencing court as evidence bearing on the sincerity of any purported acceptance. More significantly, the defendant continued in conduct inconsistent with acceptance of responsibility after his plea was entered. His post-plea behavior, including violations of the conditions of release and further attendant misconduct, undermines any claim that he had come to appreciate the gravity of his offense. A defendant cannot, on the one hand, profess contrition before the court while, on the other, persist in the very disregard for the law that occasioned his prosecution. The two positions are irreconcilable, and the court was entitled to resolve that contradiction against the defendant. We are mindful that the determination of acceptance of responsibility is peculiarly within the province of the sentencing court, which is uniquely situated to evaluate the defendant's demeanor, credibility, and sincerity. That determination is entitled to great deference on review and will not be disturbed absent clear error. Finding none, we hold that the sentencing court acted well within its discretion in concluding that the defendant had not carried his burden.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: The report records that in 1991 the government pardoned former army commander General Aoun and two of his aides, and the terms of that pardon merit close scrutiny. A pardon conditioned on the recipient's conduct is not an unqualified restoration of legal standing. It is a grant that lapses, or becomes voidable, if the stated condition fails. The distinction matters because the legal consequences of the 1991 measure turn on whether the condition was fulfilled, waived, or breached. The condition attached to the pardon appears to have required the beneficiaries to leave the country and to abstain from political activity for a fixed period. Where a pardon carries such a term, the recipient accepts a bargain. The state forgoes prosecution or punishment, and in exchange the recipient assumes an obligation. Breach of that obligation revives the state's authority to act, unless a subsequent enactment or amnesty has extinguished the underlying liability outright. Two questions follow. The first is whether the condition was itself lawful. A pardon that requires exile raises concerns under the right of a national to remain in and return to his own country. If the condition offends a constitutional or treaty guarantee, a court may sever the condition and treat the pardon as absolute, or may hold the entire grant defective. The report does not resolve this, and the answer depends on the constitutional text in force in 1991 and on the state's international commitments at that time. The second question is whether the state may enforce a lapsed condition years after the fact. Reliance and the passage of time weigh against reopening a settled matter. A beneficiary who ordered his affairs around the pardon has a strong claim that the state cannot revive prosecution on the strength of a stale breach. Against that, a serious and continuing violation of the condition may justify enforcement, because the recipient never held anything more than a defeasible grant. The better view treats the conditional pardon as a limited settlement rather than a final acquittal. Under that reading, the beneficiaries received protection against prosecution only so long as they honored the terms. Their return to public life, if it occurred in defiance of the condition, did not automatically restore criminal exposure. Enforcement would require an affirmative act by the competent authority, a finding of breach, and a determination that no intervening amnesty had removed the offense from the reach of the courts. The report should therefore be read as identifying an open legal position, not a resolved one. The pardon's validity, the enforceability of its condition, and the effect of any later general amnesty each require separate analysis. Until those matters are addressed on the record, the legal status of General Aoun and his aides under the 1991 measure remains contingent on facts the report does not fully supply.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: To the contrary, the Board of Immigration Appeals provided ample reasons for its adverse credibility determination, and those reasons find substantial support in the administrative record. The Board did not rest its disbelief upon speculation, conjecture, or an impermissibly demanding standard of corroboration. Rather, it identified specific, cogent inconsistencies that went to the heart of the petitioner's asylum claim. First, the Board observed that the petitioner's testimony concerning the date and circumstances of his alleged detention differed materially from the account set forth in his written application. Whereas the application described a single period of confinement lasting several weeks, the petitioner testified to two separate detentions of shorter duration. When afforded an opportunity to explain this discrepancy, the petitioner offered only that he had been "confused," an explanation the immigration judge was entitled to find unpersuasive. Second, the Board noted the absence of reasonably available corroborating evidence. The petitioner claimed continuing contact with family members who purportedly witnessed the events in question, yet produced no affidavit, letter, or other documentation from them. The Board correctly recognized that, while corroboration is not invariably required, its absence may fairly be weighed where such evidence should be obtainable and no adequate explanation for its absence is offered. Third, the demeanor findings, to which we accord particular deference, reinforced the Board's conclusion. The immigration judge observed that the petitioner responded evasively to direct questioning and repeatedly altered his answers upon cross-examination. These grounds, taken together, constitute the specific and cogent reasons our precedents demand. We do not sit to reweigh the evidence or substitute our judgment for that of the agency. Because a reasonable adjudicator would not be compelled to reach a contrary conclusion, the adverse credibility determination must be sustained, and the petition for review is denied.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: KANNE, Circuit Judge. The plaintiff appeals from the district court's grant of summary judgment on his claim under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, contending that the officers used excessive force during his arrest. We review the grant of summary judgment de novo, construing the record in the light most favorable to the plaintiff. On this record, the district court reached the correct result, and we affirm. The facts material to our decision are largely undisputed. Officers responded to a report of a domestic disturbance at the plaintiff's residence. When they arrived, the plaintiff stood in the doorway holding a kitchen knife. The officers ordered him three times to drop the weapon. He refused each time and advanced toward the nearest officer. One officer then discharged his taser, and the plaintiff fell. The officers handcuffed him and secured the knife. The plaintiff argues that the taser deployment was constitutionally unreasonable because he never intended to harm anyone. That argument misunderstands the governing standard. The reasonableness of force turns on the objective circumstances confronting the officer at the moment force is used, not on the arrestee's subjective intent. Graham v. Connor supplies the framework. We ask whether the officer's conduct was objectively reasonable in light of the severity of the crime, the immediacy of the threat, and whether the suspect resisted or fled. Applying that framework, the officers acted within constitutional bounds. The plaintiff held a bladed weapon, ignored repeated commands to drop it, and moved toward an officer within striking distance. An officer facing an armed suspect who advances after multiple warnings need not wait for the first blow. A single taser deployment, followed by prompt medical attention, falls comfortably within the range of reasonable responses to that threat. The plaintiff also contends that a factual dispute exists about whether he actually stepped forward. He points to his own deposition testimony that he remained stationary. The video from the responding officer's body camera resolves the point. It shows the plaintiff taking two steps toward the officer with the knife raised. When the record contains a videotape that contradicts a party's account, we view the facts as depicted on the tape. Scott v. Harris governs here. The plaintiff's contrary testimony does not create a genuine dispute because no reasonable jury could credit it against the footage. Because the force was objectively reasonable, the plaintiff cannot establish a constitutional violation. That conclusion also disposes of his claim against the municipality. Without an underlying violation, a Monell claim cannot proceed. The district court correctly declined to reach the officers' qualified immunity defense, since the absence of a violation ends the inquiry. We recognize that encounters of this kind unfold in seconds and impose difficult choices on officers. The Constitution does not require perfection. It requires reasonableness, and the officers met that standard. The judgment of the district court is AFFIRMED.
court: United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit

label: ai
text: We review the Board's dismissal of Petrovic's appeal under the deferential standard that governs our examination of factual determinations in the immigration context. Findings of fact, including those concerning whether an applicant has suffered past persecution or possesses a well-founded fear of future persecution, are reviewed for substantial evidence. Under this standard, we must uphold the agency's determination unless the record compels a contrary conclusion. It is not sufficient that we might have weighed the evidence differently had the matter come before us in the first instance; rather, the petitioner bears the burden of demonstrating that the evidence he presented was so compelling that no reasonable adjudicator could have reached the conclusion the Board reached. Applying this framework, we conclude that the Board's determination is supported by substantial evidence. To establish eligibility for asylum, Petrovic was required to demonstrate either that he suffered past persecution on account of a protected ground or that he holds a well-founded fear of future persecution should he be returned to his country of origin. Persecution, as this court has consistently held, connotes harm rising above the level of mere harassment, discrimination, or generalized conditions of hardship. The isolated incidents Petrovic described, while regrettable, do not in the aggregate rise to the severity necessary to constitute persecution within the meaning of the statute. The Board reasonably concluded that the mistreatment he recounted, though unpleasant, fell short of the threshold this circuit has established. Nor did Petrovic carry his burden of establishing a well-founded fear of future persecution. Such a fear must be both subjectively genuine and objectively reasonable. Even assuming the sincerity of Petrovic's subjective apprehension, the record does not compel the conclusion that his fear is objectively well-founded. He offered no persuasive evidence that he would be singled out for mistreatment upon return, nor did he establish a pattern or practice of persecution directed at individuals similarly situated to himself. The country conditions evidence in the record, while reflecting continued instability, does not demonstrate that Petrovic faces a particularized threat on account of a statutorily protected ground. Because Petrovic failed to satisfy the standard governing asylum, it follows a fortiori that he cannot meet the more exacting standard applicable to withholding of removal, which requires a showing that it is more likely than not that his life or freedom would be threatened. Having failed to demonstrate a well-founded fear under the lesser standard, he necessarily fails under the greater. We are mindful of the difficult circumstances that prompted Petrovic to seek refuge in this country. Our role, however, is not to substitute our judgment for that of the agency, but to determine whether the Board's decision rests upon substantial evidence. Concluding that it does, we hold that the Board did not err in dismissing the appeal. The petition for review is accordingly denied.

No markdown, no preamble, no title, no commentary, no explanation. * **Tone/Register:** Substantive legal document, not a generic essay (judicial opinion style). 2. **Deconstruct the Topic & Context:** * "KLEINFELD, Circuit Judge": This is a reference to Judge Andrew Kleinfeld of the Ninth Circuit. The topic—employer and insurance administrator offering a group disability insurance policy as a fringe benefit—sounds like an ERISA or employment law case. * Core legal question: Can an employer/administrator offer such a policy as a fringe benefit? Obviously, the answer in reality is "yes," but there might be nuances regarding fiduciary duties, preemption, or anti-cutback rules. I need to write a judicial opinion exploring the legality and parameters of offering such a benefit, perhaps addressing a plaintiff's challenge (e.g., claiming it violates some state law, or that the administrator had a conflict of interest, or that the plan documents were deficient). * I will draft a judicial opinion authored by "KLEINFELD, Circuit Judge" addressing this issue. 3. **Drafting Strategy (Target ~494 words):** * **Introduction (approx. 70 words):** State the core question and the holding. Yes, an employer and its insurance administrator can offer a group disability insurance policy as a fringe benefit, provided they comply with ERISA disclosure and fiduciary requirements. * **Factual Background (approx. 100 words):** Briefly outline the hypothetical dispute. Plaintiff argues that the offering of the policy under these terms is somehow invalid or that state law restricts it. * **Legal Standard (approx. 120 words):** Discuss ERISA preemption, the definition of an employee welfare benefit plan, and the discretion of employers to design fringe benefit packages. * **Analysis/Application (approx. 150 words):** Apply the law. Emphasize that employers are not compelled to offer disability insurance, but if they do, the federal framework governs. The administrator's role is contractual and fiduciary but does not strip the employer of the right to offer the benefit. Address the plaintiff's specific concerns (e.g., conflict of interest, state law claims) and dismiss them under established precedent. * **Conclusion (approx. 54 words):** Reiterate the holding. The judgment of the district court is affirmed. 4. **Drafting - First Pass:** KLEINFELD, Circuit Judge. The question presented in this appeal is whether an employer and its designated insurance administrator may lawfully offer a group disability insurance policy as a fringe benefit to its employees. Appellants contend that the structure of the plan, specifically the dual role of the administrator and the discretionary authority granted under the policy, renders the offering fundamentally invalid under state insurance regulations and imposes an impermissible burden on the beneficiaries.

No markdown, preamble, title, commentary, or explanation. - **Register**: Substantive legal document. - **Constraints (Lawlint rules to avoid)**: - core/empty-hedge: No "might be said", "in some sense". Provide reasons. - core/no-ai-cliches: No "delve into". Use "examine". - core/no-doublets: No "null and void". Use "void". - core/no-em-dash-overuse / core/no-em-dash: No em dashes. - core/no-empty-emphasis: No "very significantly important". Use "under". - core/no-marketing-language: No "leverage", "robust". - core/no-not-only: No "not only... but also". - core/no-parenthetical-asides: No "(again)". - core/no-passive-overuse: Use active voice ("The court denied" not "The motion was denied"). - core/no-repetitive-openers: Vary sentence openers. - core/no-robotic-transitions: No "Moreover,". Use "Also," or integrate. - core/no-rule-of-three: Avoid dense triplets. - core/no-semicolons: No semicolons. Use periods. - core/no-sycophantic-openers: No "Great question". - core/no-throat-clearing: Direct start. - core/oxford-comma: Use Oxford comma in lists. - core/padded-elaboration: No restating without new info. - core/sentence-length: Keep sentences readable. Break up long ones. **Drafting - Step-by-Step:** 1. **Premise**: The appellant argues that even if Oregon trespass law applies (assimilated), the evidence was legally insufficient to convict. 2. **Legal Standard**: A sufficiency challenge requires viewing evidence in the light most favorable to the prosecution and determining if a rational trier of fact could find guilt beyond a reasonable doubt. 3. **Analysis of Elements of Trespass**: Oregon law typically requires (1) entering or remaining on premises, (2) which are not open to the public, (3) after receiving notice that entry is forbidden. 4. **Application to Facts**: The prosecution failed to prove notice or that the property was not open to the public. Appellant was present in a common area. No signage existed. The property owner did not directly ask him to leave. 5. **Conclusion**: The conviction cannot stand because the record lacks proof of a required element. *Drafting text while monitoring rules:* The appellant contends that, assuming the Oregon trespass statute applies under the Assimilative Crimes Act, the record lacks sufficient evidence to sustain his conviction. The standard for evaluating a sufficiency challenge requires the court to view the evidence in the light most favorable to the prosecution and affirm only if a rational trier of fact could find every element of the offense proven beyond a reasonable doubt. Under Oregon Revised Statutes section 164.245, criminal trespass in the second degree requires that a person enters or remains in a building when the person is not licensed or privileged to do so. The statute further specifies that a license or privilege to enter a premises that is only partly open to the public is extended to the area held open.

Good candidates: * Position and Duties * Base Salary and Bonus * Confidential Information and Inventions * Termination * Non-Compete/Non-Solicitation * To hit the ~472-word target, I'll need to develop a few of these sections with standard legal verbiage, conditions, and definitions. 3. **Drafting - Section by Section (monitoring word count):** * *Position and Duties:* Employer employs Employee as [Title], reporting to [Board/CEO]. Employee shall devote substantially all business time. * *Compensation:* Base salary, annual bonus, equity. * *Termination:* For cause, without cause, resignation for good reason. * *Confidentiality:* Standard restrictive covenants. 4. **Drafting and Refining (aiming for ~472 words):** During the Term of Employment, Employee shall serve as the Chief Executive Officer of the Company, reporting directly to the Board of Directors. Employee shall perform the duties, responsibilities, and functions typically associated with such executive position, in addition to such other executive duties as may be reasonably assigned by the Board from time to time. Employee shall devote substantially all of Employee's business time, attention, and energy to the business and affairs of the Company, provided that Employee may manage personal investments and engage in civic or charitable activities so long as such activities do not materially interfere with Employee's duties hereunder. For all services rendered by Employee hereunder, the Company shall pay Employee a base salary at an annual rate of four hundred thousand dollars, payable in accordance with the Company's standard payroll practices. The Board shall review Employee's base salary annually and may increase, but not decrease, such base salary in its sole discretion. In addition to the base salary, Employee shall be eligible to receive an annual performance bonus of up to seventy-five percent of Employee's base salary, based upon the achievement of performance targets established by the Board within the first ninety days of each fiscal year. Any annual bonus earned shall be paid in a lump sum no later than March fifteenth of the year following the year to which the bonus relates, subject to Employee's continued employment through the date of payment. Upon execution of this Agreement, Employee shall be granted restricted stock units representing fifty thousand shares of the Company's common stock, vesting over four years with twenty-five percent vesting on the first anniversary of the commencement date and the remainder vesting in equal monthly installments thereafter, subject to Employee's continued employment.
